May 14, 2026

OCP 2026 EMEA Keynote

Open Compute Project(23.7K) ·169:38 ·199 views · 2026-05-14
Raúl Álvarez (European Market Development Manager, Open Compute Project)
The 2026 OCP EMEA Summit highlighted a major industry-wide transition toward high-density AI infrastructure, driven by open collaborations in high-voltage direct current (DC) power, advanced liquid cooling, and custom silicon like the Arm AGI CPU designed to support the rapid rise of agentic AI.
TL;DR The 2026 OCP EMEA Summit highlighted a major industry-wide transition toward high-density AI infrastructure, driven by open collaborations in high-voltage direct current (DC) power, advanced liquid cooling, and custom silicon like the Arm AGI CPU designed to support the...

TL;DR

The 2026 OCP EMEA Summit highlighted a major industry-wide transition toward high-density AI infrastructure, driven by open collaborations in high-voltage direct current (DC) power, advanced liquid cooling, and custom silicon like the Arm AGI CPU designed to support the rapid rise of agentic AI.

OCP EMEA Growth and Regional Sovereignty

  • Expanding European Presence: OCP CEO George Tchaparian announced that the Barcelona summit registered over 2,000 attendees, making it the largest EMEA event to date.
      • Key Metrics: 70% of attendees are local to Europe. European entities make up 23% of total OCP membership, 38% of startup members, and over 30% of global contributions.
      • Market Outlook: An independent IDC study projects that deployment of OCP-recognized equipment in Europe will reach $15 billion by the end of 2029.
  • National AI Cloud Hubs: Hyperscalers are investing heavily in local European infrastructure to meet strict digital and data sovereignty requirements.
      • Microsoft's Expansion: Azure Partner Architect Bryan Kelly highlighted Spain Central in Madrid, Microsoft's first Spanish cloud region, which achieves a Power Usage Effectiveness (PUE) of 1.12. Microsoft is also expanding its next-generation zero-wastewater closed-loop cooling design in Aragon to support the local EU Data Boundary.
      • NVIDIA's Sovereign Initiatives: Javier Pemán discussed Europe's push to build "AI Factories" (collaborative research centers like the Barcelona Supercomputing Center) and "Giga Factories" (sovereign cloud service providers at scale) to keep processing local and compliant.

Power and Cooling at the Megawatt-Rack Scale

  • Addressing AI Workload Volatility: Unlike traditional, stable power profiles, AI workloads experience massive power spikes and fluctuations. Google’s Amber Huffman announced joint work with AWS, Meta, Microsoft, and NVIDIA to define grid mitigation standards (e.g., load ramp rates, forced oscillation, and phase jump) to protect utility grids.
  • The Shift to Direct Current (DC) Architectures: Traditional 48V power distribution cannot scale to meet upcoming megawatt-per-rack AI clusters.
      • Project Mt. Diablo: Developed by Google, Meta, and Microsoft, this project disaggregates power into a sidecar delivering ±400V DC, leaving every rack unit dedicated entirely to compute.
      • 800V DC Distribution: Sébastien Cruz-Mermy (Schneider Electric) detailed the shift toward 800V DC systems. For blocks over 5MW, Schneider is pathfinding centralized DC distribution at the facility level using Solid-State Transformers (SSTs) to eliminate AC-to-DC conversion losses at the rack.
  • Transitioning to Liquid Cooling: Air cooling is hitting physical thermal limits. NVIDIA's Pemán noted that while average rack density is currently 150kW, it is projected to hit 250kW by late 2026, 650kW in 2027, and 1MW soon after, making Direct Liquid Cooling (DLC) mandatory.
      • Project Deschutes: Google contributed its final 1.0 specification for this fifth-generation cooling distribution unit (CDU), integrating the Redmond hot aisle containment system to support dense, liquid-cooled deployments.

The Rise of Neoclouds and Modular AI Factories

  • Workload-Specific Customization: Neocloud providers are differentiating themselves from standard hyperscalers by co-designing tailored platforms for specialized AI developers.
      • Crusoe: Peter (Crusoe) explained their "electrons to tokens" vertical integration, where the company sources stranded energy, manufactures its own electrical switchgear, and deploys high-density GPUs under its own software stack.
      • Denvr: Amy (Denvr) showcased a PUE of under 1.04 using immersion cooling. Via OCP collaboration, they shrank a standard 6U H200 SXM server down to 3OU (half volume), allowing 14 units to fit in a single rack compared to the standard four.
      • FarmGPU: JM (FarmGPU) discussed optimizing open-network fabrics for inference and training. FarmGPU is partnering with Solidigm to address storage bottlenecks, predicting that next-gen inference with KV caching will require up to 16TB of storage per GPU in the upcoming Rubin chip family.
  • Standardization via ORV3: Across the board, Neocloud panelists credited the Open Rack V3 (ORV3) standard as the single most critical asset for deployment speed, interoperability, and long-term hardware lifecycle planning.

Custom Silicon and Open Hardware Innovations

  • Co-Developing the Arm AGI CPU: Paul Saab (Meta) and Eddie Ramirez (Arm) introduced the Arm AGI CPU, designed specifically for orchestrating "agentic AI" workloads.
      • Meta's Rapid Porting: Meta successfully ported its entire, massive production codebase to Arm in just 90 days.
      • Hardware Specs: The chip features 136 Neoverse V3 cores and sub-100ns memory latency. In an air-cooled ORV3-compatible rack, designers can pack 30 two-node reference servers, delivering over 8,000 high-performance cores in a single 36kW footprint.
      • LPDDR in the Data Center: Meta successfully deployed mobile LPDDR memory in production, delivering 15% more compute cores per rack compared to traditional DDR5 configurations.
  • AMD Helios and Open Adapter Card (OAC):
      • Helios Rack: AMD's Robert Hormuth introduced the Helios rack scale architecture (slated for Q3), which leverages 16 existing OCP standards to deliver 72 GPUs and 260 TB/s of aggregate bandwidth.
      • The OAC Spec: To combat the physical limitations of the horizontal OAM form factor, AMD collaborated with Intel, NVIDIA, Qualcomm, and Dell to launch the Open Adapter Card (OAC) workstream. By turning the card vertically, the design eliminates GPU thermal shadowing, extending the viability of air cooling alongside liquid options.

Notable Quotes

"Last year felt like a decade of AI breakthroughs. I don't know about you, but I feel like we're aging in dog years." — Amber Huffman, Technologist, Google Cloud
"If you want to go fast, go alone; if you want to go far, go together. That's what OCP is all about." — Robert Hormuth, Corporate VP of Architecture & Strategy, AMD
"Power is the new currency of the world. It is the new gold." — Paul Saab, Software Engineer, Meta
"We flirt between the line of what we know and what we think we know. We need to be on the field and test." — Tony (on physical testing of high-voltage DC architectures)
Please welcome Open Compute Project European Market Development Manager Raul Alvarez and Open Compute Project Solution Provider Program Manager Ewa McCurdy. Good morning everyone and welcome to the 2026 OCP EMEA Summit here in sunny Barcelona. We are very happy to have you all here. Hope you already had a great start of the day. It's amazing to see our global community gathered here: leaders, engineers, innovators from across EMEA and beyond. And this community keeps growing, from 700 of us in Lisbon, to 1,100 of us in Dublin. We are now here in Barcelona. We are on track to be the biggest EMEA summit so far. More than 350 sessions, 20 panels, and 12 executive sessions. We are counting over 2,000 attendees in this edition of the summit. I think that deserves a round of applause. So this is what OCP is all about: people together to share ideas openly and build better infrastructure together. At OCP, our mission is simple but powerful: to bring hyperscale innovation to everyone. And here in Barcelona over the next two days, that mission becomes real. We will be diving into the biggest challenges in our industry, from sustainability and energy efficiency to the rapid transformation driven by AI and the need for scalable open infrastructure to support it. And if there's one thing I've learned from this community, it's that no one solves these challenges alone. Exactly. What makes OCP special is all of you: the willingness to collaborate, to share, and to push boundaries together. So whether you're here for the keynotes, the expo hall, or those hallway conversations, those just might make the most impact. Also, don't miss the Future Technologies Symposium. We will be looking ahead at what's coming next. So take this opportunity to connect, to ask questions, and to challenge ideas. And enjoy Barcelona, because innovation is better with a bit of sunshine. Thank you all for being a part of the Open Compute Project. Thanks for joining us and welcome to the 2026 OCP EMEA Summit. And now, please join me in welcoming our OCP CEO, George Tchaparian. Hola Barcelona! Thank you for being here, thank you Raul, thank you Ewa. Really appreciate you guys being here today. Yes, it's very exciting to come to Europe and conduct our EMEA Summit here in Europe. And this summit is special, as they were mentioning. We have expanded our summit to 2,000 participants like you. Thank you very much. And also, very importantly, this year we're celebrating the 15th year of OCP's formation by Meta, by Facebook. So that needs a round of applause as well. Most importantly, what we're seeing is that 70% of the population here that is attending is from Europe, which is exciting and a very important metric for me to see how we are expanding in Europe. And this is very important to see. It's not the reverse that we used to have, like people coming from outside to Europe, but 70% of you are from here. Thank you very much for that as well. Now, I would like also to emphasize and mention that what's happening with Europe is amazing. This is the vibrant community that you can see. Our membership is growing. By the end of the year, we're probably going to be about 700 members worldwide, but you can see about 23% of it today as of today is from Europe. Our startup membership is very, very healthy. They are coming in as startup companies. And as you can see, about 38% of it is from Europe. Contribution-wise, there is an amazing contribution from all of you from the community coming in, and 30% of it and more is coming from Europe. We have commissioned an independent study with IDC to see how our equipment, which are recognized equipment of OCP, is being deployed and utilized. Specifically for Europe, we wanted to see that, and they said it's going to be about $15 billion by the end of 2029, which I believe is a conservative number. Now, we also conducted another very important survey. You're going to get a white paper on this eventually. That survey was about how OCP is really helping the industry move forward. And they came up with a simple way of representing it. They said, "Okay, there are two extremes." As you can see, one is the captive, in-house development and the other one is all the standards, such as—if I give an example—such as IEEE. But OCP is in the middle. OCP is bringing these two together. And this is all about accelerating innovation, as we say: from hyperscale innovation to all. And even moving to other areas of the region, it's about how OCP is trying to make sure that time-to-market is accelerated, development is accelerated, and scale is growing like crazy. It is being accelerated, and you can take advantage of that, by the community, for the community, bringing it all together. And this is where OCP is coming together. So thank you very much for that. I really appreciate the volunteers worldwide, thousands of you, who are really helping to make this happen. Now, we decided to give a good theme for this EMEA Summit, and for the year. We said, "Okay, Leading the Future of AI." And this is for recognizing all the good work that you have done, all the good work that our board members are doing, board member companies, our advisory board members are doing, the community is doing, and the volunteers are doing. We're expanding worldwide. If you look at the entrance when you come in, you can see how regionally we're expanding in terms of OCP Ready, OCP experience centers, and also service providers. It's incredible. So with the recognition of all, and especially our hyperscalers and the board members that I know are working very hard—they compete, but they bring it all together in terms of scaling AI clusters around the world. Last year, actually, we put together the vision, and I communicated this during our San Jose Global Summit. We've done a lot of good progress since then. You can see here there are two parallel, horizontal ways of approaching this. One is for strategic domains. In the middle, of course, it was all about Cloud DC, cloud data centers, that we all know. But at the two sides of it, you can see how it's moving towards all the AI data centers, and on the other side, where it's being transformed to the regions. Instead of calling it edge, we're calling it regions, but this is where edge is there, and also enterprises are there. This is exactly where the inferences are growing to make sure that it's really evolving and trying to go to the public, and go to the regions as well. So we call that "from hyperscalers to regions." Of course, we also realized that OCP is not the same OCP we used to have: chips, open hardware, open server, open networking, storage, connectivity, racks. It's that and beyond that. It is also all the way to the grid. That is really important. We're calling it "from grid to chips," or "chips to grid," and that is the technology scope. This is where we expanded our advisory board, we expanded ourselves, and you're helping us a lot in terms of making sure that the essence of power, the essence of cooling, the energy, the infrastructure build-up, and the system management are all coming together. So this is the vision of the data center ecosystem moving forward that we are pushing for the next, let's say, two to three years. With that also, there's a lot of progress that we have made since last October. But I want to make sure I highlight a couple of them. Unfortunately, I cannot really speak about all the contributions that you have made, but a couple of them are important. In the AI data center facilities side, we have critical projects that are really evolving, and these are important in terms of power distribution and OT/IT telemetry and management. New alliances were formed, very important alliances, like EPRI and Current OS. So that's on the side of what we're pushing about energy, about how we connect to the grid, and all that. In the middle, this is where we've been very successful, we've been very much evolving, and this is where hyperscalers come in. We've done a lot of good in terms of building clusters that you can leverage. This is where ESUN is coming, SUE-T, and contributions from all our hyperscalers: Meta, Google, and Microsoft for the Mt Diablo project, and then Google with the Deschutes projects. And you can see also how the connectivity is really working there in terms of contribution in the middle, especially the wide rack, etc. And lastly, this is what we're calling the AI computing continuum. This is where we said we're moving from the hyperscalers to the regions. This is where enterprises are coming. As you can see, a lot of partners are coming here to help that evolution of inferences. And lastly, I should also comment that ARM is coming in with open chiplet system architecture, the open chiplet economy, opening up the BMC and then making all the connectivity like LEGO, making sure that the chip development moves faster. So thank you to all of you. And I can't emphasize enough the support I get, the support the OCP community gets, and the support OCP members get from these esteemed colleagues of ours. These are our board of directors from hyperscalers, and from other companies, as you can see. We expanded it. We have NVIDIA, ARM, we have Intel of course, AMD, and also Google, Meta, and Microsoft. So this is incredible. By the way, they compete, but when it comes to OCP, they all collaborate for your own good. Give them a round of applause, this is important. Lastly, like I said, we realized that for the other areas of the community and everything else, this whole evolution of AI in the past three years of investment is moving so fast, and there's no turning back. And we wanted also to make sure we have our ODMs in this, we have the power and energy guys in this, and we have our cloud providers. This is also where enterprises come in, and this is why we expanded our advisory board. We welcome you to come in and help us, join us in any capacity, and this is the path to graduate to become a board member of OCP as we do it. After I'm done, there will be a lot of good keynotes from the following companies that you can see. Please pay attention. They've done a great work in terms of bringing a lot of their innovation publicly to you, with a lot of specifications out there open. You can really harness this, and you can really bring it together to put on top of your IP to make sure that you accelerate the foundation, while your IP comes on top of it to make sure you move very fast in scaling your data center or whatever you're trying to build going forward. And with that, I would like to mention, changing the theme now, let's say: let's together go build the future of AI. I thank you very much. I would like to thank the volunteers, seriously, because without you, we cannot make this happen. Thank you to our board of directors, our advisory board, my team, and every one of you. Like we said, this is for the community, by the community, and thank you all for coming here and enjoy it. Okay, thank you very much. Please welcome Technologist, Google Cloud, Amber Huffman. Good morning everyone. I'm very excited today to talk to you about the fungible data center for the AI era and how we're building that together at OCP. AI compute demand is on an exponential curve. With Gemini, our customers are now consuming over 16 billion tokens per minute through direct API use alone. That's the demand signal driving everything you'll see today. All seven of our 2-billion-user products use Gemini models, along with all 15 of our half-billion-user products. From AlphaFold to Co-Scientist to AlphaEvolve, last year felt like a decade of AI breakthroughs. I don't know about you, but I feel like we're aging in dog years. And every one of these innovations runs on infrastructure we need to keep scaling. Health, engineering, creativity, entertainment—AI is touching all of it. Let's look at a few examples. NotebookLM is helping make sense of any document, any lecture, any corpus instantly. And if you'd like to learn about it on the go, you can make yourself a podcast. With Flow, we're putting cinematic storytelling tools powered by Veo directly in creators' hands. And lastly but not least, the viral nano banana, who traveled all the way to Barcelona to welcome us, and I think got the theme pretty well. Now let's dive into the AI stack. The models get the headlines, but none of it works without what's underneath: the hardware infrastructure, the data centers, and the energy. That's where we're focused today. We're deploying Google TPUs, custom CPUs, third-party GPUs, optical circuit switching, liquid cooling, and more, across both first-party and third-party data centers. These innovations are only accelerating, and that's why we need fungibility. We need data centers that can accommodate this diversity, and we can't do it alone. In October, we asked the industry, this community, a question: can we build this together? Over 50 organizations said yes. We've gone from zero to a published 0.5 specification in only six months. Now let's talk about the agile and fungible data center for the AI era. Let's start with energy. Here is why energy is hard. Traditional workloads have a relatively stable power profile that you can see in the blue. Well, with AI workloads, they look completely different, with large swings and volatility as you can see in green. So we're working jointly with AWS, Meta, Microsoft, and NVIDIA to define requirements for mitigating grid impacts—things like load ramp rates, forced oscillation, and phase jump. We're excited to contribute this work to OCP later this summer. Now moving on to power delivery. As AI heads toward a megawatt per rack, 48 volts hits a wall. The converters and bus bars fill the rack and leave no room for the accelerators, which is kind of a problem. So Mt Diablo solves this by developing plus-minus 400 volts DC, getting more than a 10x jump in power, where the power is disaggregated into the sidecar. This was built with Meta and Microsoft. Now here's what this looks like. The sidecar sits beside the compute racks, delivering plus-minus 400 volts DC, and every rack unit stays dedicated to compute. Looking even further out, we're going fully native LVDC, eliminating AC-to-DC conversion at the facility level with integrated battery storage for the grid mitigation challenges. Check out the LVDC talk from my colleague on Thursday for more details. Now let's look at cooling. On cooling, Project Deschutes is Google's fifth-generation cooling distribution unit. We contributed the 0.8 specification in Q3 2025, incorporating everything we learned from the previous four generations and over a decade of at-scale deployments. The industry responded. By Q4, six vendors delivered prototypes. Now we've contributed the final 1.0 specification, which includes the Redmond hot aisle containment system to support high-density liquid-cooled rack deployments. This is a complete production-ready spec. Finally, let's move on to server halls and systems. In Q4 2025, we contributed the 0.3 open data center spec to give the industry a common blueprint and a starting point on the Open Data Center Initiative vision. In Q1, we published the 0.5 spec with Google's expectations for 2027 and 2028 TPU blended density. These are the design points the industry needs to be planning for right now. We're now collaborating with AMD, Meta, and NVIDIA as we head toward the 1.0 spec later this year. To learn more, join the Open DC Initiative talk Thursday. Together, we can put this all together: energy, power, cooling, server halls. This is the AI data center blueprint, and this is what we can define together through OCP. Now none of this scaling is responsible if it isn't sustainable. To put things in perspective, last year, a single Gemini text query was equivalent to nine seconds of watching TV. With every new TPU generation, we are laser-focused on reducing this further, as you heard with the TPU v5e and TPU v5p announcements last week. And I'd recommend checking out Dave Patterson's recent Google Cloud blog talking about the compute carbon intensity reductions that Google has achieved over the last few years. Now OCP's sustainability approach rests on four pillars: addressing heat, energy, and water, then looking at carbon-intelligent life cycle analysis, clean backup power, and low carbon materials, and doing all of this in partnership with iMasons Climate Accord, Net Zero Innovation Hub, and others. Now if we start with materials, we've developed an evaluation framework that you can see on the left for low-carbon alternatives, and the first focus area is low-embodied carbon concrete. OCP is supporting learning modules through the OCP Academy to help the ecosystem scale adoption. Now you can't reduce what you can't measure consistently. These product category rules define the physical scope: power modules, mechanicals, platform trays, all the way down to the individual PCBAs. That boundary ensures everyone accounts for the same components and makes true apples-to-apples comparisons possible. Now you also need data quality. It matters just as much. This scoring system, developed with AWS, Meta, and Microsoft, matches data quality for the usecase. For example, the highest level, carbon-aware design, requires a full LCA analysis, whereas for hotspot screening, a simplified assessment could be sufficient. For clean backup, we're working with the Net Zero Innovation Hub on performance-based evaluation standards. Technology-agnostic, these cover PEM fuel cells, long-duration energy storage, and clean-fueled generators. The focus is on the electrical interface and energy conversion performance, and not on picking technology winners. Google's environmental report covers more on the important progress we have made. Learn more in the sustainability track on Thursday, where Googlers will dive into data quality scoring, clean backup specs and testing, and product category rules. Now, it's time for understanding how we move forward together as an industry, and nano banana decided to bring his friends. I don't know why the strawberry has a drill and a hard hat, but I mean, it's cool. Like, people have to have their fashion. Now, Google believes in open innovation very deeply. We serve in leadership roles across all 10 of these industry efforts, and many are interrelated. For example, we delivered Caliptra in partnership with both OCP and Chips Alliance. Stop by the Chips Alliance booth to learn more. Our partnership with OCP is accelerating. I love to see that curve, and I expect to see it keep going that way as we head toward 100+ contributions. OCP is really special in that it lets us align on 80% of requirements very quickly—within three, six, or nine months—and then apply that to product execution. This is specs at product speed. Now, there's a challenge in standards complexity. If you look at the Ethernet spec alone, it was developed in 1983, and since then has gained over 7,000 pages. If you look at PCIe, NVMe Express, and DDR5, if I wanted to just sit down and read all of that, it would take me 370 hours combined, and I'm not sure what I would personally absorb out of that. Now you layer on OCP's 200+ workstreams, and no human can hold all of that information. So we have a tremendous opportunity where AI can help. AI can read the entire corpus, all 10,000+ pages simultaneously. It catches conflicts and ambiguities that humans may miss, and it never forgets: every decision, every version, every contributor. AI-assisted standards development is a substantial opportunity for our industry. So join us on this journey. The fungible data center is a shared challenge, and it requires all of us working together. Thank you. Please welcome Schneider Electric Data Center Innovation Vice President, Sébastien Cruz-Mermy. Hi everyone, and I'm very pleased to be with you today to talk about this very exciting topic, which is pulling a lot of attention from the industry, as you know, which is about how to deploy these 800V DC systems that are enabling the next generation of rack density. And of course, the session today is about 10 minutes, so I'm going to leave you mostly with the key foundations and principles to consider to design 800V DC systems, and what are the different architecture optionalities that you can activate going beyond the sidecar option in particular. So first, the driver of the industry, of course, everything is pulled by this GPU roadmap and dynamic that we see: the increase of the TDP, the increase of the packaging of the GPU in one single rack, and the scale-up dimension. All of that, to improve the performance of the AI cluster, and the tokens that are generated out of these AI clusters, is pulling this rack density up. And this new rack density generation is changing almost everything, in a sense that the usual systems that have been used to be deployed in the data centers so far, with 480-volt AC systems down to the racks and that IT space, where the AC-DC conversion is done on the racks by itself, is reaching a limit, especially when you touch density which is close to 400 kilowatts per rack. And the limits are coming from multiple factors, like the connections and the feeders that need to be managed within the racks, but as well now the valuable space which is actually taken by power shelves or other battery and capacitor units that you need to integrate in the rack potentially, leaving less actual space for the compute, okay? And as well, the power capabilities of the bus bar at the back of the racks are reaching somehow a limit when you have this low-voltage level. So, that is driving mostly two shifts, okay, for the industry. The first one is: let's put the AC-to-DC conversion outside the rack. First point. The second one is about: let's drive the voltage up to enable more power to be distributed down to the racks. And of course, as the industry is looking to this GPU and density roadmap, the clear first immediate step is to activate the AC-to-DC conversion close to the IT rack, okay? This is what we call localized direct current architecture, where actually we are developing a sidecar, a power conversion rack, which is performing the AC-to-DC conversion and 800-volt DC distribution down to the racks aside the rack directly. And that is bringing a lot of advantages for the industry. First, you minimize the AC ecosystem disruptions. And also, you reduce potentially the failure risk, and the failure zone, sorry, against a couple of IT racks only. And you're also being able to shorten some qualification and technology risk exposure that you can get by deploying new architectures on your systems, and you're able to activate as well and leverage some existing supply chain capabilities coming from the electrical vehicle industry in particular, so going faster in terms of scalability and deployment. It may require a couple of higher capabilities around the cable and the bus bar, but all of that is completely manageable. And as well, we will see different forms that will take place on the industry as the technology matures. We have just seen the Mt Diablo, let's say, configuration developed with the OCP. We know that NVIDIA is also doing one configuration, so there are other configurations on the market that will help to converge and try to harmonize over time. But of course, when you start to think about deploying 800-volt DC systems, and you start to make the first step—which is about getting the AC-to-DC conversion out of the rack—and you look at this density increase of the rack, it becomes quite obvious that there are also opportunities to deploy and develop some more centralized direct current architectures to move the AC-to-DC conversion up to the gray space, up to the facility level. And that is for the purpose of freeing up some space, valuable for the compute in the IT, instead of it being consumed by the power distributions. And that kind of architecture will most likely emerge as we see the rack density touching a level of a couple of hundred megawatts by 2028-2029. But to develop such kinds of centralized direct current architectures, you need to develop a set of portfolios of solutions. It's not one-size-fits-all, okay? Depending on the power block that you are looking to address on your AI cluster, you may decide to have an option of localizing the direct current with sidecar options, which will be a very good option. You may also activate some low-voltage converter models at the centralized level or an SST configuration, solid-state transformer, as you want to address some power block around 1 to 5 megawatts. But when you want to go beyond 5 megawatts, this is where SST technology, solid-state transformer technology, is becoming pretty promising, with higher voltage distribution to be anticipated for this kind of power block. What is important to notice, especially where you expect to deliver and deploy centralized direct current architecture, is that it is just not about the conversions, okay? The protections, the distributions, and the conversion all together have to be considered as a full system, and a real full portfolio enabling this system deployment for 800-volt DC power distributions needs to be enabled beyond the conversion. This includes the new generation of ACB and MCCB breakers, including the power panels, and including the busway that need to be qualified and tested as a system to enable the full distribution of the 800-volt DC at a centralized level. So, it will turn to a point where we will have in front of us a set of options. Of course, my goal is not to dive into all these architectures. But you will have access to a set of options in order to address the right deployment of your AI cluster and deploy the 800-volt DC system in the data center. And this option and choice will be mostly driven, first, by the tech maturity—we have seen we have a roadmap of readiness of different technologies—but also by the mission profile, the flexibility, the availability, and the block size that you are looking to address in your data centers. The first immediate step, as we said, is around the localized DC direct current with the sidecar, okay, that will enable deployment of power blocks up to 1 megawatt roughly, with also some use cases around the modernization and upgrading of IT rooms that could be quite interesting to activate and to consider as well. And as we are looking to deploy higher power trains and power blocks on your AI clusters, and as the technology matures and evolves, this is where you will touch some centralized direct current with low-voltage converters, SST, and potentially, when you look at power trains much bigger than 5 megawatts, this is where the SST technology and centralized architecture on SST and full 800-volt DC distribution could be considered. So, it also means that the 800-volt DC adoption will go mostly by progressive steps, moving from what we call a coordinated integration model to a more open, interoperable model as the standards mature. And by coordinated integration model, we mean really getting the connections and the coordination between the conversion system, the protection, and the distribution system all together to enable the right engineering certainty around the safety, the performance, and the right operating conditions and stability of your 800-volt DC system as a whole. And this is where the converter decision and choice from a technology standpoint will help you to really manage the right selection of your protection and distribution system as well. And this is where the industry has the opportunity, in the short term, to really leverage existing technology—electromechanical breakers, ACB, MCCB—and to really be rated, qualified, and tested at the right voltage and capacity to enable the full deployment of the AI cluster and 800-volt DC system in safe and right performance conditions. And of course, as the standards evolve and define how these subsystems need to operate, be tested, and qualified together, this is where we will see more flexibility in the architecture and the decision to connect different subsystems together. So, as you have understood, this is very much a full system approach that you have to consider when you are looking to deploy 800-volt DC architectures and power system distributions. This system approach has to be really considered against the conversion, the protection, and the distribution, and that is quite instrumental and critical, especially when you look at architectures beyond the sidecar, or the centralized direct current architectures. And this is the beginning. There are a lot of topics that need to be touched and dived into further, and where associations like OCP are helping the industry to collaborate together to really go deeper on DC protection coordination, on the management of AC loads and DC loads in the same power systems, on the way the grounding should be harmonized between the different two-wire and three-wire configurations, and also on the monitoring and control that should be really touched in a specific way to enable the right stability, safety, and performance of the 800-volt DC, and all that for the purpose of enabling the scalability of such system deployments in the near future. So, I want to thank you for your attention, and if you are interested to know more, I'm just leaving you here with two papers that we have released recently, and that will give you much more detailed insights on how to deploy 800-volt DC architectures: the one from Schneider, released a couple of weeks ago, and the one developed with the OCP as a working group—a cross-collaboration of cross-industry leaders—to provide guidance around the low-voltage direct current power distributions for 800-volt DC systems. Thank you. Please welcome NVIDIA Cloud Partners, Supercomputing and AI, Javier Pemán. Good morning, everybody. Paraphrasing Monty Python, I'm Javier, I'm from Barcelona. I'm very happy to be here. Thank you to the OCP team for hosting this event here in Barcelona. We are very glad that you came here. I hope you're having a great time. Let's take a look at the video first to understand a little bit of what we are going to be talking about. Well, as Amber has pointed out before, the demand for AI—the increase of the demand—is insane right now, and it's growing exponentially: 10 times the size of the models. The reasoning models that you guys are using right now consume five times more, and they create five times more tokens than they used to. I did a query the other day. I asked one of these models—one of those frontier models—a very simple question, and it took 13 minutes to respond. At the end, it created some Python code that I had to react to. It was fantastic, but it was 13 minutes of thinking. That is insane, the amount of compute that that requires. That is why, for every generation of new GPUs that we develop, we assume that we need to be able to produce 10 times more tokens for the same power consumption, in order to make the whole thing sustainable. We are at the agentic era, I think. Everybody has already passed the wave of the "wow" on the B2C kind of, yeah, very cool ChatGPT era. Now we are in the industry adoption era, and this is the obsession right now of Jensen, and it is very much moving very fast into physical AI, robotics, and machine-to-machine AI. This is a new dimension where we will see all the grid AI required, and all the telco operators being very relevant once again. Frontier models and open models: frontier models have been the key to the success of AI adoption, as I said before, on the consumer side. On the industry side, industries need open models to be able to be personalized, to be able to make them their own, and to create their own by personalizing those models very openly. It is impossible to have only frontier models to develop everything because it will be unfeasible, okay? We need open, more specific models. This is one of the obsessions of NVIDIA: openness. The other one is globalization and adoption. In Europe, we have a problem. We like putting rules on everything, okay? But we need to be also producing technology and adopting and helping the industry adopt technology. At NVIDIA, we have been very, very busy the last few years trying to help the European community and the different countries try to reach some level of sovereignty, okay? We know that this is a very interesting topic for everybody. From our CEO to every single one of the VPs scattered throughout Europe, there have been many communications to help them on the sovereign initiative, and I'm going to share a couple of them now. This is going to drive 10 times more power consumption in Europe for AI compute this year, okay? This number is actually obsolete. We know that this year, it's actually not 10 times; it's closer to 20 times the amount of power initiatives and data centers that there are in Europe, scattered throughout Europe. The two main initiatives that I want to share, just in case you guys are not aware of them, are the AI Factories and the Giga Factories. AI Factories are very, very relevant in Europe. It's very interesting. AI Factories are all those research centers that, up until now, normally only used to do basic research. Now they are being asked to do much more applied research together with the industry. The objective of the AI Factories is to bring the AI to small and medium businesses and to startups. How? I can share with you: BSC, Barcelona Supercomputing Center—I encourage you to visit it here in Barcelona—is the most beautiful data center in the world, and one of the largest data centers in the world. They have this program called Connects. Connects is a program that they have built so that small and medium businesses and startups can go there and work with them and with the supercomputer to develop up to a POC or a minimum viable product of an AI project, in order for them to embrace AI in a very easy way. Once they are in the POC, they have to leave the center, because this is not a production center; this is just a center to help the industry embrace AI and get ready. Okay, once it's ready for production, you need to move where? What Europe hopes is that they will move to the Giga Factory. A Giga Factory is a hyperscaler. So, AI-specific hyperscalers, an NCP—how we call it at NVIDIA, okay? It's a data center focused on AI at scale, okay? And Europe is going to build five to seven of these in the next few years. They were supposed to start last year, but you know Europe, you know bureaucracy, so that's where we are right now. But we have a few problems. And at NVIDIA, we are being banged by all the problems about power consumption, about water consumption, and all that. Yes, it is true that at least in Spain, data centers represent only 0.5% of the power consumption, but we are expected to go to 2-3%, and that is a multiplier, yes. But it is still only 2-3%. However, we need to be worried about how to make this more sustainable. It is true that if you look at every one of our GPUs, every generation consumes twice as much, but the truth is that it creates more than 14 times more tokens, so really we are being very efficient. And we need to embrace projects that help us collaborate with the industry. What we do at NVIDIA is we normally work very closely from the very design phase, so we work with the Schneiders, with the General Electrics, and with the Vertivs. We do digital twins of absolutely everything, so that in order to design something, if you actually do a digital twin from day one, you create a reference architecture that then you can build together and test. I don't know if you have ever been to NVIDIA headquarters, but we have a wall there we call the MGX Wall, where we have all the different pieces from all our different partners that we release as an open reference. They can implement that however they want, in a collaborative way. And that is the wall that I most love showing any of my clients. NVIDIA cannot do it on their own; it is impossible. We need all these partners, we need open projects, and we need OCP organizations to make sure that we work together. NVIDIA doesn't want to make the last buck; let the other guys do it, and we will focus on what we're good at. Hyperscalers are a great example; they are green by definition, and they force themselves to do that. We need to follow. And with regards to water consumption, the WUE: forget about air cooling, it's gone. If you ever want to work with NVIDIA, it's definitely going to have to be DLC. Let me show a video of one of our OCP partners who has an implementation of this. So, our obsession is we focus on making sure that every generation will be extremely more efficient. We're going to bring now many more chips, much more specific: chips specific for inference, chips specific for security, and chips specific for the edge. This is how we contribute also to more efficient open compute. However, what you will always see in the papers when people try to mislead information is that we consume a lot. It is true that if you look at the density of a rack, we are currently at 150 kilowatts. By the end of the year, we'll be in the 250s, and next year, yes, we're going to have a rack where we are going to need all these sidecars because it's going to move very fast to 650 kilowatts. And the year after, Jensen already announced the 1 megawatt rack. So, in conclusion, we at NVIDIA use AI to make sure that from the design phase, we can do better and do things differently. We work collaboratively, and we understand that the environment is going to be crucial for adoption—global adoption. And we think we're doing our part, and we think we're doing a good collaborative work with the different initiatives. So, at the end, the fourth point is very relevant: we need to make sure that the ethics and the implications of human interaction are actually taken into account. So, that's also very, very concerning for Jensen. Thank you very much. Please welcome Open Compute Project Vice President, Market Intelligence and Innovation, James Kelly. Thanks, everyone. Save some of your applause for the panel. It's a pleasure to be back here talking about Neoclouds at another summit. At the Global Summit, we announced the "Scaling AI for Neoclouds" new workstream and brought a couple of Neoclouds up on stage. And we're going to do more of the same here today. So, with that, I'll welcome my panelists. Let's bring up YG from Scaleway. We've got Amy from Denvr. Give them a round of applause as they come up, please. Peter from Crusoe, and JM from FarmGPU. All right, let's have a seat. So, of course, the Neoclouds are all developing infrastructure and Platform as a Service based on accelerated compute, right, AI factories. I'll let them do a little bit of intros as I ask questions, but I first wanted to say: Scaleway is really Europe's cloud and AI provider. 100% European. They're developing their own autonomous tech stack, and they're on track to becoming the leading European hyperscaler alternative to the international hyperscaler giants. Next, we've got Amy from Denvr. They're a Canadian full-stack neocloud delivering AI infrastructure and platforms. Their modular approach enables ultra-efficient, zero-water rapid data center builds with leading cooling tech such as immersion. And then we've got Crusoe, the energy-first AI factory company that is vertically integrated from electrons to tokens, and they're building gigawatt-scale AI factories up to 10 billion dollars. And then we've got FarmGPU, JM here. They're a neocloud infrastructure provider delivering sovereign AI compute through their cluster management stack and open source OCP networking, with world-class AI storage and a partnership with Solidigm. So with that, you know, each one of you is fundamentally different from a hyperscaler and also different from each other. So I wanted to start, by way of introduction, by asking you kind of like: what's the core bet that your company is making, how is that something that a hyperscaler can't or won't do, and how is it different from other neoclouds? We'll start with YG. "Yeah, thank you James, and I would say for us at Scaleway, one of the key differentiators is that we are a European company to the core, and we're betting a lot on European AI and inference in Europe, and that's a bit different from what other hyperscalers are doing. And what's quite interesting in our way of doing it, and the flexibility of being a smaller neocloud around that, is being able to work very closely with all of the customers and co-design with them exactly what they want, and not having to say, 'Oh, you're a small customer, you're going to get there because of with all of that, that's it.' So, it's really kind of the key differentiator for me on being European and being able to co-work with everybody to do exactly what they need." Amy? "Thank you. Well, Denvr Dataworks is vertically integrated, which is fairly unique in the space. What it means is we control everything from infrastructure design all the way to the cloud experience that our customers use. What it allows us to do is to really optimize across each layer of that stack for performance, for efficiency, and also for cost savings, which we're then allowed to pass on to our customers. So from an infrastructure perspective, that level of control and innovation that we can really commit to allows us to have those high-efficiency modular builds. We can deploy them in 6 months or less. We're able to achieve a PUE of less than 1.04 using immersion technology and a really high-density GPU footprint. On the cloud services side, what we're able to do is connect customers to their work quickly, so they can access the cloud in three clicks. That's it. You know, and then they also have access, as YG mentioned, to a really custom experience where they have technical services to back them up." "Thanks, Amy. So, from the Crusoe side, right, the way we think about this is, you know, neoclouds are different from hyperscalers in the sense that we are specializing for AI workloads. And so specifically within the neoclouds, there's also other differentiation, right? So within Crusoe, we pride ourselves on our vertical integration. And as we say, 'from electrons to tokens.' And so what that means is that we source the energy, we build the site, we deploy the GPUs, and then we also build the cloud services on top of that, right? And so that's the vertical integration story. And you know, at the cloud level, right, we are also, you know, even optimizing for the AI developer experience, whether that's using just standard compute instances all the way to managed services—whether you're using our managed Kubernetes offering, or our managed inference offering. And so from that perspective, that's how Crusoe's differentiating ourselves: through vertical integration, all the way from energy, to making our own electrical switchgear to beat lead times, and then all the way to the cloud platform side." "Yeah, at GTC this year, Jensen said, you know, 60% of the GPUs go to hyperscalers and 40% to everyone else. Well, we're part of the everyone else, and the needs are diverse. We've made two big bets: one is that storage is going to be a key element for AI. Our partnership with Solidigm, we're basically pathfinding all the latest ISVs and innovations with storage, like stuff like KV Cache and vector databases, and then the other is confidential compute. So I think one of the things we've seen is that this is not something that all the hyperscalers and a lot of the neoclouds can offer, because there's a big trade-off with being able to do confidential compute and do a training cluster, basically at the single server or single GPU level. So, something we can deploy with our stack across, you know, the Intel TDX and AMD SEV is something I think we have a unique capability in, and it's going to be interesting for sovereign AI as we secure models and the data while it's being processed." "Great, so that was a pretty good introduction. As you can tell, like, four very different companies. I think it's a good mix. You could say a mixture of experts here. So let's get into more prompt engineering. You know, when you're building AI factories around the world, you've got different constraints, right? Whether it's GPUs, supply chain, cooling, or capital. I wanted to ask you guys—or at least maybe a couple of you can touch on the constraints for neoclouds—it's a long list of things, but what's the biggest bottleneck that you're facing, and how is it different than maybe a year ago, as things change quickly? So maybe I'll throw it to you, YG." "Yeah, I would say for us, I'm pretty sure it's very different from the other guys, being in Europe. I would say our main constraint is really access to power. When we're talking a few megawatts, it's doable, but nobody's talking a few megawatts today on an AI cluster. We're talking tens of megawatts, and it's complicated in Europe. We have a very good and very stable electrical grid, but it is quite complicated to connect to and to be sure that it's going to be done in a timely manner. And I would say another part of the constraint is as well, kind of on the being-in-Europe side of it, on the supply chain. We are talking a lot with everybody that is doing OCP, Open Rack, and so on and so forth, but nobody's building in Europe. So we have to import everything to Europe, and it's a bit of a pain on that front. And I'm pretty sure, like the discussion that we have with James and all of the co-panelists, a year ago it was completely different, and in a year it's going to be completely different. So it's very, it's really a pure snapshot right now on exactly what is a pain for me and for us, and it's most likely going to change in the next few months." "How about you, JM?" "Well, Cloud was ready. In my latest blog post, I came up with, you know, the four 'Gettings.' It was: getting facilities, getting electrons, getting hardware, and getting customers. And I think, you know, getting the right sequence of events where, now some of the neoclouds are getting unprecedented demand, but now we have to go solve all the rest of those gaps. It was nice to see the presentation from Google on making some of these data center specs available so that we can give these to our data center partners well in advance. I think SemiAnalysis had something along the lines of like, 'If it's involved in the AI computing stack, it will be sold out.' I think as I mentioned, storage is definitely the unsung hero of AI. We are basically predicting that this next-gen inference that's going to be driving scale with KV Cache will be driving up to 16 terabytes per GPU in the Rubin family. People are not prepared for how much data centers need to grow just to accommodate the storage for inference. So, this is some of the stuff that we could pathfind through our lab." "Awesome. Okay, let's go to Amy and Peter. So, Peter, Crusoe: an energy-first neocloud. One of the things when we think about energy is the rack and how many kilowatts, as we saw the curve on Javier's slides. So, when you're thinking about high-powered racks, liquid cooling is not optional, as Javier said, anymore. Can you tell me a little bit about what you guys are doing from a cooling strategy perspective?" "Yeah, absolutely. So, from a cooling strategy, right? Like Javier said, liquid cooling is not an option anymore. And so that has two implications at Crusoe. One, from a siting standpoint, right? Everything that we're siting now is going to be DLC-capable. And so from that perspective, Crusoe in particular, we utilize closed-loop, non-evaporative systems. And so we have to make sure that the new sites that we have in our pipeline and that we're currently developing abide by that. Secondly, from the OCP and the hardware standpoint, this also gives us an opportunity for packing more capacity into the same amount of density. I'm talking specifically from an ancillary services standpoint, right? So, your typical server, CPUs, and storage nodes, aside from the GPU nodes. And so from that perspective, going from 2U form factors to 1U form factors so that we can also align to some of the liquid cooling that's being required from the NVIDIA side to reach these higher kilowatts per rack. You know, what that actually gives us is more flexibility in terms of utilizing stranded power, right? So being able to fit more capacity into the same form factor, and at a more granular, say, 1U level, that gives us more ability to utilize the stranded power. So, you know, from that perspective, that's how Crusoe's currently thinking about liquid cooling moving forward." "Amy, maybe tell us what Denvr is doing in the space and how it relates to OCP, too." "Yeah, absolutely." So there's a push and a pull between thermal and power limitations and constraints that really drive a lot of infrastructure design for everyone. You know, there's a drag-and-drop way that you can build, you know, at the hyperscale level. For everyone else, many times it becomes a much more custom decision based on your own workload needs, your own compute needs, and your existing facility. From a cooling perspective, Denvr is is actually cooling environment agnostic. We are performance-driven. What that allowed us to do is actually to make a commitment to liquid cooling. We were probably the first or one of the first neoclouds in the space about four years ago to actually implement immersion cooling and DLC in our data centers for production use. So this isn't a science experiment; it's actually proven to be reliable in production. From an OCP perspective, OCP has been an amazing forum for us to find forward-thinking innovators like ourselves that have really complementary skill sets and capabilities. We've involved ourselves in many projects within OCP that have allowed us to really progress the technology for our own internal implementation and also to enable the general ecosystem around us. So, tomorrow we actually do have a talk where we're going to be highlighting one of our innovations that came through collaboration. It's an immersion-optimized server design, it's a GPU server, it's an HS or an SXM H200 server. And the really cool thing that we were able to do by thinking outside the box and collaborating was to shrink the form factor of an H200 SXM server from 6U, which is normally about this big, down to 3OU, so about half the volume. We were able then to also think differently about the thermal profile in that server, and by doing so, we're able to go from a traditional deployment which can only fit about two or four H200s per rack to a system that actually supports 14 H200s per rack. That is an incredible advancement in performance and density that you can achieve, and we've been actually operating those servers in an immersion environment for two years now. So again, it's not a science experiment; it actually is a reliable means for us to get our work done. Okay, great. Let's follow the OCP thread. This audience builds and ships OCP-recognized hardware. So I wanted to ask, specifically in terms of like tangible impact on your ability to deploy AI, where has an OCP standard or specification or design kind of benefited your guys' different neoclouds? Maybe start with Peter. Yeah, so from the Crusoe side, I think the ORV3 rack spec has been most impactful on Crusoe. You know, one of the things that I've learned at Crusoe in my time here is, when you're operating at scale, standardization is key to speed, right? And so from that perspective, knowing that we are deploying an ORV3 rack and knowing which GPU can can fit in there is very key. One, for speed of deployments, and two, also just knowing what other GPU SKU optionality we have, right? And you know, the key that I'm trying to make here is that we can't build a snowflake for every data center or every deployment. And if we do, what we found is that just slows us down, and so hence the standardization is key. And lastly, on top of that, right, I think one of the understated things is that in this AI craze, we think about GPUs on a year-by-year basis. But remember, data centers are long-term leases or long-term investments, right? So 10, 12, 15, 20 years. And so from that standpoint, right, I have to think about this from a multi-generation hardware life cycle perspective. Knowing that there's a standard over the upcoming years whereby servers and GPUs will fit in there, that goes a long way in our planning process and deployment process. How about you, Amy? ORV3 has been certainly impactful for us as well. It has allowed us to design hardware, like I just mentioned, that actually can be adaptable for a different cooling environment. It allows us to scale quickly. It also gives us a little bit of a de-risk from a reliability perspective and also a supply chain perspective. So for us, it also comes back to economics. If there are consistent standards, it means there are more examples of that standard being used. It builds confidence in us that we can actually implement this technology in a way that our customers will will enjoy, and that it won't really require a lot of of maintenance. We we understand exactly what the profile of that technology and the interconnected nature of that technology is. For us, the other the other group that has been extremely impactful has been the immersion project. The immersion project has multiple different workstreams underneath it; it's an area where we contribute quite often. Again, it's a new technology area for some, and it allows us to have a public forum where we can disclose, "Hey, these are our success stories, these are our challenges," and to work collaboratively to resolve them. Cool, JM, you've got the mic. I know you've got a recent FarmGPU logo on an OCP contribution. Yeah, it's exciting. As of as of today, the the Hedgehog and Celestica open network fabric for inference and training is is on the OCP contributions page. We were the first neocloud to pilot that, and a big part of that was actually going through, you know, the real nickel benchmarks and actually benchmarking the entire cluster performance, comparing it versus the best-in-class InfiniBand and tuning the performance. So that was all part of this reference architecture. We're going to be open-sourcing all the tweaks that were needed, you know, to make it work at scale, and, yeah, sharing the learnings with the community. That's awesome. Yeah, so this is the key OCP questions, so why don't we round out the panel, YG. How about Scaleway? Yeah, I'm going to be a bit of a broken record around answering ORV3, obviously. For me, it's super interesting, and it's exactly what Peter and Amy said: it's our ability to copy-paste it everywhere. We're moving from something that was super complicated, where you're saying, "Oh, but I want like a multi-page Excel file to be sure that it's going to fit in a data center," to just one question: Is it Open Rack V3 compliant? Yes or no, that's it. So, it's very, very nice to be able to just copy-paste it everywhere. Awesome. Okay, so we've got a European and a Canadian neocloud here, and I know that one of the regional themes is that regulators are pushing hard on, you know, digital and data sovereignty. So, can you tell a little bit about how OCP's open standards have kind of helped your ability to serve customers that need those sovereign infrastructures? Maybe we can start with YG, since you've got the mic and you're based here. Indeed, I'm going to do the European side of it. I would say for us, it's kind of jumping back to ORV3 and being able to copy-paste everywhere. In Europe, it's a single continent, but there are a lot of different countries, legislations, and everything. Obviously, everybody wants their data in their country, and it's a good and a bad thing at the same time. It's impossible to say, "Oh, I'm going to do a 2-gigawatt data center somewhere in Europe and roll with it," because nobody is going to want exactly that. But the fact that we're able to, with OCP, have one reference design and have like three questions to any data center to be sure that it's going to fit in or not, makes it very easy. We can say, "Oh, you want AI, you want an edge spot somewhere. Are you compliant with A, B, and C? Yes? Okay, we can deploy." Or if you're not, we can just change and tweak a bit to be compliant with that, and it's going to be easy to deploy on that front. Cool. Yeah, maybe let's pass the mic to Amy. So, originally, I think most companies actually kept their data center on-prem. So, if you're sitting in the room and you're shaking your head and you're like, "Yeah, I, I know. We used to do that. It was a long time ago." The reason why that changed is because people started moving to the cloud. They lost that connection to their servers, to their data, to their workloads. And now what we're seeing is a dramatic change back in the other direction. It's not necessarily on-prem, but the foundational question that is driving it really is, "Where is my data?" And what level of control do I have over my data? Do I have access to it? Do I understand the laws that the company that is responsible for my data is beholden to? The data center might be located in Canada, but if the parent company isn't, then your data might be subjected to the local regulations and laws of the parent company. And so it's really important now to understand, as AI becomes a strategic imperative both in the public sector and also in the private sector, to have that visibility. Sovereignty is something that isn't new; it's just something that's being asked for in this new technology space of AI. There are concerns about it, and we're actually seeing quite a bit of customer interest now, which is a difference from last year. I know you asked earlier, James, "What has changed in the past year?" This is something that's at the forefront of customers' minds now. So, Denvr has been actively working in this space to be able to deploy our modular data centers for for the public sector, for the private sector, specific to customers that do care about sovereignty and control. So, that's something that is that is top of mind for us. It's something that I know everybody in this room is also working through today. Awesome. Okay, so we're we're running out of time, but I wanted, maybe JM, you're involved in in the workstream very closely, do you want to give a shout-out for people that don't know about the workstream yet? Yeah, actually we have, I think, like 70 members on on the mailing list right now, and we've got good participation. You know, we had the kickoff at the Global Summit, and we had folks like Crusoe, Lambda, and CoreWeave, you know, kind of chime in. We're getting a lot of feedback on what what would be the largest impact activities for OCP scaling neoclouds to work on. Like for instance, last week we had the the hardware group come in and tell us about all the things going on in Redfish and hardware management, unified systems for multi-vendor GPU and XPU deployments, and just a lot of like common problems that we're all going to be solving. So, if you guys want to chime in, you don't have to be a neocloud. We have lots of members from the rest of the OCP community that can kind of just learn from what our challenges are, and either we're going to be the customers or the partners, or, you know, deploying the the software. So. Thanks, JM, thanks, everyone. Let's give the panel a round of applause. Please welcome AMD Corporate Vice President, Architecture and Strategy, Datacenter Solutions Group, Robert Hormuth. Morning, OCP! How are we doing today? Everybody had a good start to the day? It's a quiet crowd. I do have a point in this where I'm going to ask you to stand up or raise your hand, so there may be some exercise involved here. But what I want to talk today about is, how are we going to, you know, progress in the future from really kind of the—well, the title, right?—"From Training Titans to Inference for Billions." You know, how are we going to use open infrastructure in this next era of AI to really do that? And I think to get started, we have a little step back in time. Everybody thinks, you know, AI is what, a year, two, to five years old? You know, this was actually research back in the '50s by Alan Turing—some of the original research. And if you think of the journey the industry had to go on from the 1950s, 75 years later, this is probably the slowest tech adoption curve we've ever seen from when it was first stipulated or researched. But if you look at all the problems that we had to solve in the industry, first we had to get off of vacuum tubes to transistors. We had to invent semiconductors. We had to invent the CPU. We had to invent DRAM. Somewhere along the way, we had to invent storage, and then we had to connect it together with networking, and then we had to figure out how to do data centers. And it's been a long journey. It's been a 75-year journey up to this point. It feels like a hockey stick, and it certainly is. But if you really step back and think about the time and all the steps that we've had to progress, we still have a lot of those same steps ahead of us—they're just nuanced and a little different. And so, if we talk about Inference for Billions, what does this mean? You know, we're we're really shifting to scaling, you know, intelligence from—you know, the initial wave was around, you know, these foundational models, large training models. You've seen a lot of the different folks talk about Gemini and how big they are. But if we're going to have Inference for Billions, it's not going to just be the jumbo giant foundational models. We're going to have a large scale of AI from the smallest device to the largest device, to, you know, very specific models to very complex models. And OCP is going to play a critical role here, not just for the titans but for everybody if we're going to really scale this to billions and billions of users around the world. And so, we've got to collaborate, and that's where, you know, OCP has been a great foundational place to bring us together to collaborate. And again, if we talk about the first wave of AI, it really started with, you know, the the hyperscaler GPU clusters, the foundational models. You know, we all had the, you know, the ChatGPT moment that went hockey stick, and and we've been on that hockey stick. And it was very centralized data centers that were very, very specialized to do that foundational training. But that did limit some of the flexibility to how many people could embrace training or inference because it was very centralized in a few big data centers, and so that was really the first wave. We're starting to shift. You know, the gravity is shifting now towards this, you know, from training to inference, right? It's moving to training models—you know, we're still always going to have training models, we're always going to need training. But if we're going to have inference for billions, we have to start thinking about the global distribution importance, state sovereignty, and power restrictions in different parts of the world. Not everybody's going to stand up a megawatt data center or a megawatt rack, so we have to think broader. And so, we have to think about the new infrastructure priorities to to enable that. And inference is really a different problem. You know, it's more cost-focused, tokens per dollar. It's more about throughput efficiency. It's about real-time; it's about data, you know, processing the data. Now, I for one, you know, I'm happy to know that, you know, we drive the Waymos of the world, but I'm kind of happy that the intelligence is in the car and not across some, you know, cellular link somewhere deciding to turn left or right before it hits a person or something. So I'm kind of glad that it's at the edge of the car. But that means also we have to get operational efficiency. And operational efficiency is not just the energy, the sustainability, but the supply chain. You know, as part of the operational deploy and use, we've got to make sure we have a diverse supply chain. How many people right now would like to have less DRAM manufacturers? Anybody? I I think we'd all like to see a little bit more right now; we'd like to see more competition, more supply. And so, we have to get to the scalable and flexible infrastructure, exactly what Amy was talking about, you know, for the large training side. And we have to make sure that we work together to avoid closed systems. We have to limit our embracing of the closed systems because, at the end of the day, they don't scale. All right, you've all heard the saying, "If you want to go fast, go alone; if you want to go far, go together." That's what OCP is all about. We have to go far if we're going to reach billions to every corner of the world, and there are huge advantages to these open systems. And what I wanted to highlight from an AMD standpoint is, you know, what do you get with open? So, we we've talked publicly about our Helios rack that's coming in in Q3, but what I wanted to highlight is we didn't invent everything. We actually leveraged a ton of stuff from OCP. Yes, we have some new contributions that we'll be making when it—that we'll be making. But if you look at the list, right, we have, you know, from everything from Open Open Rack Wide to the quick connects, to the DCSCM, to the OCP NIC, to OCP Safe for security, to OpenSIL for firmware, we've leveraged. We didn't have to reinvent the wheel; we actually got to leverage a lot of your work. And so, my exercise for you: how many of you were involved in any of these projects? Raise your hand. Anybody? Well, I want to thank you all. Thank you for helping us accelerate innovation in the industry by bringing it together. And, you know, the the point is, while it's great to work on shiny new contributions and continue to innovate, leveraging work that's already been done is what OCP is about. Yes, we can go work on new items and bring them into the market, but leveraging so we can go faster is how we're going to get it to billions and billions. And so we were able to use, you know, 16—I know there's more, that's about all I could fit before I shrunk the text. But we leveraged a ton of OCP so we didn't have to reinvent the wheel. And what did we achieve? What did we achieve with OCP and all that leveraging of Helios? Right, we, you know, made a new rack-scale architecture. This is, you know, it's a—it's a big boy rack scale; it's an OR Wide. It's about 7,000 pounds, it's about 300 kilowatts, so it's not for the faint of heart, but it's for that foundational training and large, large scale inference. But, we were able to leverage many industry standards, right? We used our Infinity Fabric—the fabric between our CPU and GPU for that cache coherency. That one's not open; that one's an AMD proprietary. But we were able to donate part of that into the ecosystem for the UA Link over Ethernet for our scale-up network. We were able to use UEC for the scale-out network, and we were able to connect our AI NIC on that UA Link so that we could actually get more bandwidth with less lanes because UA Link runs faster than PCIe. So again, we were able to leverage these standards. And that's been able to let us do, you know, co-hardware/software design. So, for day zero, we come out of the box with optimized models, and the proof is in the pudding here. I mean, we've got 72 GPUs in this Helios rack, 260 terabytes per second of aggregate bandwidth, you know, for this full big load-store shared memory pool between all the GPUs to work on the biggest models and the smallest models of inference at the same time. And, you know, this is great, but the world is not always going to just embrace that rack scale. There are other users of of inference, and, you know, in the industry, we've had kind of two other form factors that have been embraced for that sub-rack category. Not everything—you know, liquid cooling is definitely going to be needed in some of the biggest, but there's still a lot of environments, a lot of enterprises, and a lot of edge that are going to need air-cooled solutions. And, we've been embracing, you know, the 600-watt CEM, or the UBB OAM form factor, and I would argue that the PCI CEM is going to continue. I mean, that's the standard interface for volume, high volume servers—that's going to continue. On the UBB OAM, you know, AMD has been embracing that openness for a number of generations, but it's really run out of steam. There's a lot of challenges with the form factor, the, the shadowing of the GPUs makes air cooling almost impossible, and, you know, it's it's challenging. It's running out of steam. So, I'm happy to say that, you know, we've been working with some other partners in the industry that you'll get to see a session on today, for the first time. We kicked off a project in OCP called OAC, or the Open Adapter Card, and the way to think about this is, this is basically just a vertical OAM. We took the limitations of the OAM and flipped it vertically, so we get some Z-height relief, we get rid of the shadow GPU, and we get rid of cable flyover and power delivery problems. And we've been working with Intel, AMD, Nvidia, Qualcomm, and Dell on a private workstream that we'll discuss tomorrow on the 30th at—what is that? Well, we'll do a a deeper dive into how this is progressing and our goal of getting a 1.0 spec, you know, towards the Global Summit, towards the end of the year. And the idea behind this is a multi-vendor ecosystem. You know, we're going to we're going to specify the OAC card. OEMs, ODMs, neoclouds, and hyperscalers can innovate at the platform, rack scale, and design all they want. And the great thing that we hear from the broad base of customers that we've talked to is, you know, the intent of OAC is we're going to continue air cooling all the way up through liquid cooling, so we will be able to do both because not every environment is going to have—not going to have liquid. So, again, please attend the session tomorrow if you want to learn more and provide feedback. So, at the end of the day, right, open wins. Open collaboration is is what's going to win at the end of the day. That leads us to sustainable AI. You know, we do need to work on like some shared standards and platforms and benchmarking for AI to make that a little bit easier for everybody. How do we compare and contrast? And OCP is a great coordination layer, right? We have MOUs with OCP between UEC, UA Link, MLPerf, and different things. It's a great coordination point that we can go, you know, bring everybody together, whether they're in OCP or they're outside, but bring it all together in one place under the hood. And so AMD, as you know, is very committed to open, and that's going to be our continued theme. And so, my kind of call to action here is, you know, commit to open, open modular infrastructure. Amy talked about the need for flexibility. Engage actively in the open ecosystems, whether it's in OCP or UEC or UA Link, you know, participate because all of these things are going to have to come together for us to scale. And we really have to start thinking about investing now, you know, planning for the rack scale, or advanced cooling technologies, or advanced fabrics. If we don't want the last—if we don't want 75 more years before we hit billions, we need to accelerate the adoption for all because at the end of the day, you know, inference is a is a global—at global scale, it is not a single-company problem. It requires us to have this open collaborative ecosystem. So, thank you very much, and have a good rest of your track at OCP. Please welcome Microsoft Azure Partner Architect, Systems Planning and Architecture, Bryan Kelly. Thank you, thank you very much. Yes, it's great to be back here in EMEA, and especially in in Barcelona, one of my personal favorite places to visit. Speaking of EMEA, we should talk about some of our data center operations here. So, Microsoft has operations in over 16 European countries with over 200 data centers across the continent. These data centers are designed with sustainability and energy efficiency throughout the design. They support European independence, and are the foundation for Microsoft's EU Data Boundary. They're advancing the digital transition and generating economic growth for the region.
