According to IDC, Microsoft AI and cloud infrastructure in Spain will add more than 10 billion Euros to the national GDP, and it will contribute to the creation of over 77,000 jobs by 2030. Spain has become a strategic AI cloud hub for Europe, and it's entered a new phase of digital competitiveness. That's why Microsoft opened Spain Central in Madrid, our first cloud region in Spain, and it's why we're advancing a major multi-campus expansion in Aragon. The Madrid data center is designed to achieve a power usage effectiveness, which is a PUE, of 1.12. And in this climate, that's among the lowest in the industry. The backup generators in that facility in Madrid run on renewable biofuel, which reduces the net carbon emissions. The expansion in Aragon will run our next-generation closed-loop cooling design, which has zero wastewater. The expansion is about enabling Spain's public sector and regulated industries to use advanced AI while respecting local resources. These data centers will deliver sovereignty and resilience all within the country's EU Data Boundary. Building efficient data centers is, of course, only one dimension. To maximize value, at-scale infrastructure operations must also be power-aware. A software-defined data center is where critical infrastructure, such as power and cooling infrastructure in the data center, along with AI and compute, storage, and network infrastructure, are all operating under a unified control plane, designed with ML-driven decisions to shape resources and workloads for maximum performance and maximum efficiency. This truly unlocks the benefits of operating at scale, and going forward, Microsoft will collaborate with the Open Compute Community on our learnings in this space and the techniques that we use. Complementing power-efficient data center designs and power-aware operations is power-efficient infrastructure. Microsoft's Maia 100 chip embodies that. It's designed for efficient inference at scale. The power-performance optimized Maia 100 chip delivers 30% better performance per dollar than our latest-generation hardware. Maia 100 delivers 10 petaflops of FP4, and 5 petaflops of FP8, in under 750 Watts. While efficient design, efficient operations, and efficient infrastructure help us scale, security is the permission to scale and the permission to do business in a digital economy. It underpins every layer of our tech stack, and is our number one priority. This focus is visible in our sovereign cloud offerings. Digital sovereignty is the capability to participate in the digital economy securely, independently, and with self-determined controls. Microsoft has the most compre-comprehensive set of sovereign controls. The offering spans public cloud and private infrastructure, and ensures customers can choose the right balance of compliance and capability for their needs. The solutions are designed to enable organizations to have more control across the public sector, as well as cater to the specialized needs of sovereign private clouds and national partner ecosystems. From a physical infrastructure perspective, we have sovereign controls at every layer of our hardware stack. Every layer shown here represents a design point to prevent unauthorized access, tampering, or external dependency from undermining an organization's control of its digital estate. Azure Boost isolates control-plane functions from customer data processing, reinforcing security boundaries in hardware. Azure Boost was designed in collaboration with standards from the PCI-SIG standards body. Caliptra is an open-source silicon root of trust designed for trustworthy computing. Caliptra provides transparency of the silicon root of trust, enabling verifiable and auditable security right down to the deepest layers of the silicon. Caliptra was designed in collaboration with Open Compute. Confidential compute is the cornerstone of sovereign data protection. It ensures data remains protected in use, as well as at rest and in transit. Microsoft is a founding member of the Confidential Compute Consortium. Datacenter Secure Control Module (DCSCM) secures baseboard management controllers that provide hardware health monitoring to ensure they are secure. This is another Open Compute innovation designed through collaboration. An integrated HSM is a hardware security module built directly into the server. It protects cryptographic keys in use and ensures only data owners control access; these modules are essential for sovereign data protection. Together, these layers create a vertically integrated trust chain from silicon to software. They form the foundation of what makes our sovereign cloud a reality. There are things I wanted to mention here about the project. Yeah, thanks, Paolo. I can confirm I had the privilege of having front-row seats to watch this massive collaborative effort with dozens of companies involved. So, internally, it's been an exceptional experience and an incredible piece of work that they've been able to produce. Shifting externally a little bit to Brian, you're working across facilities, real estate, and IT teams on this. In your experience, where does the conversation most break down, and who is usually in the room when it does? Thanks, Rob. So, really, we have, with all those areas that you mentioned, a nexus of codes and standards where you actually need to be able to permit a facility and to be able to do so with standards that are available to actually use and put in place. So, there's been a concentrated effort on being able to take the building blocks that have been really worked on over time within CurrentOS, ODCA, and so forth, and that have resulted in gap analysis being led by UL on the North American side. That is being further worked out on the IEC side to similarly address the challenges that exist in this emerging technology space. Really, our effort has been to identify through this white paper common terms so that we can be able to share the same language—things like low voltage. Certainly, in the rack space, it's had a certain meaning, traditionally more associated with the 50-volt existing distribution within that rack. As we look at the data center facility, which is where this power distribution paper is based out of, we consider what is relevant to the actual codes that we have in place. Whether it's in Europe for the Low Voltage Directive or whether it's in North America with the National Electric Code, 1500 volts is the level at which we consider. And so, we especially then look to how we can actually scale things. Is it 400, 800, 1600? Well, that would be a challenge, and so, one of the things that we've actually already taken from this white paper effort is putting in place public inputs to try to change the landscape. We want to make provisions so that one cannot have to discard power sources that have been 800 volts, but actually, in a restricted way and in a safe way, be able to control that voltage down to 750 as a max. You could put two of them in series, things like this. So, yeah. Excellent. Great examples. Tony, as we solve through those problems, then we actually have to get things in the field. You're leading proof of concept on interoperability. What does success look like, and how far away are we? So, I'm going to answer with two steps. First is interoperability—what does it mean? In other words, it's plug and play. It's putting together pieces of equipment from different manufacturers, and it should work together. So today, we can explain how it works on paper, but we need to demonstrate it in the field. So where we are today: today we did this excellent white paper that you can read—150 pages or more. There are tools on the internet where you can summarize it, too. So, that kind of explains this concept, but now we need to shift to reality and do some proofs of concept so we can verify that our hypotheses are true, and then we can just scale up. And what we want to make sure is, when we go in the field, this type of system—LVDC architectures, high power, higher voltage—we want to verify that it is stable, safe, and scalable. Excellent, thank you. I'm going to give an advance here so you can kind of see where we're headed next while we get into a couple more questions. Paolo, I'll bring it... I'm sorry, JP, I'll bring it back to you. Kind of more as we realized this was a problem. You've been inside this problem a long time. It actually was your move over to Eaton to focus on power. What was the lightbulb moment for you when you were like, "This is the next thing we have to go solve"? Yeah, I was at Oracle at the time. For context, I led data hall design for all of Oracle Cloud Infrastructure, so most of the air-cooled standard designs and the liquid-cooled standard designs I directly led or directly supported. And we were designing the 131,000 GPU cluster, and I realized that from a congestion management standpoint alone, I needed to be able to coordinate that better. And then from the power density map, I couldn't fit 16 whips into one rack. That was 24 months ago. So, I realized that I wanted to get the first scale-up going, or at least the design done, but I realized I had to pivot quickly to what was going to stop us. That was going to be... we're talking about 800V direct current, but in addition to that, fault ride-through and AI waveform mitigation were the first two, which were going to be impacted either in an AC or a DC architecture. But then once you get to the actual densities you need, you're going to need to have that direct current just to fit it all in one box so that you can then actually operate it. A little bit more about my past: I was a critical facility engineer at one time in the industry, so I've personally had to spin these up, doctor revenue, and touch every little portion of it. So how the ergo person is actually going to be able to build, maintain, and service this is going to directly affect your OpEx and then your token revenue generation. Excellent, thank you. Paolo, as we have kind of the timeline here, the roadmap, when you look at what the subproject has produced so far, what's the piece of work you're most proud of, and why do you think it matters to the people here in the room? So, I'm really proud of the collaborative effort that this project was able to deploy in such a short time. I see people here in the room who were contributing, who were really rolling up their sleeves and writing text and contributing. I think from the beginning, we had more than 90 official meetings and many unofficial meetings during these months. It has been a very interesting ride. I really invite everybody to join the breakout sessions tomorrow to learn more and to contribute. So, the doors are more than open. Thanks. Brian, we touched on standards, touched on things, and others have touched on technology. So is it a technology problem, a standards problem, or a people problem? So, I'd say it's a mixture of all of those. And really, this is one of the reasons why we've kicked off three new workstreams within the power distribution project. Those include the operations side of controlling safely as we move from 50 volts to 800 volts, people's interactions, and so forth; and the EMC aspects and performance. Obviously, there are a lot of communications and a lot of challenges here, but how can we also keep in mind the personal safety aspects of this associated with grounding technologies? Ultimately, we also need this to be interoperable and work. The head start already exists on the Mount Diablo side. We recognize that, and we want to be able to both interface with that but also scale and prepare for the future as voltages go higher and so forth. We really look forward also to that collaborative joint project between power distribution and rack and power. Thanks, Brian. All right, Tony, we've had, I mean, an incredible amount of momentum. Paolo mentioned the amount of work we got to see get produced in five months. I mean, it really was ideated in October. It was a global summit where we really pulled the crew together here, the team together, and it grew so quickly. I think I heard that the groups.io subscription—so if you're on the OCP website and you click the mailing list, that's how you join these individual conversations—was pushing as one of the top. It's hundreds of people who are interested in this topic. So if the party is going to continue and our momentum is going to stay high, Tony, what in the next 12 months do you think needs to happen? So in the next 12 months, first, we need to update the white paper. We're going to have feedback, so we just need to update and listen to others. And second, as I said, we need to enter real-life installations. We can leverage what others have done before like CurrentOS and ODCA—they have projects, so we can learn from them. We can learn from simulation, but simulation is only as good as the input you enter. So right now, sometimes I feel that we flirt between the line of what we know and what we think we know. We need to, again, be in the field and test. And this is kind of the call to action right now for everyone in the room: we need to build a megawatt-scale proof of concept, not a 10-kilowatt setup in the garage to try it out. We need to do something real, and we need to have something where we can easily swap equipment because there will be different manufacturers and technologies. That way we can play with it, verify whether our theories are good or not, and then come back to the white paper or other specs to update them. In the next couple of weeks and months, I hope we can achieve this kind of thing. Excellent, thank you. You know, I think the questions I asked deep-dove into each of your areas of expertise, or at least the scope of work that you've had in this project. I want to open this up to the panel, so anybody can feel free to answer this next one. Maybe before you walked into this room, you weren't thinking about direct-current power so much. When does it become a problem for people in this audience, and, you know, how much warning will they actually get that DC power is the barrier and is at their doorstep? Yeah, yeah, I'll go back to what I learned from liquid cooling, right? So, I developed liquid cooling at Oracle along with Craig Pennington, Vali Sorell, and many, many others at Oracle. So, we started talking about it in '23, we realized we had to invent it in '24, and then it rolled out in '25. We talked about direct current in '25, we've been actively working on this right now in '26, and I would expect a similar response in the market—that '27 is likely to be at your doors. Anybody agree or disagree? Is everybody on the same page? DC is not new. It's something that has been used in different industries—railway, marine, and so on—because there was a need. And I think right now we have the need. DC for data centers is not new as well; it has been done in other geographies. But here we need to shift because of the specific reasons that we explain in the white paper, and that JP explained. So it's a need, and we have to do it. Excellent, thank you. I'll close it out with one question. We have deep-dive sessions tomorrow, which Paolo mentioned, and they're up here. So if you have questions, there's no opportunity here on this stage, but if you get into the breakout rooms, those are the opportunities to ask questions. Please join us. For anyone who walks out of here in the next few minutes, what's the one thing that you think they should carry with them? I think direct current is something we can do. It's coming. It helps tackle certain certain challenges, and there is a body of knowledge available across the industry through collaboration. So, please join us in in this journey, and every contribution is more than welcome. Excellent. Well, thank you all. To the audience, let's give our collaborators a round of applause, please. Please welcome Meta Software Engineer, Paul Saab. Hello everyone. Super excited to be here today. Hope you all are still awake. I'm a software engineer at Meta. I've been there for over 18 and a half years, and I've worked across all of our infrastructure, you know. When I started we were 40 engineers; I don't know how many we are today—a lot—but I've seen massive amounts of scale. You know, I've worked across our storage, our network, our compute, and now, most recently, AI. But what is infrastructure at Meta, right? We build and design our own data centers. We deploy the networks inside of those data centers. We also build the backbone that connects all of our data centers together. We build and design our own servers, as we all know. More recently, we've been designing our own silicon. But what's also true is we build all of the foundational software that runs every service at Meta—from Facebook, to Messenger, to Instagram, Threads, WhatsApp, and now Meta AI. I'm sure many of you use at least one, if not multiple. But on the AI side, we're starting to integrate AI across all of our product offerings, you know, whether it's chatting in our apps, taking a photo and asking the AI, "What is this photo?" or transcribing your own audio, or even doing live translation through the Meta AI glasses. By the way, those are really cool. Or it could be what my kids like to do, which is imagining themselves as a unicorn rainbow using Meta AI. Or as I like to do, putting a shark swimming behind me as I do my weekly Alcatraz swims. AI is everywhere. But AI is also causing a massive power explosion. You see, it took us just over 15 years to reach 1 gigawatt of capacity. This year, we are deploying 1 gigawatt in one location. So let's rewind: 15 years, 1 gigawatt deployed across the world, and now we're putting it in one location. Crazy! Well, not that crazy, because over the next few years, we're going to be putting five of those in one location in our high-power data center the size of Manhattan. Okay, I thought the first 15 years of my career was crazy scale; now we're compressing all of that scale and accelerating it exponentially. So what does this mean? Power is the new currency of the world. It is the new gold. And so, I don't want to waste my currency. I'm going to look at how I can optimize my power to get more compute and more AI in our existing footprints. We started to look at our rack power, right? Eighty percent of the power that's delivered to our racks is CPU and memory. But you know, the optimizations of the past, over the last 20-plus years of the company, have been taking code and optimizing it. You'd think after 20-plus years we'd be pretty good at optimizing, and so there are very few places left for us to eke out massive step-function changes in our CPU utilization. Right? We still see wins—1% here, 1% there—which still add up at the gigawatt scale, but we really need a step-function change. Many of you have heard about Arm. It runs in our phones, our laptops; every hyperscaler has Arm in their fleet. We didn't. And one of the big reasons why a company of our size might be reluctant is you have to port your code, right? Because you can't just rely on benchmarks. I can give you a benchmark that shows something amazing, but when I actually run the workload in production, it doesn't really pan out. So when I approached our leadership about how we should go and explore this, I actually got yelled at. I got told, "It's going to take hundreds of engineers, many thousands of hours of cross-functional organizational work, getting it on roadmaps of every team." Basically, in my mind, a lot of FUD. So what did I do? I ignored them all. I took five close engineer friends of mine, and we got to work. And you're going to think, "Five engineers? That's going to take a while; you guys have millions and millions of lines of code." Well, we did it in 30 days. Sorry, 90 days—three months. That was without AI; that was without an LLM. That was just grinding for three months straight. And it wasn't just like one application; every single line of code in our codebase built and ran on Arm. But then the next problem happened: I needed a CPU, and I didn't have one. We went to every partner, and we couldn't find anybody that would deliver a minimally TCO-neutral CPU. So what do you do? You go to the source. Many of you probably heard, last month we, alongside Arm, co-announced the Arm AGI CPU—you'll hear about it later. But as part of this effort, one of the things that we did not want to have happen was another hyperscaler building an Arm CPU, talking about an Arm CPU, benchmarking their Arm CPU, and you couldn't buy it except from a cloud. Well, we don't run a cloud. So that was one of the reasons why we partnered with Arm, so we could provide and help deliver an Arm CPU for the world. All right. Compute problem solved. What about memory? It's not like there's another architecture that we could just go and fiddle around with to get a step-function change in memory. Well, there is: it's called LPDDR. It allowed us to deliver 15% more cores in a rack than the standard DDR5. Now, you must think we're crazy: laptop and phone memory in the data center? That's not reliable. Well, I'm here to tell you it is. We've deployed thousands and thousands of these units, and it's been working just fine for us. And then, in the future, LP6 will solve many of these issues that you might bring up that make you feel more comfortable about deploying LPDDR in the data center. So let's bring it back to OCP. Later this year, there's going to be rack-level and board-level designs submitted to OCP, so that the world can actually start developing and building Arm-based systems. So that's where you all come in: we want to see more and more innovation in the Arm server space. We want everyone to start to consider and deploy LPDDR, and by working together, we can actually make these things happen, right? OCP was started to basically push the envelope of hardware and data center design, and we'll be able to push the industry to adopt these things if we all work together. And why do we do this? Cat photos, of course! No, but really, there are billions of people that rely on us every day, and we can't continue to deliver those services if we're just wasting money throwing inefficient systems out there. We need to scale, and we need to make those step-function changes so that we can continue to deliver all of these services, and also deliver AI and every other new thing that comes along without having to build gigawatts and gigawatts and gigawatts for life. So, I want to thank you all for listening to me today. I look forward to working with you all. Thank you. Please welcome Arm Vice President of Marketing, Eddie Ramirez. All right, I get the privilege of being the last keynote of today, holding you from lunch. But also, I get the privilege of telling you about what Arm is doing in the data center. And the challenge that Paul did mention was that he wanted a new processor at the speed of software; he wanted development of hardware at the speed of software. So how do we do that? How do we develop leading-edge products at scale and at speed, and then look at how those products can handle the next wave of AI workloads? You've heard Anil and others talk about agentic AI. I wanted to give you a little bit of context on how quickly agentic AI is coming. One interesting statistic was if you just look at OpenCL, when it was released on GitHub just a few months ago, it has now become so popular it is the most starred program on GitHub—past Kubernetes, past Linux. In fact, in March, there were 38 million visitors to the GitHub page. Tens of millions of developers are now out there looking at ways of deploying agents. The velocity of this wave of AI is going to be even faster than the last. So let's think about how many agents will actually be running in an enterprise environment. Well, by some statistics, it'll be hundreds of agents today, climbing to tens of thousands of agents for each enterprise. And the reason that's important is because this is going to have a fundamental shift on how we design and deploy infrastructure equipment in this age of agentic AI. Agents are a force multiplier for inference operations. Agents work autonomously. Yes, they could be your personal assistant, but they're also going to be able to actually do real work and automate workflows. Agents will be running 24/7, cooperating with other agents. It's not far-fetched that by next year's OCP, many of you will be using agents to book your travel, book your meetings, and summarize the sessions that you could not attend. So if we think about agents in mass deployment—and I talked about them being a force multiplier—they're likely going to end up having 15x or higher inference requests than what you see today. And this fundamental change is going to impact not only cloud servers that are going to run agents, but those cloud servers are going to kick off work with other agents and look at AI servers as well. The AI server has typically used processors to really keep the utilization rates of the CPU as high as possible. But with massive amounts of agents, you're going to see this term that's now referred to as an "agent swarm." They're going to use all the capacity—the compute capacity in the cloud today—stress the network, and stress the system design points that were really defined before agents became widely used. And so, the CPU itself is going to have to change. Massive amounts of agents are going to swamp the CPUs of today and force us to think about how we actually scale faster than ever before. The CPU will be the orchestrator of agent workflows, with every core running an agent on high-performance CPUs at the lowest latency possible. So at Arm, we stepped up to this challenge, and we did something we have never done before: we built a silicon CPU product for the data center. There have been several Arm CPUs in the cloud before today, but with the help of Meta and the collaboration that we've had with them, our mission was to deliver the world's most efficient agentic CPU. We wanted something that could deliver best-in-class performance, leveraging our Neoverse V3 processor, something that could scale, and most importantly, deliver the power efficiency that Arm is known for, so that we can actually scale rack density in the data center. If I look under the hood, we tried our best to achieve a balanced design point: 136 Arm Neoverse V3 cores, I/O to be able to support AI accelerators, sub-100 nanosecond latency to memory, memory controllers next to the compute cores, and more importantly, the fastest DDR memory that we could find today. All of this gives you that balanced design point to be able to run agentic AI workloads. Now, to support system designers and really enable a fast track for deployments, we're also building fully production-ready reference servers, leveraging the latest OCP standards. We've got a 1U server compatible with the ORV3 rack designs. In a single 1U, that can give you incredible amounts of rack-scale density, taking the best of the OCP standards that the community has developed and putting them into practical implementations that will be available by the end of this year. Now let's talk about racks. A 36-kilowatt OCP rack can now support up to 30 of these two-node servers. You're talking about an incredible 8,000 of Arm's highest-performing cores in an air-cooled rack. And we didn't stop there; there was a lot of mention today about liquid cooling. By the end of this year, you'll see OCP-compliant, liquid-cooled racks—liquid-cooled racks that are eight-node, 42 wide, with over 45,000 Arm CPUs in such a rack. For those neo-clouds who are already ready to adopt liquid cooling, you can now scale like never before. And so, I want to take a moment to thank our friends at Meta because, as Paul said, their mission was not to build another custom CPU. Their mission was to be able to scale the Arm ecosystem like it hasn't been scaled before. We felt like, in working with Meta, we achieved true software-hardware co-development. While they were optimizing software for Arm, we were building the AGI CPU. Together, we built the most optimized solution possible. We also have several other deployment partners that we announced a month ago, showing examples of solutions that we can build together with hyperscalers and make available through places like OCP. I also want to announce a new partnership, especially being here in Europe. Verda, Europe's fastest-growing neo-cloud provider, is actually working on deploying Arm's AGI CPU in conjunction and alongside Nvidia's GB300 racks. What's amazing about this solution is that we have Arm on both sides. And it's not just an ISA—a common ISA that allows you to deploy side-by-side racks of compute and heterogeneous AI servers. It's the fact that we've been working closely with Nvidia on server specifications that allow more commonality with the way software interacts with these servers. This allows the ability for agents to autonomously assign workloads to either of the two racks, and that's where OCP and standardization really help accelerate hardware deployment. At Arm, we're not just taking from the OCP community; we're trying our best to give back. We're contributing in several areas, but three that I wanted to highlight are around chiplet enablement, server standardization, and server designs. Our chiplet standardization is all meant to be able to deliver custom silicon at a faster rate than ever before, with different vendors working on chip design to accelerate what can be done. Server standardization, as I mentioned, has the whole goal of having software just work on Arm CPUs—whether that's Arm's AGI CPU, Arm CPUs in the cloud, or Arm CPUs from other vendors. If we can reduce the fragmentation, we can accelerate deployments. Additionally, we are committed to contributing our server designs to OCP to allow local European builders to quickly take schematics, reference hardware, and firmware to create derivative designs, proliferating even more solutions into the local European ecosystem. At Arm, it's in our DNA to be an ecosystem company, and we've tried our best to do that for the past 30 years. There's no better place to build ecosystems than within OCP. We're very excited that we've got quite a lot of partners engaged in our chiplet enablement programs. You'll hear about server standardization in sessions today and tomorrow, and soon, you'll see a plethora of server designs. I'm very excited to be part of OCP and to work with this community to see what we can build together. Thank you.

LIVE: Vice President JD Vance Delivers Remarks On Efforts To Combat Fraud In Bangor, ME

Forbes Breaking News(5.6M) ·69:56 ·5.2K views · 2026-05-14
JD Vance (Vice President of the United States)
Vice President JD Vance and the Department of Labor announced a national "zero-tolerance" crackdown on federal benefit fraud, revealing that up to $135 billion in pandemic unemployment funds were stolen and issuing a mandate to all 50 states to imple...
TL;DR Vice President JD Vance and the Department of Labor announced a national "zero-tolerance" crackdown on federal benefit fraud, revealing that up to $135 billion in pandemic unemployment funds were stolen and issuing a mandate to all 50 states to implement stricter Medicaid...

TL;DR

Vice President JD Vance and the Department of Labor announced a national "zero-tolerance" crackdown on federal benefit fraud, revealing that up to $135 billion in pandemic unemployment funds were stolen and issuing a mandate to all 50 states to implement stricter Medicaid oversight or risk losing federal funding.

The Massive Scale of Benefit Fraud

  • Unemployment Insurance (UI) Losses: Acting Secretary of Labor Keith Sonderling reported that of the $888 billion in UI benefits paid during the pandemic, an estimated $135 billion was lost to fraud and improper payments.
  • Egregious System Failures: Federal investigations uncovered systemic lapses in verification, including:
      • Duplicate SSNs: $29 billion paid to the same Social Security numbers across multiple states.
      • Deceased Recipients: $139 million sent to deceased individuals.
      • Underage Claimants: $1 billion sent to children under the age of 14.
  • Maine-Specific Impact: Maine has recorded over $93 million in overpayments, with only $7 million recovered to date.

Federal Task Force and Recovery Efforts

  • Enforcement Strategy: Vice President JD Vance is leading a multi-agency task force to shift the government from a "pay first, verify later" model to a "prevention-first" approach.
  • Asset Recovery: The Department of Labor is currently:
      • Freezing $520 million currently held in bank accounts.
      • Tracking $912 million sitting on dormant prepaid debit cards.
  • Advanced Technology: The administration is deploying AI and advanced identity verification systems to detect fraud rings and international scammers.
  • Bottom-Up Prosecution: Vance likened their strategy to "mob movie" investigations, targeting low-level fraudsters to flip them against high-level criminal enterprise leaders.

State Accountability and Conditional Funding

  • The Medicaid Mandate: The administration recently sent letters to all 50 state Medicaid systems, warning that federal funding will be withheld if states do not aggressively empower Medicare Fraud Control Units (MFCUs).
  • SNAP Reform: Vance called for a complete overhaul of the Food Stamp (SNAP) program, criticizing the current system where states simply "bill the federal government" without verifying if recipients are eligible, alive, or legal residents.
  • Criticism of Maine Leadership: Vance targeted Maine Governor Janet Mills, accusing the state administration of:
      • Failing to cooperate with federal anti-fraud investigators.
      • Allowing "festering" fraud within immigrant communities (citing a specific $15 million case involving interpretive services).
      • Protecting illegal aliens over Maine taxpayers.

The "Non-Victimless" Nature of Fraud

  • Identity Theft: Vance emphasized that fraud victims include citizens whose credit scores are ruined when their identities are stolen by illegal immigrants or fraud rings to claim benefits.
  • Impact on Providers: Fraudulent billing undermines honest physicians and corrupts the Medicaid reimbursement system, threatening the solvency of the program for those who truly need it.
  • Economic Shift: Secretary Sonderling stated the administration's goal is to transition from UI dependency to "high-paying American jobs," specifically highlighting the return of shipbuilding industries to Maine through apprenticeships and workforce development.
"Fraud isn’t just about saving money. It’s not just about protecting taxpayers; it’s about protecting the services that you rely on... Call me old-fashioned, but I don’t think that dead people eat, so certainly they don’t need food stamps."

— Vice President JD Vance

"888 billion dollars in unemployment benefits were paid... as much as 135 billion of that may have been stolen through fraud. Think about that. 135 billion dollars of your money, gone."

— Keith Sonderling, Acting Secretary of Labor

"The threat to the Medicaid program is not us keeping fraudsters away... it’s the fraudsters raiding that program to begin with. That’s the threat to the Medicaid program."

— Vice President JD Vance

If I'ma be single, I'm gonna need a double shot of that heartbreak proof and see the world through whiskey glasses. And I ain't drunk, darlin', no more at AM. Mr. Bartender, hit me again. Don't want to think about her, or wear a ring without her. Don't want to hit the karaoke bar, can't sing without her. So make them drinks strong, cause brother, she's gone. And if I'm ever gonna move on, I'm gonna need some whiskey glasses. Cause I don't wanna see the truth.
