Ain't nothing else to do but sip on Mama's truth and see the world through whiskey glasses. I need a better view of me drinking you. Yeah, line 'em up, line 'em up, line 'em up, line 'em up. Knock 'em back, knock 'em back, knock 'em back, knock 'em back. Fill 'em up, fill 'em up, fill 'em up, fill 'em up. Cause if she ain't ever coming back, I'm gonna need some whiskey glasses. Cause I don't wanna see the truth. She's probably making out on the couch right now with someone new. Yeah, I'm gonna need some whiskey glasses if I'm gonna make it through. If I'ma be single, I'm gonna need a double shot of that heartbreak proof and see the world through whiskey glasses. Ain't nothing else to do but sip on Mama's truth and see the world through whiskey glasses. I need a better view of me drinking you. Yeah. Please welcome Keith Sonderling, Acting Secretary, U.S. Department of Labor. All right, Maine, we're here. Are you excited? Are you excited to hear from our incredible Vice President how he is working every single day to protect your taxpayer dollars? Well, there's no better time or no better place than to be in Maine to have that discussion. So I'm Keith Sonderling, your Acting Secretary of Labor, and in this capacity, I have the privilege to serve on the Vice President's task force to eliminate fraud. That is the honor of my life. You know why? Because Americans like you, you're sick and tired of watching your hard-earned tax dollars get stolen by fraudsters, scammers, and criminals. I know you're also sick and tired of Washington, D.C. turning our back on you and not getting to the bottom of this. Under President Trump's leadership and thanks to Vice President Vance's willingness to make a generational change, those days are over. You know why? Because this administration believes government should serve American workers and job creators. We are not serving those gaming the system anymore. And nowhere was government failure more obvious than in our unemployment insurance system. During the pandemic, what started as emergency relief for hardworking Americans became one of the largest taxpayer dollar frauds in modern history. So 888 billion dollars in unemployment benefits were paid during that time, but guess what? It's estimated as much as 135 billion of that may have been stolen through fraud. Think about that. 135 billion dollars of your money, gone. That was meant for you, for your families, and for hardworking Americans. But instead, criminals, fraud rings, and overseas scammers took this money. But now, due to the Vice President's leadership and his task force, we are working every day to make sure that never happens, to get that money back and hold those accountable with law enforcement. Since the task force began, we've already uncovered widespread fraud and are aggressively preventing it from ever happening again. So here's some examples. We found that nearly 29 billion went to the same Social Security numbers across multiple states. Oh, hold on, I got better ones. 139 million got sent to dead people. All right, ready for another one? This is a good one. One billion was sent to children under the age of 14 seeking unemployment insurance. Under 14, that doesn't work, cause they can't work. So it got so bad that as much as 17 billion dollars in fraud may have gone out in a single month. We learned that our systems were overwhelmed and exploited, and who got stuck with the bill? All you, yeah, the taxpayers. Maine is a smart group because you know all too well about these pandemic uninsurance programs. More than 93 million dollars in overpayments were put on the books here in Maine, and barely seven million has been recovered. But that's changing. Thanks to the Vice President, paying benefits first and verifying later never works. We must prevent fraud before it happens. And President Trump and Vice President Vance looked at this disaster and said, not in this administration. Now the Department of Labor is taking action to restore integrity with one goal: to protect your taxpayer dollars. We are strengthening our identity systems and we're working with artificial intelligence to make sure that this never happens again. We are deploying strike teams in states like California and we're cracking down on organized fraud. Above all, I'm working very closely with our Inspector General, Anthony DiSpizito, who is fighting every single day to recover stolen tax dollars before they disappear forever. Currently, the Department of Labor is working on recovering 520 million dollars that we had banks freeze. We're also tracking down 912 million sitting on prepaid debit cards in dormant accounts, because every dollar we recover is a dollar that belongs to you, the American people. And finally, this administration believes that unemployment insurance was never meant to be a permanent way of life. The system was only designed as a temporary bridge to get workers back on their feet. So not only is this administration restoring accountability to these programs, but more importantly, we're bolstering workforce development programs that work, like apprenticeships. And here in Maine, shipbuilding is coming back. The President promised industry would come back. President Trump is delivering on these jobs here in Maine. We're making sure that you have the skilled workforce, not to create a dependency on these unemployment insurance systems that were scammed, but to make sure that you all have the skills to be in high-paying American jobs. So as I wrap up, let me be clear. As you're about to hear from our incredible Vice President, he has zero tolerance for fraud. His task force is restoring accountability and trust, ensuring that all of your taxpayer dollars benefit you. He is fighting relentlessly to make the federal government accountable to the American people again, and it's an honor to work alongside him as we put you first. Now, for what you've all been waiting for, it is my absolute privilege to introduce the Vice President of the United States of America, JD Vance. Well, we love you too, and it's great to be in Maine today. Now before we get started, I gotta poll the crowd a little bit because, you know, it's lunchtime. And so when we get back on Air Force Two, I told the team we need to get lobster rolls now that we're here in Maine. So what is the recommendation of this Bangor crowd? Where do we get lobster rolls from? We have a lobster roll guy right here? Okay, fantastic. So where do I need to go? Whoa, Eagles Nest? Eagles Nest? What about, somebody—I heard Lobster Boy, is that...? Okay. Word to the wise: never come to a crowd of Maine and ask them where to buy lobster rolls, cause I've gotten 14 answers in the past 15 seconds. But it sounds like we got plenty of options, and I'm proud to be here in Maine and proud to be in a state with hardworking people, hardworking fishermen, hardworking shipbuilders, and the people who make the United States run. God bless you, Maine, I'm proud to be here. Now I want to give a great shout-out to our Acting Labor Secretary Keith, who's done an amazing job helping us fight fraud but also protect American working people. One of the things that the Trump administration, the President, has ordered us to do from day one is we are trying to create good American jobs for the American people. Who is sick of leadership that ships our jobs overseas? Aren't we proud to have a President who protects your jobs and keeps them right here in America? But here's the thing, my friends, because you all work hard, because you all pay your taxes, because you do things the right way, it is time to have leadership in Washington that treats you the right way and protects those hard-earned tax dollars. When the President of the United States said, "JD, we have got a fraud problem and I want you to tackle it," I was so proud and so happy to be able to do it because I realized that fraud isn't just about saving money. It's not just about protecting taxpayers; it's about protecting you, the money you send to the government, and the services that you rely on. So for once, for the first time in a very long time, you've got an administration in Washington, D.C. that is fighting for you, fighting to protect your tax dollars, and fighting to put the fraudsters in prison, which is where they belong. And we're gonna talk about that cause everybody asks me—the President asks me—I hear from people all the time who say, "Is going after fraud hard?" And I say unfortunately no, cause there is a lot of fraud in the federal government. It's like fishing in a barrel. There is always something. Every week I get a report from these guys—we're gonna talk about that in a second—but it's unbelievable how much you have been fleeced by your own government over the past 15, 20, 30 years. Nobody was looking at this, nobody was asking difficult questions like, for example, are dead people getting food stamps? Call me old-fashioned, but I don't think that dead people eat, so certainly they don't need food stamps. Unfortunately, they vote for Democrats; they don't vote for us, my friends. But why is it that we have people who are driving Lamborghinis? And that is true. People driving Lamborghinis who are getting low-income assistance that's supposed to go for housing and food for our people? Why is it that we have people in hospice, claiming hospice benefits, but who have no terminal illnesses? And by the way, here's the crazy thing: very often it's not a person who's even using their own identity. They're claiming that somebody else—they're stealing somebody else's identity, claiming hospice services, and then when that hardworking American goes to apply for some other benefit, they get tagged by the bank as being a fraudster even though it wasn't them. It was somebody who's stolen their identity. Who here knows somebody who's had their identity stolen? Isn't that a shame? At least half of the hands shot up, and the reason why we've allowed identity theft to fester in this country is because the government wasn't going after fraud. And ladies and gentlemen, that changed the moment Donald J. Trump became the President of the United States. Now I want to talk about all that, but I want to give a few notes of shout-out here because we have a lot of great local officials and a lot of great future officials that are here today. First we have your great former Governor Paul LePage. Paul, where are you at, man? There he is. Now Paul is a great guy, a guy who loves the state of Maine. And we're gonna talk a little bit about how he was the biggest advocate for your tax dollars and the biggest threat to fraudsters that ever existed in the state of Maine. And here's the problem is fraud has festered in Maine because this guy is no longer the Governor of Maine. So we're gonna send him to Washington and fight fraud at the federal level. Now my friend and former Senate colleague Susan Collins was not able to be here. Susan is back in D.C.; she doesn't like to miss votes. And here's the thing I'll say about Susan Collins is sometimes I get frustrated with Susan Collins. I almost wish that she was more partisan. But the thing I love about Susan is she is independent because Maine is an independent state. And frankly, if she was as partisan as I sometimes wish that she was, she would not be a good fit for the people of Maine. So let's give a shout-out to Susan Collins who's doing a great job. We have got Senate GOP leader Trey Stewart. Where is Trey at? Raise your hand, Trey. Good to see you, man. A young guy, a good-looking guy, younger and better looking than I am, so I don't like you, Trey. I don't like you, but you're doing a good job. We got House GOP leader Billy Bob Fockinghem. Where's Billy Bob? We've got Ranking Member Chad Perkins. Where's Chad Perkins at? Chad, thank you, man. And we've got the County Sheriff Troy Morton. Where's the County Sheriff at? Troy, thank you. Thank you for your service and for all that you and your law enforcement folks are doing for our great country. We've also got some reporters from the Maine Wire, people who've been fighting fraud. So let's give a round of applause to John Featherston and Steve Robinson. See you guys. So here's the thing that I've learned in fighting fraud for the last few months and focused on this issue: fraud is the ultimate non-victimless crime. There are multiple victims in fraud. What we think about when somebody steals your tax dollars and goes and spends it on something it's not supposed to be spent on, we think of you all as the victims. The American taxpayer, the Maine taxpayer, the people who wake up, who go to work, who do what they're supposed to do and don't want their money stolen by people who are doing the opposite of what they're supposed to do. You are the first victim of fraud. But you know what else I've realized is that you're not the only victim of fraud. Because when people steal your money and it doesn't go to where it's supposed to go, there are a lot of people who are out there who are suffering in the process. I'm talking about physicians who were denied their Medicaid reimbursements because a fraudster stole their identity and charged for services that weren't actually being provided. I'm talking about the good doctors who do what they're supposed to and don't like it when corrupt healthcare providers go and claim services that aren't even necessary. So then the victim is the doctor that gets stolen from, it's the taxpayer that gets stolen from, but it's also the innocent patient who should be able to know that when their doctor prescribes something it's meant for them and it's good for them. I'm talking about the construction worker who can't buy a cell phone because when an illegal alien goes and steals their identity and applies for SNAP benefits, now all of a sudden they're tagged in the banking system and their credit score is ruined. Not because they did a single thing wrong, but because somebody stole their identity. Fraud is exactly what happens when you've got a government that is not fighting for the American people, but is fighting for fraudsters and illegal aliens. And it had to stop, and under the Trump administration, we are fighting it every single day. And again, it is every single day I learn of something new. I learn about people who say that they're claiming for hospice services, but in fact they're not providing any services and they're making hundreds of millions or billions of dollars in the process. Talk about something that we've seen in Minneapolis but also in Maine. We have seen people go out there and say that they're providing services to autistic children, when in reality they maybe don't have any children at all or they certainly don't have autistic children. So they get paid millions and millions of your dollars to provide services that they're not actually providing. And oh by the way, what happened to the autistic children and their families who actually need those services and need a competent government to ensure that they're doing the right thing? My friends, this has gone on for far too long. You have been fleeced by your own government for far too long and we are stopping it every single day. Now let me tell you about a guy though who did not stand for fraud; he did not stand for fraud when he was in the state government and he will not stand for fraud in the United States House of Representatives representing you, and that's Paul LePage. Now I hate to say this because I love this state. The first time I ever visited Maine actually was probably four or five years ago. Actually it was exactly four years ago cause I remember our daughter who's now four was probably three or four months old at the time. And we came up here and we stayed for a week. It was one of the most amazing trips I've ever had in my life because this is a beautiful state, as you all know, with incredible people, with good food. This is America. This is the very best of America right here in communities like this. And so I have a special affection for this state, but I can't even compare to your great former Governor. Because while I love this state as an outsider, he loves this state as somebody who has fought for it every single day. A guy who came to work every single day protecting your tax dollars, protecting your essential services, protecting our local law enforcement, making sure, common sense, that the government actually works for the people who deserve and have the right to be in the United States to begin with. Makes sense, right? That's what it should be. But because I love this state I can say I am heartbroken by what has happened to the fraud that's being perpetrated on the taxpayers of this state. Because outside of Minnesota and California, which are probably number one and number two, if they're the gold medalist and the silver medalist, I heard somebody shout out New York. Maybe New York is the bronze medalist, but let me be honest, maybe Maine is the bronze medalist. Cause if you look at the fraud that has happened in this state, if you look at the illegal immigrant communities who have taken benefits that ought by rights go to the people in this room, what you have in Maine is a festering problem. People have been taken advantage of and they've been stolen from and your government hasn't done anything about it. You ask yourself, why did Maine go from a state that did not have a serious fraud problem to one where I can honestly say it's one of the worst states in the union? I'll give you two answers and two politicians. Number one is Janet Mills and number two is Joe Biden. And thankfully, thankfully, one of them has already been kicked to the curb and one is on her way out the door, exactly as it should be. Now here is what Paul LePage was doing—and I'm not just blowing smoke, my friends—he took concrete action to try to fight back against fraud. He doubled the number of fraud investigations when he was the Governor of this state. That's an amazing thing. He required personal photos on EBT cards so that we could make sure the people getting those benefits are the ones who deserve those benefits. And he cracked down on the international fraud rings that were taking advantage of this great state. You have to ask yourself, why is it that you have a government that seems more focused on the illegal aliens growing weed illegally than it is on the taxpayers of this state? Why is it that you have a government that seems not to care about you but sure as hell cares about Somali fraudsters who are making millions and millions of dollars off of your taxpayer money? And the answer is because you have a government that hasn't fought for you. Let's send a Governor who did fight for you to the U.S. Congress; let's send Paul LePage to the U.S. House. Now here's another thing Paul said, and this is why I blame Joe Biden because you cannot talk about the fraud problem in the United States of America without acknowledging that Joe Biden's administration let in tens of millions of illegal aliens all over the country. This includes the state of Maine. And unfortunately, you still got way too many of them. It's one of the reasons why we have devoted ourselves over the past 18 months and we're gonna keep on fighting to get illegal aliens out of the United States of America. We gotta fight for our own people. So here's the difference though, the difference one administration, the difference one political leader makes: we are now trying so hard—and I know y'all don't like Janet Mills, I don't especially care for her either. But I would love to work with the Governor of Maine to stop the fraud that's being perpetrated in this state. This should not be a red state or a blue state issue; this isn't Republican or Democrat; this is common sense. This is protecting every single person in this room from being defrauded by their own government. But you know what she's done? She has prevented local police from working with us to get criminal illegal aliens out of this state. She has actually fought back against our efforts to identify fraud in the Medicaid and Medicare programs, which by the way, not only does it steal from you, but it means that those programs are going to go bankrupt because all the money is going to the fraudsters. So let me ask you this simple question. Do you like having a government official who fights for illegal aliens over you? No! Do you like having a state Governor who doesn't work with the federal government to fight against fraud? No! So let's kick Janet Mills to the curb, and let's send Paul LePage to Washington to help us fight the fraudsters and protect all of you. Send them to Gitmo? No comment, sir, but I kind of like sending the fraudsters to Gitmo. We gotta get 'em out of this country, and we gotta get 'em in prison. Here's the thing. Let me talk a little bit about just one person cause I think it highlights how bad the fraud problem has been in the state of Maine. We had an illegal immigrant in this community who said that she was providing services, interpretive services to other immigrants. Okay? So Medicaid reimburses people to provide interpretation services for people who don't speak English. Which by the way, people should be speaking English in the United States of America, I think that's common sense. But this woman, her name was Rakia Mohammed. And it turns out that Rakia Mohammed was not a particularly upstanding citizen, okay? So while she said that she was providing interpretation services, do you know what she was actually doing? Providing zero services and collecting 15 million dollars over a five-year period that was going directly into her pocket. And that happened pretty much during the entirety of the Biden administration, and no one in the Biden administration thought to step in and stop this ridiculous fraud. Now here, I have two questions when it comes to Rakia Mohammed stealing 15 million dollars from every single person in this room. Question number one is, why was this person in the United States of America to begin with? Call me crazy, but I think that when we welcome new immigrants to the United States of America, they should be people who enrich our country and bring some good skills into our country, not a person who's going to steal 15 million dollars from the people in this room. But second of all, why on earth are we allowing a person to steal 15 million dollars from all of you without the state government lifting a finger to stop it? Why did it take Washington coming in from the outside to ensure that this person who was defrauding you is going to go to prison? And ladies and gentlemen, that's exactly what's gonna happen—convicted guilty and she's going to jail, which is where she belongs. Now with all these things though there's a good news, bad news situation. So the good news is that she's going to prison and she's not going to be committing fraud. But the bad news is the same system that allowed these people to take advantage of the taxpayers of Maine has not been changed at all by the local government. I'm telling you, and I'll say this to the cameras, I'll say this to the Governor and anybody else: if you want a partner in fighting fraud, you've got one. This does not need to be Republican versus Democrat; this needs to be fraudster against non-fraudster. This needs to be good government versus bad government. So I will extend the hand to anybody, whether they've got a D next to their name or an R next to their name. If you want to help the Trump administration fight fraud, you are going to find a willing partner. And by the way, as much as I'm a Republican—you all know this, I'm the Republican Vice President of the United States—we have found some blue states that are willing to work with us. Unfortunately, Maine is not among them. So whenever you replace Janet Mills with somebody, I hope you replace her with somebody who's going to help us fight fraud, protect you, and protect the programs that the people of Maine rely on. And the bad news for Rakia Mohammed is that she's going to prison, hopefully for a very long time. Now the good news for her though is I think she might have a future in Democratic Party politics in the state of Maine. Unfortunately, the Maine Democrats, elected Democrats, seem to really like fraudsters; they might just elect this woman the next Governor or the next Senator from Maine. Who knows? But not if you have anything to do with it, am I right? Are we gonna elect a fraudster to the Governor of Maine? No! Are we gonna elect a fraudster, a pro-fraud Democrat to be the next Senator for Maine? No! Absolutely not. We're gonna send representatives, senators, and governors who fight for the people in this room. We're gonna do it by rejecting fraudsters, sending them to prison where we can, and taking away the money in all cases. That's how we fight fraud successfully. And I want to just say one final thing about fraud because I think it's such an important topic. You heard the great Secretary of Labor say this. What is so different about our approach in this administration is that we're not just sending money out the door and then trying to find the fraudsters afterwards. We're actually making the money conditional on state governments fighting fraud to begin with. Now why does this matter? Because that's where you've got the most power and that's where you've got the ability to stop it from going to fraudsters to begin with. So here's what we did yesterday. The Trump administration, we sent out 50 letters to every state Medicaid system all over the country and we basically said, very simply, that we're gonna take away your money unless you get serious about fighting fraud. Common sense. And what that means is that we're gonna be able to ensure that there are proper controls, that there are proper filters, that before somebody pays out benefits they ensure that your money is going to where it should go. And I gotta say, as a person who has in the past benefited from some of these programs, this is a very personal issue to me. One of the things I love about our country, and I certainly think it's true about the great state of Maine, is that we're generous people. We don't want American citizens to go hungry; we don't want low-income kids to not be able to afford a bite to eat. We want to make sure that if you're a poor child or poor family, you get an opportunity to see a doctor even if money is particularly tight. That's why these programs exist. They exist for American citizens to look after our fellow citizens, and I think that's a great and generous thing about the United States of America. Again, my family has benefited from some of those programs in the past. But you know what destroys those programs, and not just destroys those programs, but destroys the spirit of generosity that makes those programs possible? It's when local officials and state officials and federal officials, it's when they let the fraudsters take advantage of you instead of fighting for you. So when I say that I'm fighting against fraud, I'm not just fighting to save your money, as much as that matters to me. I am fighting to preserve the sense that Americans look after Americans. But you know who we don't look after? We don't look after fraudsters and we don't look after people who have no right to be in the United States of America to begin with. So let me close with this, then I'll take a few questions from the reporters. What we've done here in the last 18 months, I'm incredibly proud of. We have managed to take the fight to the fraudsters, we've managed to save hundreds of billions of dollars of your tax dollars, and God willing we're to save hundreds of billions of more in the next two and a half years. But we cannot do it without you. And one of the coolest things about this effort, maybe my single favorite thing about this anti-fraud effort, is that many of the big leads that we've had have come from citizens and journalists. They have come from people like Nick Shirley, who was a 22-year-old kid. A 22-year-old kid who had the audacity to go to the people who were receiving your tax dollars and ask questions. Something frankly, it would have been great if the national media showed a little bit more interest. But I just want to say to all of you, for caring about this issue, for being engaged in this issue, for sending tips into our fraud task force, the website's right there, and actually making it possible for us to go and investigate this stuff. So keep your eyes and your ears to the ground. There is unfortunately fraud all around us. If we're gonna get at this problem, we're gonna need citizens to take it seriously. I know you are; let's just keep at it. Thank you. Thank you all for having me. We'll take a few questions from back in the peanut gallery there. Go ahead, guys. Can we get a microphone for them? It's hard for me to hear all the way back there. Can you hear me now? I just got invited to go cross-country skiing and snowshoeing by this lovely young lady here, and I gotta tell you, ma'am, I appreciate that. I love this state, and we will make sure we get the brochure. But here is the thing about cross-country skiing: it's a lot of work. When I'm doing leisure activity, I like to be a little bit more lazier than that. I kind of like a nice hill where you just glide down. Cross-country's a lot of work. Vice President Vance, Rich McHugh from News Nation. We have been reporting here in Maine since last year on fraud. We've been reporting in Minnesota on fraud. We're seeing prosecutions happen in Minnesota and in California, but some of the things that we're hearing from people and whistleblowers is you have to go to the org structure, the leadership, and that's not happening. How will this administration go after not just the people who are perpetrating it, but the people who are enabling it and overseeing it in the first place? It's a good question. One of the reasons why I raised this question of certain amounts of fraud being so low-level that people weren't even looking at it, is it's impossible to get to the high-level fraudsters unless you're sometimes willing to look to the low-level fraudsters too. I mean, how many of us have watched a good mob movie? And how do those prosecutions always start? You never start with the boss. You start with the low-level guys who then give you some sense of the criminal enterprise, and then you can go after the high-level fraud and the high-level crime. The fact that we were turning a blind eye to fraud under a million dollars meant that we weren't even looking at the people who were sitting on top of some of these fraud rings. And we do see already signs in Minnesota, for example, of some very sophisticated stuff. I mean, sometimes I see these fraud rings and I think to myself, "You know, sir, if you just started like a real business, you might actually be a halfway productive citizen." Instead, you put those talents into fraud, so we're gonna put all our talents into throwing you in prison if that's how you're going to approach that. That is the most important thing: you've got to be willing to go everywhere and look at all fraud, or you're never going to be able to look at the high-level guys. Very often what will happen is you've got 10 people who are skimming a million dollars off the top. Then they kick up a few hundred thousand dollars to somebody above them. It turns out that when you look at the whole network, you've got a lot of people who are making millions and millions and millions of dollars for every low-level guy that's making 500,000 dollars or something like that. So our approach has been to go everywhere to look at everything, and that's how we're going to get the fraudsters. Trust me, we are going to get them. Britney DeCaton from WABI in Bangor, wondering what the Mills administration and the state government could do today to begin working with you on this issue. Yeah, so I'll give you two very concrete examples. The question was what could the local administration, the state administration do to help work with us on this issue. First of all, one of the biggest problems that we have in Medicaid fraud is—again because these programs are state-administered—the states have to actually empower their anti-fraud people to look into what's going on. We have what's called—every 50 states, or all 50 states should have what's called an MFCU, a Medicare Fraud Control Unit. And we need the local government to just empower that Medicare Fraud Control Unit to look at the fraud, to prosecute it, and then of course they can ask the federal government if they reach a case that's so big they need a little help. We would be happy to help. We just need the state government to work with us a little bit better. Exactly. And unfortunately I just don't get the sense, having talked to her and my people have talked to her, I don't get the sense that the state government here is particularly interested in working on this problem. But I'll give you another example. We know one of the biggest drivers of fraudulent federal benefits is SNAP, it's the food stamps program. Again, this is a program which actually has even less federal oversight, as shocking as that is, even less federal oversight than the state Medicaid program. Every month or every couple of weeks the state of Maine, the state of California, all 50 states will submit a bill to the federal government and say this is how much we spent on food stamps. There's no verification of a single person; there's no confirmation that let's say a person was on food stamps and passed away, that that person has stopped receiving benefits. There's no confirmation that you don't have illegal aliens receiving food stamp benefits. So I always laugh a little bit when I hear people like Gavin Newsom or Janet Mills say, "We don't let illegal aliens access the food stamp program." And I say, how do you know? The number one thing that I would like them to do is to help us set up the systems to where if you're getting food stamps, we confirm that number one, you're eligible and you actually need the program. Number two, you're not an illegal alien. And number three, you're still alive. It is not a high barrier to demand that Janet Mills confirms that the people who are receiving food stamps are actually breathing. I'm a pretty conservative guy, but I think that's a pretty low bar for Janet Mills to clear. We would love to work with her on that; we encourage her to do so. Richard, ABC 7 and Fox 22 news here in Bangor. In your remarks as you mentioned, the administration has warned that states could lose federal funding if they don't aggressively prosecute Medicaid fraud. If funding is cut, how will the federal government ensure that low-income Mainers, particularly those in rural areas, are still receiving necessary medical care? Well, let me say two things. First of all, there is no administration in American history that has done more to make secure and safe and dependable the rural healthcare system than the Trump administration in the working families tax cuts bill. Now you all think of that primarily as a tax piece of legislation because it was. But you know that there were tens of billions of dollars put into rural healthcare stabilization funds so that all the rural hospitals and clinics that were closing under the Biden administration—we could make sure those clinics are able to stay open for the people in Maine who need them. That was a very, very important part of the legislation. The second thing I'd say is, whether it's Medicaid or any other program, we don't want the people, and we're setting this up so that the people who need these programs and are eligible for them, they're not going to suffer the consequences. We want the fraudsters and we want the bureaucrats who enable the fraud; we want those people to suffer the consequences. The threat to the Medicaid program—I always think some of these political, you know, partisan types get this exactly backwards—the threat to the Medicaid program is not us keeping fraudsters away from the Medicaid program; it's the fraudsters raiding that program to begin with. That's the threat to the Medicaid program. And so what I'd say to anybody who's worried about the Medicaid program: help us fight back against the fraud because we want low-income people in this state to be able to get the healthcare benefits they need. But that's not going to happen if the fraudsters steal all the money that should go to the people who actually need it. Seamus Oathat with the Maine Wire. In the recent budget, Janet Mills and the Democrats enshrined essentially protections for fraudsters; if they get their federal funding cut, the state will pay for them. How do you think that that can be fought on the federal level without just taking those currently diffuse costs and putting them even more directly onto the Maine taxpayers? Well, I had not heard of that, but unfortunately it doesn't shock me. I think that this is where when we talk about what can you do, what is my advice to you in order to stop the fraud? Advice number one is throw the people that enable the fraud the hell out of office. They're not worthy of your support and it's going to keep happening unless we kick 'em out of office. But the answer is we have a number of tools at the federal government to force compliance with our anti-fraud statutes and anti-fraud provisions. We're working very hard, again, to work with these states, but if the states aren't going to work with us, we are going to force them to comply with the anti-fraud mandate. It's very simple. We're going to use those tools at every step of the way. I don't frankly care whether the far-left politicians in this or that state try to protect the fraudsters; we're not going to protect 'em under the Trump administration and that is my solemn promise to every person in this state. Jackie Mundry from WMTW in Portland. It sounds like it's been a challenge to work with the Mills administration on this issue. We have a gubernatorial election coming up in November with primaries in just a couple weeks. Do you think it would be easier to work with a Republican governor? If so, why, and do you plan to endorse any of them? I know a number of them are here today. That's an understatement. Well, first of all, congratulations to those guys. I mean, I've run for political office; I always admire anybody who's willing to put their hat in the ring and try to represent the great people of this state or any other. It's an amazing thing. Thank you all. What I would say is obviously I'm a Republican, so I think it's going to be easier to work with some of these Republican candidates. But the simple fact is, it couldn't be harder than it is with Janet Mills. Again, there are a few states—California, New York, Maine, Minnesota—there are probably five or ten states that are just impossible to work with because it's clear they don't want to go after the fraud. Now I don't know why that is. Part of that could be they're complicit in it themselves. Part of that could be they know that a lot of their donors have gotten rich off the fraud. Part of that could just be laziness. And part of that could be because they honestly in their hearts care more about illegal aliens than the people they represent. There's a combination of explanations, but I guarantee you, whoever's the next Governor of Maine, it cannot be worse when it comes to anti-fraud than Janet Mills. We'll do one more question then we'll hit the road. Thank you, Mr. Vice President, for taking questions. I'm Phil Hirschkorn from News Center Maine. So it turns out that Maine does have a Medicare fraud control unit. You mentioned that a few minutes ago. I'm wondering—you said Maine could be the third worst state after states like California and Minnesota, which have proven tens of millions, maybe hundreds of millions of dollars in fraud and many federal convictions. So far what we've heard about from the federal government in Maine is questionable 45 million dollars in the autism services which you mentioned. We've also heard about 1.7 million dollars in questionable billing by a Somali immigrant-run NGO. My question is, what else do you got? What else has your task force flagged that we should be concerned about? Because those amounts are a lot, 46 million, 1.7 million, but they don't really compare to California and Minnesota. What else should we expect from your task force? Well, let me say a few things. Ladies and gentlemen, we've got biased reporters in all states, it's okay, trust me. I can handle it. But here's what I'd say. Number one, you talk about the scale of the fraud being less in Maine than it is in California. Well, California has a population that's much higher than Maine, so that's not necessarily a fair comparison. Number two, we frankly have been at this less long in Maine than in other states. This is something where what we—I would say, you notice a pattern. And you're right, innocent until proven guilty, but what I've noticed is a pattern. When you see certain types of fraud in certain types of programs, that's the tip of the iceberg. And when I see that autism services fraud in the Medicaid program in the Somali community, but not just that—I see local and state politicians who don't care about policing that fraud—that's the tip of the iceberg. And we're going to find out just how deep that iceberg is. I suspect—I can't promise, but I suspect we are going to find hundreds of millions of more dollars every single month that we look in the state of Maine because this is not a state that takes it seriously. This is the third point, and the most important one: I do not actually like having a contentious relationship between the state government and the federal government. We want people to help us find the fraudsters. It goes a lot quicker, it's a lot easier, it requires fewer resources from the federal government, but you find more fraud when you're working with people at the local level. What I find so preposterous about Maine is not just that we found tens of millions of dollars of fraud in a relatively small state; it's that we've had no cooperation from the state government. So when I say that the state government—when I say this is the third, maybe the fourth worst state when it comes to fraud—what I mean very simply is that you've got a Governor and you've got a set of bureaucrats that don't care about fraud. And all I'm saying is Donald J. Trump and JD Vance, we care about fraud. We care about the people of this state, and we care about your tax dollars going to where it's actually supposed to go. Are we—do guys, do we have somebody over here who's got an issue? Okay, let's get a doctor in here. Sounds like we got somebody who maybe fainted or... Got them? They got them? Okay. All right. So let's, if we would, ladies and gentlemen, if you would just make a pathway so the law enforcement and the doctors can get to that person. We okay? We're good? She's got medical attention. She's got care. Okay, sounds good. Well, look, I just want to be respectful, so I'm not going to answer any more questions here. I'm going to let this person get the treatment and the care that she needs. Let me just say two quick words. Number one, let's all say a prayer for our friend here and make sure that God looks after her health. And number two what I'd say is, it is the coolest job that I've ever had to be the Vice President of this great country. It's the coolest thing to be able to come here in the beautiful state of Maine in the beautiful month of May and to be able to spend some time with you. Let me just say that this job has given me more confidence than ever that this country's best days are ahead of it. As much as I'm sometimes frustrated by Washington, D.C., and as much as I'm sometimes frustrated by the politicians who seem to care more about illegal aliens than they do the citizens of this country... What I have learned over the last 18 months is that we have the most talented, the most compassionate, and the toughest people anywhere in the world. Ladies and gentlemen, America is the best country and it's going to be the best country for many years to come. God bless you all. Thank you for having me. It's good to see you all. Thank you.

President Trump & Chinese President Xi Meeting

C-SPAN(1.9M) ·31:10 ·104.4K views · 2026-05-14
Donald Trump (President of the United States) Xi Jinping (President of China)
President Trump’s state visit to Beijing focused on recalibrating the U.S.-China trade relationship and securing a commitment from President Xi to increase pressure on North Korea's nuclear program.
TL;DR President Trump’s state visit to Beijing focused on recalibrating the U.S.-China trade relationship and securing a commitment from President Xi to increase pressure on North Korea's nuclear program. Addressing the Trade Deficit: President Trump emphasized the need to...

TL;DR

President Trump’s state visit to Beijing focused on recalibrating the U.S.-China trade relationship and securing a commitment from President Xi to increase pressure on North Korea's nuclear program.

Trade and Economic Imbalance

  • Addressing the Trade Deficit: President Trump emphasized the need to correct the "one-sided and unfair" trade relationship between the two nations.
      • Market Access: The U.S. delegation pushed for greater access to Chinese markets and the protection of intellectual property.
      • Commercial Deals: The summit was punctuated by the announcement of over $250 billion in commercial deals and investment memorandums, though many were non-binding.
  • Mutual Cooperation: President Xi emphasized that "win-win cooperation" is the only viable path for the two largest economies, suggesting that the Pacific Ocean is large enough to accommodate both powers.

Denuclearization of the Korean Peninsula

  • Increased Pressure on Pyongyang: A primary objective for the U.S. was to secure a firm commitment from China to fully implement UN Security Council resolutions against North Korea.
      • China’s Role: Trump urged President Xi to "work very hard" and use China’s economic leverage to persuade the Kim Jong Un regime to abandon its nuclear ambitions.
  • Shared Strategic Goals: Both leaders reaffirmed their commitment to a nuclear-free Korean Peninsula, though they differed slightly on the tactical approach to sanctions and dialogue.

Diplomatic "State Visit-Plus"

  • Unprecedented Pageantry: The Chinese government characterized the event as a "State Visit-Plus," involving an elaborate welcoming ceremony at the Great Hall of the People and a rare private tour of the Forbidden City.
  • Leader Chemistry: Throughout the public portions of the meeting, both leaders highlighted their personal "chemistry" and the importance of a strong personal rapport in managing the complex bilateral relationship.

Regional Stability and Human Rights

  • Security Concerns: Discussions touched upon maritime security in the South China Sea, with the U.S. advocating for a "free and open Indo-Pacific."
  • Rule of Law: While trade and security dominated the agenda, the U.S. side maintained its stance on the importance of human rights and the rule of law within the framework of international norms.
"I don't blame China. After all, who can blame a country that is able to take advantage of another country for the benefit of its citizens? I give China great credit." — President Donald Trump
"For China and the United States, cooperation is the only right choice, and a better future would only be achieved through win-win cooperation." — President Xi Jinping

Innovation, Investment, and Partnerships: Eric Schmidt at Milken 2026

Eric Schmidt(35.5K) ·43:00 ·750 views · 2026-05-14
Eric Schmidt (Former CEO, Google) Chris Coons (U.S. Senator) Adam Smith (U.S. Congressman)
The United States must pivot from antiquated, high-cost military platforms to autonomous, AI-driven robotic systems to maintain global deterrence while addressing a $40 trillion national debt that now costs more in interest than the entire defense budget.
TL;DR The United States must pivot from antiquated, high-cost military platforms to autonomous, AI-driven robotic systems to maintain global deterrence while addressing a $40 trillion national debt that now costs more in interest than the entire defense budget. The...

TL;DR

The United States must pivot from antiquated, high-cost military platforms to autonomous, AI-driven robotic systems to maintain global deterrence while addressing a $40 trillion national debt that now costs more in interest than the entire defense budget.

The Paradigm Shift in Modern Warfare

  • The "Robot-First" Future: Eric Schmidt argued that the U.S. should cease investing in manned fighters and ships, moving instead toward land, sea, and air drones. He noted that in the Ukraine conflict, precision weaponry has become so lethal that there are "essentially no woundings"—combatants are either dead or alive.
  • Lessons from Ukraine: Senator Chris Coons highlighted that Ukraine is currently conducting successful drone-only assaults and has pushed back the Black Sea fleet using naval drones, demonstrating a fundamental transformation in conflict.
  • Cost-Effectiveness as Strategy: Schmidt’s company produces Shahed drone interceptors for ~$10,000, while the enemy’s drones cost ~$30,000. He emphasized that new defense tech offers "100-to-1 or 1,000-to-1" cost-efficiency differences compared to traditional systems.
  • Algorithmic Deterrence: Future deterrence will rely on "agentic" AI—autonomous systems with an "AI brain" that can plan and operate within the laws of war. Schmidt warned that the U.S. must win the race to develop these before China.

Fiscal Reality and Defense Reform

  • The Scarcity Mindset: Congressman Adam Smith stressed that with a $40 trillion national debt, the U.S. can no longer solve defense problems by simply "throwing more money" at the Pentagon. He called for a shift from a "Department of War" mentality to a "Department of Defense" focused on cost-effective deterrence.
  • Legislative Progress: The SPEED Act has begun to reform the acquisition process, incentivizing faster innovation. Representative Smith noted that the Pentagon is starting to open up to venture-backed defense tech firms (e.g., Anduril, Saronic, Palantir, SpaceX) to break the traditional prime contractor monopolies.
  • Flexibility in Funding: To foster innovation, the panel called for more "flexibility in money," specifically addressing the "color of money" problem where software is often incorrectly categorized as maintenance rather than a leading capability.

The Agentic AI Revolution

  • Beyond Chatbots: Schmidt described a shift toward AI Agents—systems with memory and learning loops that can autonomously plan travel, manage corporate databases, and conduct predictive analytics.
  • Model Context Protocol (MCP): This new technology allows users to manipulate entire company databases using plain English, enabling a level of scenario planning and predictive analytics that was previously impossible.
  • Recursive Self-Improvement: Schmidt noted that we are "halfway through the beginning" of systems that can develop their own ideas and learn on their own, a shift that will fundamentally change both business and military planning.

Strategic Partnerships and Workforce Talent

  • International Co-production: Senator Coons advocated for reducing barriers (like ITAR) to manufacture munitions and systems at scale with allies like Australia, Germany, Japan, and Finland, specifically through frameworks like AUKUS.
  • Workforce Pivot: Congressman Smith highlighted the "Software Factory" in the Army as a model for recruiting technical talent. He argued for a shift from traditional four-year degrees to specific, problem-solving technical skills.
  • The Lack of Urgency: Senator Coons expressed deep concern regarding the lack of urgency in U.S. political and military systems compared to the "existential urgency" found in Ukraine, warning that the U.S. needs to move on a 21st-century timeline.
"We shouldn't be doing any of [the traditional history] in the future. We should be sending robots instead. We don't need more ships; we need more drone ships. We don't need more jet fighters; we need more non-human powered jet fighters." — Eric Schmidt
"I’m always fond of the Winston Churchill quote: 'Gentlemen, we're out of money. Now we have to think.' I think [scarcity] incentivizes you to go find solutions." — Congressman Adam Smith
"We are dealing with a 19th-century political decision-making system in the midst of a 21st-century acceleration of technology... We have to be agile and we have to have a sense of urgency if we're going to win this moment." — Senator Chris Coons
Please welcome the panel on Innovation, Investment and Partnerships, moderated by David Trulio, President and CEO, Ronald Reagan Presidential Foundation and Institute. Well, welcome. So much to discuss. We're going to dive right in. We have Senator Chris Coons on the far end of the panel. He serves, as many of you know so well, on the Senate Appropriations Committee, where he is ranking member of the Senate Appropriations Subcommittee on Defense. He also serves on the Senate Foreign Relations Committee and as a member of the Judiciary Committee's Subcommittee on Intellectual Property. The Senator is focused on fostering American innovation and encouraging investment in research and development. Congressman Adam Smith, to my immediate right, represents the ninth district of Washington State. He serves as the ranking member of the House Armed Services Committee. He's held the post of the highest-ranking Democrat on the committee since December 2010. In addition, he was chair of the House Armed Services Committee during the 116th and 117th Congresses. And then of course, Eric Schmidt, the prominent technologist, investor, and philanthropist, the former CEO and Chairman of Google. Among his various current endeavors, he is Chair and CEO of Relativity Space. I couldn't think of a better panel to dive right in. So let's start with Ukraine. Senator Coons, we're four-plus years into this conflict. What lessons do you see from Ukraine, and what is the call to action for the business leaders and investors in the room with us and within the sound of your voice? Thank you, David, and thank you to everyone for being here and for the chance to speak at this important forum. Look, the lessons, I think, of the war in Ukraine, Russia's war of aggression against Ukraine, are clear: the Ukrainians have developed and continue to innovate a fundamental transformation in conflict. This includes both using naval drones, air drones, and ground drones. They recently recaptured territory in Zaporizhzhia using a drone-only assault on a Russian-defended position. They have pushed the Black Sea fleet a long distance and have managed to defend their coastline successfully. With some significant partnership from Eric, they are delivering state-of-the-art both Shahed drone interceptors and offensive drones. The United States should be embracing them with open arms and investing in our partnership. We should be using it to help solve our current impasse in the Strait of Hormuz and dramatically reducing the cost of defending our homeland. We need it to build out what we need to project power and security for our allies and partners in the Western Pacific and in Europe. That we are not doing that, but instead gearing up to invest hundreds of billions more in antiquated systems and antiquated processes, is a huge missed opportunity. I look forward to a conversation about how we change direction in time to defend our innovation, our security, our alliances, and our civilization. Well, Eric, you've been to Ukraine. You're working on a book on the future of warfare. Why does Ukraine matter in terms of technology, innovation, global security, and what are the implications for the Indo-Pacific? Well, I start every day by believing that you actually have to fight for democracy. You have to actually fight for freedom. I wish that were not the answer, but I think there's plenty of proof. If we have to fight, I want America to win. I personally believe, and the Senator said it so brilliantly, we're at a moment when all the technology assumptions shift. The simplest way I can explain it to you all is all those movies, all that great history, all those wonderful courageous things—we shouldn't be doing any of that in the future. We should be sending robots instead. So, we don't need more ships; we need more drone ships. We don't need more jet fighters; we need more non-human powered jet fighters, and frankly, missiles. We don't need more tanks; we need more very powerful drones. Now, I sit and go like, how could that be possible? Well, remember that the tank is a hundred years old. There was a time before tanks were invented, and it was of course a fantastic weapon in World War I. We all know the story of nuclear in World War II. Technology can change war and change—change quite quickly. So, the simple answer is that the future of war, which hopefully we will not be having, is powerful robotic solutions. Call them drones, call them what you want, land, sea, and air, that are controlled by powerful networks using artificial intelligence that are highly precise. I can give you plenty of facts to support my argument, but I'll give you a simple one. There are essentially no woundings in the Ukrainian war. The weapons are so powerful and the drones are so accurate that you're dead or alive, and I say that with sadness. Ranking Member Smith, your reaction. Well, I think I want to emphasize two points that were made there. And number one that doesn't get talked about enough is what Eric said about fighting for democracy. Yes, there's a huge technological aspect to Ukraine that I think both Senator Coons and Eric pointed out well, but also this is fundamentally who we're supposed to be about here in the US. Ukraine is very clearly fighting for economic and political freedom and for the concept of sovereignty. We should be all in on that. That is our global battle right now. Russia, China, Iran, North Korea, various terrorist groups—they want to smash the economic and political freedom world that was built, more or less, after World War II. We have a great example in Ukraine as to where we should be fighting for it, and we have not made that clear. I mean, really, literally rolling out the red carpet for Vladimir Putin and dressing down President Zelensky in the Oval Office is the exact opposite of where the United States of America should be. And second, to the point of technology, we have a $40 trillion debt. We don't have a ton of money. We desperately need cost-effective solutions to meet our needs in a thousand different areas, but certainly in defense. And we have found that through the technology that Eric just described vastly better than I can. That's where we need to make the investments. Don't come in here asking for $1.6 trillion in a ridiculous defense budget given our fiscal climate. Let's use our knowledge to meet our deterrence needs in an incredibly cost-effective way. We have the technology, we just need to embrace it. So, one fact is that the amount we spend on the national debt is now, in interest on the debt, greater than the defense budget. So, Eric, what is your perspective on the ability to bring costs down through technology and particularly through artificial intelligence and drones? What, as a business leader, are your thoughts on that? Well, it looks to me like the military's not run the way you would run a business. I guess that's not a surprise to anyone in the room. Wouldn't you use the most effective solution, measured by cost, availability, and accuracy, to do the job? Unfortunately, in the case of defense, it's about destruction. It's about destruction of infrastructure, destruction of people and so forth. That's called the kill ratio, unfortunately. And the new defense tech, of which my company is just one of many, have a hundred-to-one, thousand-to-one kind of differences. And that's measured in real combat: Anduril, Saronic, Mine, a couple of others. We are known about—I'll give you an example. My company has the number one Shahed interceptor in the world. We have the highest accuracy and so forth. We charge, you know, roughly $10,000 for it, plus or minus. That's the list price, discounts are available, and the enemy has Shaheds which are about $30,000. So we're already cheaper. At some point at the Pentagon, one of the generals said, "Who knew that America had a company that had the leading one?" I said, "Well, I did, and I told you." So I think that there's some kind of a delay in understanding in the government. It may just be that there's so many people, right? So you find one person, you get them convinced, but there's so many leaders of one kind or another. It takes top-down leadership to say, "We want to save money, we want to be more effective, we want to make America more secure." If I could just add on that, I think this is a problem throughout government. You know, when I'm back in DC, I'm fighting this battle with the Pentagon. Whenever they have a problem, they want more money. Back home in the Seattle-King County, Washington State area, we're trying to figure out how to get affordable housing and how to deal with homelessness. It's always like, give us more money, give us more money, give us more money. And I'm always like, okay, what are you doing with the money that you have? And it's almost like I'm speaking a foreign language. They're like, no, you don't understand, we have a problem, we need more money. What's to talk about? That mentality in the modern era, when we have so much global competition and a massive debt, is killing us. Let's start thinking about how can we get more out of what we're spending. And again, the beauty of it is, the technology that we have available to us from AI to a bunch of other things really gives us the opportunity to do that. We got to change the mentality at the Pentagon. And I will say, Deputy Secretary Fineberg—I took one shot at the Trump administration, I'll say one positive thing—Deputy Secretary Fineberg gets this and he wants to get us to that place. A little higher up, we want to build a $20 billion battleship for some stupid reason, so we got to fight that off to really focus on the people who get this. It's a fundamental culture change, and it's not just Republicans, it's Democrats, it's everybody. It's always like, how can we figure out how to get the most money for us, and don't talk about anything else. And if we don't change that mentality fiscally, we're going to be buried. So, from an oversight and a legislative perspective, what are you and Senator Coons doing to change the incentive structure and to change the behavior that's led us to this place? Well, I'll just quickly and then turn to Senator. We did acquisition reform last year, the SPEED Act, in a very bipartisan way. Mike Rogers, chairman of the committee, was as important on this as anybody. Terrific job on that. We worked in a bipartisan way with Senator Wicker and Senator Reed to speed up the process and really incentivize innovating faster at scale. So we set that up, and then we got to work on the culture at the Pentagon, but I'll... Hey, can I just add, the SPEED Act was one of the best things you've ever done in your long career. It really did enable this whole industry to get started. Senator? We had a meeting late in the appropriations process last year with Deputy Secretary Fineberg. He came in with a huge presentation and asked for 26 billion in multi-year procurement authority for more than a dozen of the most critical munitions. It was so late in the process that both Senator McConnell, the chairman, and I said, "We can't possibly give you that much money this late. We'll give you four billion, but we'll give you multi-year procurement authority for this whole range of munitions." And then we want to meet with you and hear from you regularly about what you're doing to really push the primes, to really try and crowd in private sector investment in partnership in facilitation, and then to work with you to try and reduce some of the barriers. That's been a positive. Two things that I've been pushing: first is partnering with our allies in co-production. We are not taking anywhere near enough advantage by reducing some of the barriers, like ITARs and MTCRs, and using partnerships like AUKUS to actually manufacture at scale some of the critical both munitions and systems where we're not there yet. Whether it's with Australia or Germany, with Finland or with Japan, we can and should strengthen our defense industrial base by genuinely partnering with others. I'm going to be pressing for the Pentagon to scale and advance the acquisition of cutting-edge systems, to reduce total cost, to expand our partnerships with our trusted allies, and to further accelerate multi-year procurement. Last, the use of other transactions authorities is a key piece of getting outside the traditional procurement process that is sclerotic and wasteful. Are there bright spots that you're seeing in some of the commands or in different parts of the Pentagon? So, look, I'm concerned that both the combatant commands, the services, and the primes are resisting the concept that Eric's talking about and that I endorse and that Adam's done more than I have to advance, which is to rapidly move towards a fundamentally different style of warfare. But in combatant commands like AFRICOM, SOUTHCOM, and SOCOM, they have missions and responsibilities that spread over such vast geographies. They have such modest resources that they are most likely to adopt drone technologies for surveillance, for engagement, and for remote action. So I'm going to be pressing and pushing on them and the services they're principally responsible for supporting their work, and I hope we'll get some uptake this year. Eric? I'll give you an example, and you're seeing the change now under a lot of leadership from these guys. In the Pentagon, there's now a movement to do the equivalent of a hackathon to get all of the systems to be able to connect with each other. Now you sit there and you go, "What an interesting idea, right?" Get the missiles and the radars and so forth actually talking to each other with computer systems? Wow. The problem is the programmers are dead, right? These weapons are 40 and 50 years old. So it took pounding by the Pentagon on the primes to do this, but they're doing it. So leadership can make this happen. What I would say is the other thing is people are always concerned that somehow this will affect jobs in their districts or their states or what have you. Unfortunately, and again I say with some sadness, there's going to be more people needed in the world I'm describing—more bombs, more weapons, but they're different. And we know this from the scale of destruction in the Ukraine war. So we're not going to be making fewer bombs; we'll be just making different ones to stay safe. We're not going to be—we're going to have a lot more ships, but they're going to be drone ships. We'll have a lot more airplanes, but they're going to be unmanned airplanes. So it's not that there's less jobs, it's just that they're building something different, right? If a single person were in charge, what they would say is: do a replan where you take all of the current money, which is only $900 billion plus or minus—I think it's maybe a trillion now—and reallocate it in this direction over a five-year program. And by the way, more jobs and more investment as a result, not less. Yeah, let me give you two positive examples, one on a small scale, one on a larger scale. I was down in Austin, Texas a month or so ago and visited Saronic, by the way, which is making a huge difference on the shipbuilding side. I also visited a thing called the Software Factory that the Army set up under General George. That was about how can we get to the point where DOD can buy software better, because of the many things that they're bad at, they were terrible at that, and it was causing all manner of problems. So they went out and recruited army-wide. Anyone who sort of showed a talent in this area, they recruited them and they sent them down to Austin for a two-year gig to find software solutions to all manner of problems. Some were on the lethality side, some on just the human resources side. How do we know where our people are, and how do we know where the equipment is? That's the Pentagon learning. But the coolest thing that has happened really in just the last five years is the amount of defense tech investment capital that is out there. Certainly Anduril, Palantir, and SpaceX back in the day started it, but now you've got hundreds of small companies funded in some cases by venture funds. A lot of times it's some 19-year-old kid who comes up with an idea of how to build a better drone or build a better missile, and all of that money is going in. We're starting to open up to allow them to come in and compete. That's really cool in terms of what all these individuals are doing in this new money, but the other thing that's cool about it that doesn't get talked about often enough is the impact it's having on the big primes, who we all love to hate. The thinking was, well, the big primes don't know how to compete, they just know how to monopolize. And that's not actually true. We just set up a system that incentivized them to not compete, to monopolize. Now that they're being dragged into a world where they have to compete, there's a lot of smart people at Northrop, at Boeing, at Lockheed. So now we get the benefit of their ability to innovate because we've created a competitive environment, not a monopoly environment. That's helping inch us towards the world that Eric has done such a good job of describing. And Eric, from—yeah, please, throw— Can I—I think it's worth—I'm a technologist and I think about where do we end up? You tend to think of a drone as a person and a drone and another person and a drone in combat. That's not actually what's going to happen. What's really going to happen, think about a fighter jet, you know, the F-35, $200 million. It has offensive and defensive systems. It has defensive systems to make sure it isn't hit by a rocket and so forth, and it has offensive systems to do whatever it's going to do. Every drone will ultimately have the following structure: it will have an offensive part, it will have a defensive part, and it will have a human-ish brain. This will be the equivalent of an AI LLM, which within some limits, and those limits are defined by the laws of war, operate autonomously. We, America, need to build that because that is the future. And woe is us if China does it first. They have everything needed to achieve that objective. Well, much has been said about the agentic revolution. What do you see as the implications of that and where are we in competition here? So, for this audience, everybody here knows what ChatGPT is. Everybody went through the ChatGPT moment. That's language to language. Because of emergent behavior, these systems are actually very good, especially if you ask them to start planning. You can say, "Well, show me the steps, start thinking with me." And this has spawned a notion of agents, and an agent is like a travel agent. The older people here remember the human agent that you would call and they would know who you were and they would anticipate and so forth. Younger people don't know what I'm talking about. So the industry is now obsessed with agents. And an agent can be understood as: it has input, it has memory, it has a learning loop, and it has an output. Typical example, let's think about this conference. So in a couple of years, the first thing you'll do is all of you will opt into this because you're smart. You'll have a preference agent which will be looking ahead in your schedule. It will have access to your schedule and then it'll call a conference agent. The conference agent will study this Milken conference and it'll say, "Well, who's coming and what's important?" And then you'll have a relationship agent that will actually contact the agent of the other people who you actually want to meet on the sidelines. All of this without human interaction. It'll of course check with you for preferences, and then it'll produce follow-ups, summaries, and so forth. Now you sit there and you go, "Eh, no big deal, I have an assistant." Think about how much more efficient your life will be if all of that stuff is automated, by the way, including the hotel planning and the timing. Furthermore, imagine if it concludes one year there's nobody at Milken you need to see, so the only reason you need to go is because you want to learn something and then you can connect. That's unfathomable, but you see my point, right? The value to you of the agentic revolution in terms of empowering you is huge. Agents are going to define your version of AI—the AI that you see for a few years. There are all sorts of implications from agents. By the way, just one more thing about agents. There's a series of technologies that came out. One is called MCP, Model Context Protocol, which allows you to basically take all the databases in a company and manipulate them using English. Oh my god, right? We've been waiting for 50 years for this to occur. MCP shows up and now all of a sudden people can do predictive analytics in their businesses. Predictive analytics in the sense of: if I change this, do I get more revenue and so forth. And then the next step is what is called scenario planning. Technically it's called reinforcement learning, where you have a simulator and you look at forward scenarios. Everything I've just told you is true for businesses, but it's also true for other institutions like the Pentagon, right? So you're going to see this incredible transition internally in companies. The Pentagon as an example: you have thousands of people who are planners and they think, and I think that eventually these systems will think more deeply and more accurately than the humans that we depend on. You remember by the way all that stuff about hallucinations a few years ago? It's largely under control. They're still not perfect, but we're getting there. The technology is improving that fast. Well, a question for each of you, given—especially given the investors within the sound of our voice, what are the greatest gaps that cry out for capital and investment, where folks interested in the defense tech space need to go address? Senator, I'll start with you. Well first, if you just think about the implications of what Eric just said, it'll transform deterrence too. The whole point, frankly, of our military spending is deterrence. Deterrence requires the capability to fight and win and to communicate effectively to your opponents your determination to fight and win. As they have and we have better and better and better scenario planning, it'll go all the way through it. If at the bottom it concludes they can win, the odds that we will be at war are much higher. The odds of misunderstanding and miscommunication will become more critical, but those gaps will also become narrower. Where are there places for investment? Frankly, they are all over the place. The challenge is to having a predictable return on that investment, which are mostly bureaucratic and political. But the savings for the taxpayer and the advances in terms of our capability to project force are massive. I mean, they're orders of magnitude. So, folks who are willing and able to get engaged in advocating for the transformation of our procurement system and for the more effective engagement with our partners and allies have really significant potential upside. The challenge is that our system doesn't respond quickly to market signals and is not really a market system. But our allies are rapidly rearming and are not spending the right money on the right thing just as we aren't. Very few of them have the budget or fiscal headroom to make the mistakes that we're making and just muscle through it. So I would encourage partnering with some of our more capable allies and then back-selling to us through them. Yeah, I completely agree with that. And I think the investments need to be in the areas that Eric talked about. How do you build the autonomous systems using AI to best anticipate everything? It's not as simple as it sounds. As I said, there's hundreds, perhaps thousands of different ideas out there. They're not all winners, and they don't all meet what our technological needs are going to be. So the other thing we're going to need is we're going to need really smart people like Eric and others in a position to guide those decisions, so we don't buy just every new whiz-bang system. How does that system actually give us the deterrence we want? And then that's the second point building off of the Senator's point about deterrence. I think we need to start thinking about how we build the system in terms of deterrence. I've been very much opposed to calling it the Department of War, in large part because it's not the Department of War. It is the Department of Defense, because that's what the law says, and I'm old-fashioned enough to think that we ought to pay attention to the law. But certainly the way the Trump administration is doing things, Department of War is more apt. We have to get off of this whole "escalate to de-escalate" thing, because there's no end to that. You wind up in constant conflict. I want to use these technologies to have the strength to actually deter conflict, not to use it to engage in conflict under the misguided notion that that will somehow end conflict. So getting that deterrence right is really important. Final point is just one big area is on manufacturing capability of critical munitions. We are behind on that because the systems that we are making, we don't have the manufacturing centers with the scale. Even if we decided to spend Lord knows how much money, we don't have the manufacturing capability right now to do that. We need to build that up, and then I can't say it any better than Chris said: partners and allies. We're not going to build it up on our own; we're going to need partners. It is a global economy. We can't do it all on our own, which is part of the reason we shouldn't be so busily pushing away absolutely every ally, partner, and friend that we've ever had. We need friends, now more than ever. Eric, did you want to come in on that? I completely agree with what both said. I'm pretty confident now that the US companies are going to do well. I think the government has gotten the programs. Unmentioned up until now has been DIU, which has helped stimulate all of these. There's a bunch of such groups; they've been very helpful. It's interesting that in Europe, I know a number of investors who are now trying to do the same thing because the Europeans are rearming. There are underutilized assets where they can essentially do rollups and try to create the same thing. Everybody has the same problem: they're building the wrong stuff, they don't have the right materials, and there's critical materials involved. No matter how good the technology is, people will be needed going forward, perhaps repurposed. But what—Eric, maybe I'll start with you, where do you see the talent and ability gaps and what needs to change to move the people into the right areas that you identified earlier?
