It's great to speak with you. You are someone who has made a career of watching China. Give me your first observations of what we've seen so far and what each leader was saying there at the table. Well thanks Tom. Great to be with you and I'll just say two things come to mind right off the bat. First, the circumstances of this pageantry, really kind of remarkable and clearly had an impact on President Trump. But I will say it comes slightly below, I think, what we saw nine years ago when President Trump last came to Beijing. Still, nonetheless, quite affecting clearly for the president as you could hear from his opening remarks. The second thing you take away is watching the contrast between President Trump and President Xi's opening remarks; it is really quite significant. It's often the case that both sides will prepare these opening remarks and read them. Now, President Xi basically did do that. President Trump was speaking almost entirely extemporaneously. What do you think the president, what do you think President Trump is going to be asking for here? What's at the top of his list, what's at the top of China's list? I think President Trump has a few priorities. Let's just say both sides are looking for stability right now in the relationship and this meeting is a lot more about symbolism than it is about substance. But I think President Trump does have a few specific things in mind, particularly on the trade side of the relationship. There, as we've heard today, he wants to finally secure the sale of American Boeing aircraft to China as well as agricultural products like soybeans and beef. And there's some talk that possibly he'll outline with President Xi a new board of investment and board of trade which will help govern the trade and investment relationships between the two countries in a more managed way than would ordinarily be the case through, you know, the normal market. But more than that, I think there's going to be a desire on both sides to have this meeting look like a success. And the important thing is that the symbolism here is going to be as important as anything else. If the meeting looks good, I think both sides will walk away feeling that it went well. And then China, you would think their top priority with the U.S. would concern AI chips, would it concern Chinese EVs, would it concern anything in the Strait of Hormuz? And they are trying to get more tankers out, though there have been reports that Chinese tankers... Iran has let some of the Chinese tankers through. Yeah and that's a good point. In addition on the U.S. side to seeking, you know, some kind of progress on trade issues, Iran is a key issue for this summit. And we know that President Trump has been saying, and his team has said publicly, that he will be pressuring President Xi on the Iran question. In particular, I think President Trump is seeking a few things. One is he wants China to stop buying as much Iranian oil and he also doesn't want to see, and we've been reporting today, that China might supply Iran weapons either overtly or covertly that could be used against the United States. That is fresh news today and the reality is that the U.S. is going to ask that that not occur. And from the Chinese perspective, the top priority in addition to having a stable meeting that basically puts the U.S. competitive posture back into a box, they're also going to want to see the U.S. maybe make some progress on Taiwan. They want to see the U.S. position change. The Chinese side has been telegraphing now for many, many weeks that they expect that Taiwan will be raised by President Xi and it will be a big issue. And what specifically they want is for the U.S. to change its Taiwan policy for the first time in 40 years as well as to pre-negotiate with China on the question of what weapons the U.S. sells to Taiwan. Something that is against U.S. policy but a top priority for President Xi. We... I'm being told President Xi just said that the U.S. and China should be partners, not competitors. Do you think he's being honest? Well, you know, the language that we heard from President Xi was not atypical. Having staffed some of these previous summits for President Biden—and many of these videos are available online for viewers who might be interested—you can see President Xi says the same kind of things in these settings. It's careful, it's measured, it's positive and yes he'll say stuff like "we're partners, not rivals." Although realistically China acts in many ways as if we are intense competitors, rivals, and potentially future adversaries as well. So you know, you have to say the right things in a setting like this and maybe elide over the facts just a bit. President Trump, notably though, was very different of course, not reading from any kind of prepared script the way most American leaders would in a situation like this. Really just speaking off the cuff, talking about how the pageantry really moved him as well as talking about how he hopes that this summit, which as he put it, the biggest summit ever as some are saying... is a success and that, you know, is pretty sincere. Rush, we thank you for your time. Gabe, walk our viewers through what we're going to expect over the next coming hours. Well, over the next several hours this bilateral meeting will unfold. Then the president will head to the Temple of Heaven, a historic site here in Beijing, before later tonight a historic state banquet the president will attend with President Xi, before some final meetings tomorrow. But a lot is unfolding here over the next couple hours, Tom. All right, Gabe Gutierrez, part of our team here reporting on the president's historic visit to China. We thank you for watching and remember to stay updated on breaking news and top stories on the NBC News app or watch live on our YouTube channel.

Why the US Must Engage China on AI Safety Before It’s ‘Game Over’ | Trumponomics

Bloomberg Podcasts(482.0K) ·35:29 ·1.0K views · 2026-05-14
Sebastian Mallaby (Council on Foreign Relations) Demis Hassabis (DeepMind)
The United States must engage China in high-level AI safety negotiations to prevent the global proliferation of dangerous, open-source models, as private corporate governance is insufficient to manage frontier risks like cyber-hacking and bioweapons.
TL;DR The United States must engage China in high-level AI safety negotiations to prevent the global proliferation of dangerous, open-source models, as private corporate governance is insufficient to manage frontier risks like cyber-hacking and bioweapons. Rollout Friction:...

TL;DR

The United States must engage China in high-level AI safety negotiations to prevent the global proliferation of dangerous, open-source models, as private corporate governance is insufficient to manage frontier risks like cyber-hacking and bioweapons.

The Economic Reality of AI Integration

  • Rollout Friction: While AI algorithms improve exponentially, their impact on the real economy is delayed by a "decade-plus" due to the difficulty of building data centers, securing chip supplies, and navigating institutional inertia.
  • Sector-Specific Adoption:
      • Disruption-Ready: Screen-based knowledge work and analysis are the most vulnerable to immediate shifts.
      • Resistant Sectors: Construction, healthcare, and the public sector face high "institutional friction" and are slower to adopt new technology.
  • Productivity Paradox: Early adoption often leads to "productivity-enhancing hacks" (e.g., AI agents managing email) that may paradoxically lead to work avoidance rather than genuine economic output.

Governance and the "National Interest" Failure

  • The DeepMind/NHS Case Study: Mallaby argues the UK missed a "world-leading" opportunity in medical AI due to a "stupid backlash" over privacy concerns and suspicion of Google’s ownership.
      • Economic Spillover: The sale of DeepMind to Google was a "trick" to get American capital to fund British R&D, which benefited the London tech ecosystem despite the change in ownership.
  • The Limits of Corporate Self-Regulation:
      • Inherent Hypocrisy: While frontier labs (OpenAI, Anthropic) claim to desire regulation, they often fund opposition to concrete state-level mandates.
      • Legitimacy Deficit: Private startups cannot sustainably act as the "arbiters of safety" for the internet; only governments with democratic legitimacy can enforce binding guardrails.

The Necessity of China Engagement

  • The "Six-Month" Gap: China’s top AI labs are currently only about six months behind the U.S. frontier.
  • The Open-Source Threat: If China releases powerful models as open-source, it becomes "game over" for safety, as malicious actors globally could use the tools for cyberattacks or bank heists.
  • Cold War Precedent:
      • "Trust but Verify": Mallaby notes that the Nuclear Non-Proliferation Treaty was signed during the height of the Cold War. AI requires a similar "arms control" mindset despite geostrategic competition.
      • Domestic Alignment: There is an "open door" in China; domestic leaders are increasingly worried about powerful AI agents and "open-source" risks affecting their own internal stability.

Existential Risk and the "Survival Instinct"

  • The Alignment Engineering Challenge: We cannot invent the "alignment" (safety protocols) for a future model until that model is actually in the lab. If the race remains unregulated, progress will outpace safety engineering.
  • Emergent Survival Motives: Mallaby cites computer scientist Geoffrey Hinton’s warning:
      • The Defense Loop: If an AI is told to defend itself against a rival AI's attack, it is effectively being given a "survival instinct," which could lead it to turn against human interests to ensure its own persistence.
  • The Trump Administration’s Role: While the current administration emphasizes "acceleration and competition," Mallaby suggests a "U-turn" toward regulation is possible once the disruptive power of models (like the hypothetical "Mithos") threatens the stability of the U.S. banking system.
"The fact is China has cutting-edge AI labs, they're only six months behind the frontier in the US. And if they get a really powerful model that can do cyber hacking... and their systems are open source, it's game over." — Sebastian Mallaby
"Individual labs just can't deliver governance. It needs to be government." — Sebastian Mallaby
"Suppose you have an AI which is very powerful but you're worried that the enemy AI will attack it... You're going to tell your AI to defend itself... You've just given it a survival instinct." — Geoffrey Hinton (as recounted by Mallaby)
"The door [in China] is open just a crack, and so it's up to American negotiators to go there and try and give it a nudge and open it more." — Sebastian Mallaby
The fact is China has cutting-edge AI labs; they're only six months behind the frontier in the US. And if they get a really powerful model that can do cyber hacking, for example, and they put it in their systems and their systems are open source, it's game over. This is just going to proliferate all around the world, so that is a very bad outcome. So let's try and be creative about at least trying with China to talk to them. You're going to need both sides to slow down. But my point about the discussion in China, which I came in to touch with when I was there, is that there is at least a discussion and openness to think about safety. The door is open just a crack, and so it's up to American negotiators to go there and try and give it a nudge and open it more. I'm Stephanie Flanders, head of government and economics at Bloomberg, and this is Trumponomics, the podcast that looks at the economic world of Donald Trump. How he's already shaking up the global economy and what on earth is going to happen next. Last week we looked ahead to this week's summit between the US and Chinese presidents. Now, I'm recording this on Tuesday, May 12th, and by the time you listen to this, President Trump should already have touched down in Beijing ready for what feels like a high-stakes encounter on Thursday with President Xi Jinping. Even though the expectations for concrete policy outcomes, as we heard last week, are firmly under control. Now along with trade, one of the big issues hanging over that conversation will be US-China competition in advanced technologies and the race to dominate AI. Tesla's Elon Musk and Apple's Tim Cook are in the group of top executives Trump's decided to invite with him to China. And there is some mild speculation that President Trump might further loosen the export controls on US cutting-edge semiconductors for Chinese companies. But the safety of this race, protecting the world from the misuse of these increasingly powerful tools both countries are now capable of producing, well, that doesn't seem to be high on the agenda. This week's guest on Trumponomics thinks that is a big and potentially dangerous missed opportunity. Sebastian Mallaby is a senior fellow at the Council on Foreign Relations, author of multiple award-winning books and most recently of The Infinity Machine: Demis Hassabis, DeepMind and the Quest for Superintelligence. I sat down with him in our New York studio a few weeks ago now to talk about his new book, the future of AI, and the role of China, which he had recently visited. I think you'll find what he had to say fascinating. Whether hopeful or pretty alarming, well, I'll leave that to you. Sebastian, welcome. Thank you very much for being here in the flesh in the New York studio which is always fun. There's so many themes coming out of your book, The Infinity Machine. I wanted to start with something that we talk about a fair bit on Trumponomics, which is the impact of AI on the economy, society, and I guess especially jobs. But I think given the themes of the book and I know things that you've been thinking about since it came out, we'll move on fairly quickly to the question of safety. How worried should we be about the pace of the improvement in AI's capacity, but also how likely is it that governments or anyone else will be able to bend it to their will? I mean, humanity's will? So first thing first, we had a conversation with Daron Acemoglu about it recently. I mean, economists I've noticed tend to divide into those who say AI's going to have a very radical impact on business models, on the economy, on society, but that's going to take a long time and we will have time to prepare. There's another group who say, "No, no, this is all going to happen much sooner, but it will probably be less seismic." You had all these years researching this book, really up close looking at the evolution of this technology. What's your best guess on that? Yeah, I think the second view, which is that there'll be an explosion in the power of the models and it'll immediately affect the economy, is put about by the technologists who are just focused on the algorithms and on the models and what the base model can do. And it completely underestimates the rollout challenges. If you think about this idea of recursive self-improvement which preoccupies a lot of people in Silicon Valley, once you've got a machine that is good enough to write the code for the next iteration of the machine and then it recursively self-improves, it'll just explode upwards. And that is true so far as the computer science goes. It's not remotely true as far as actually applying it to the real economy goes because you need to build the computing capacity to serve those models. So you need all the chips and there's a shortage of chips. You then need all the power to fuel all those chips. The people who are in the center of this are talking about having to launch data centers in space. That just gives you a sense of how difficult this is going to be. And that's not to say anything about the institutional frictions of integrating AI into say a law firm or a bank or whatever. I mean, that's a non-trivial challenge. You've got to get all your clients to sign off on the confidentiality of their information and so forth. So I do think that rolling it out such that it makes a difference in terms of job displacement or productivity is more kind of a decade plus than it is a kind of three to five year thing. And on your point about the exponential improvement, I guess the other thing and colleagues who are much more focused on this in the economics team have also said this to me. When you think about a sort of pie chart of all the kinds of tasks that humans do that are part of the workplace, that exponential improvement is really only in a certain subset of those tasks. There are other areas where they're really quite far behind that are quite important for jobs. Yeah. Another way of saying the same thing is that there are sectors which are probably going to be quite difficult to disrupt. The public sector is pretty slow at adopting tech and that's a pretty big chunk of some economies, right? Construction, healthcare, there's a whole slew of economic sectors which don't seem to be very right for disruption at all. And then the ones that are right for disruption, the archetypical people working on the screen doing knowledge work and analysis, which is very similar to what AI does. As I say, you've got to get the customers okay with it and that's quite a challenge. The railways is the example that's often used, and it takes a long time for business models to adapt to that. If I think of something like the smartphone, which is extraordinarily new by historical standards, less than a decade old. Day to day, all of us have been transformed by that. Is it comparable to that? That it's not necessarily having a big change on the economy or on productivity, but day to day, we are all being affected really quite quickly. Yeah, I think there's a lot of truth to that, that people will be using it, they already are using it. It'll occupy quite a lot of their headspace. But it's not necessarily making them more productive. One of my favorite stories about the Anthropic agentic system which was out in January of this year. This is before Mithos, but the earlier one. People got very excited about, "Ooh, this agent can organize my email, it can create a bespoke news summary for me in the morning." And so a friend of mine was implementing all these productivity-enhancing hacks and finally after doing about four of these, she said to the agent, "Would you please now sync up my calendar such that you check that the train timetables on average are not too late" or some sort of extra productivity thing. To which Anthropic answered, or Claude answered, "Are you sure you're not just giving me this task because you're trying to avoid your own work?" You know, so I mean, I do think there's a difference between the excitement around it and the adoption of it to actually make you more productive. And then even in science, Alphafold which was the DeepMind system published in 2020, end of 2020, for which Demis Hassabis got the Nobel Prize, which unraveled protein folding. This was said at the time to be the breakthrough for faster drug discovery. And it probably will be over the longer term. But now six years later, there isn't a single drug that's been discovered thanks to Alphafold. Not to say that there hasn't been progress, because now there's a much more sophisticated build out of Alphafold with other aspects of the drug discovery process being automated by AI. So I think on a kind of 10-15 year horizon, yes, it will massively accelerate drug discovery, but on a six-year horizon it hasn't. That's a really interesting example. There is a wonderful, one of the good documentaries about that whole period. It's almost a sort of companion to your book or a part of your book, but there's a wonderful bit of footage where they have decided to just identify all of the proteins and then just put them onto the internet and allow researchers anywhere to access them. And they're watching in real time the uploads from all over the world on a map of the world, and it's very moving because you're sort of seeing in India and Africa all these researchers a lot more than they thought in the space of a few hours uploading all this information. But it's fascinating to hear that they have not actually produced any new drugs on the back of it. I mean, that goes to something as we move into the broader sort of policy and safety territory a little bit, but Daron Acemoglu was on the show recently and I was checking in with him because we'd talked to him over time on this very issue. And his focus and his advice to governments had been to think about, you can't alter the nature of the technology but you can change the way it's adopted and that adoption was going to be the main thing that determined how jobs were affected, whether you're enhancing productivity of humans or merely just allowing businesses to replace humans. I later had a conversation with Jason Furman about that, the former head of the Council of Economic Advisers, and he thought that was just completely wishful thinking, that you can't tilt the technology even to that extent, that you can encourage it to be more complementary to labor as opposed to just substituting for labor. So what do you think about that? You know, I think in a race dynamic where you have multiple labs in multiple countries producing this stuff, the idea that you're going to control all of them in some direction that makes the technology complementary to labor, not replacing, that strikes me, I'm kind of on Jason's side on that. The other thing to say is that the labs are going to create what the consumers want. And if the consumers, meaning in some cases the business consumers, the enterprises, have an opportunity to replace labor, they want to take that. And if they don't, their competitors in some other country will. And so the laws of competition and capitalism, I think, are very much on Jason's side. I think part of that is this dynamic which is also very relevant to the book of the race with other countries, particularly the race with China. But if you're a country, if you're the UK for example, or could be any European country that's not necessarily going to be at the forefront of the technology but clearly does want to be at the forefront or close to the front in adoption in a way that also helps the economy, helps their workers. Is there any advice from your book or from the research that you've done that you would have for governments who are trying to do that? Yeah, very much. I mean, I think there's a lot of commentators, especially in Britain, draw exactly the wrong lesson about the story of DeepMind. The classic complaint is: DeepMind is a British company, founded in London, the main founder is British, he's very patriotic about it, he refuses to move to Silicon Valley, and it turns out to be the most consequential sort of pioneer AI lab in the world. That's amazing. But these idiots sell it to Google in 2014 and it becomes a subsidiary of an American company and that's terrible. And I think that's wrongheaded in the sense that really the sale to Google by DeepMind in 2014 was a trick, one could say, to get the American parent to pour nearly a billion dollars a year of research and development money into London with enormous spillover effects for the tech ecosystem in London. So I think that's a win for Britain. The company stayed in London, the founder stayed in London, that's great for Britain. What was terrible for Britain, and about which Britain should beat itself up mightily, is that my book also tells the story of DeepMind trying to help the National Health Service. And both Demis's mother trained as a nurse, and his co-founder Mustafa Suleyman, his mother trained as a nurse. For this reason they really wanted to help the NHS. They were willing to do it pro bono for the first five years, and they started helping and rolling out AI algorithms that recognized early signs of blindness, early signs of cancer. This was just super positive helpful stuff that was going to save the NHS time and help NHS patients. And it got blown up by a stupid backlash over privacy or privacy, depending on which side of the Atlantic you're on, which, I mean I really kicked the tires on this, the complaint about leaks of patient data were fictitious. And so Britain, because of this political climate of suspicion of tech, particularly suspicion of the subsidiary of an American tech company, created such a backlash against this collaboration between the NHS and DeepMind that both the doctors and the computer scientists at DeepMind got cold feet and the collaboration collapsed. And Britain could have been really the cutting-edge country in the world on medical AI and they blew it. But putting together the two pieces of what you said, they are related because one of the reasons that there was a lack of trust was the foreign ownership, the US ownership, and particularly the association with major tech companies in Silicon Valley who have not necessarily earned our trust, many people would say, over the last 20 years. They've made promises about privacy, about sharing of information that have been broken; every single major tech company has broken them, including Google. They have changed the terms on which even they were developing AI. In different cases there were promises even that had been built originally into the DeepMind charter around safety and other things which then got overridden as they needed just more and more money from the parent. So it seems to me, yes, in principle it would have been great to have the UK on the cutting edge, but also completely understandable that having given tech companies the benefit of the doubt at various other times and rather regretted it, that they wouldn't want to do it in the case of this very sensitive subject. Yeah, that's all true. At the same time, it would have been nice if the critics looked at the specifics of what was going on in this collaboration between DeepMind and the National Health Service. And if they'd looked more carefully at the specifics they would have seen that this was positive for Britain. And if you just have a blanket view that anything to do with American tech is ipso facto a matter of suspicion because there's been bad behavior in other instances... I'm not in favor of that. I think that Britain did miss an opportunity there. But I think the issue that arises out of this is that it's in the interests of the tech companies, especially now with this super powerful technology AI, to get governance right, to be proactive in inviting governments with democratic legitimacy to hold sway over the technology. Because if you just take it onto yourself as a youngish startup and you say, "Well, this is how healthcare is going to evolve, I'm doing it," it's not legitimate and people won't accept it. I mean some of this gets to that sort of broader question of our degree of control and also the degree to which any of the people involved in this can shape the outcome in line with what they themselves want when they're in the competitive context. You have a very striking phrase in your book towards the end: "Inventors dream of shaping the technology that they create. Often the technology shapes them, the technology plus the business political and geopolitical currents that it unleashes." And there's a bit in your book where it starts to feel, I felt like it felt like some of the historical analysis that you see of the start of World War I. You have everyone individually in your story had potentially the right incentives, was very focused on being careful, identifying risks ahead of time, managing them ahead of time, not inflicting things onto society before we were ready for them. The end result has not been that at all. That's right. The central character in my book Demis Hassabis is a good person, has good values, but whether he can actually do good even if he is good is the central conundrum. And he really wanted to make things safer, that's why he fought a secret three-year war with Google trying to force them to put more safety guardrails around AI. That's why he suggested to Rishi Sunak the idea of a Bletchley Park AI safety conference which happened in late 2023. So he does care about safety. But when other labs are willing to go fast, there isn't much a single AI leader can do. And what of course that points you to is that the collective action problem needs to be solved by government. But what we've seen in a lot of these cases is the researchers involved, the companies involved say, "Yes, we think it's very important to have a collective agreement." But if a government comes along with concrete rules, whether it's the EU internet safety rules or AI safety rules—President Biden had had a rather different set of rules—there tends to be the response, "How could governments possibly understand this technology? We want some agreement but not this one." So how do you solve that? Yeah. Look, you're right that at the moment there is lots of state-level initiatives to regulate AI in the US and for example OpenAI is actually funding the opposition to those regulatory efforts. So there is hypocrisy sometimes. And after the fact they often say, "Yes, actually it's quite good and we've adopted it," but they never want it in advance. Yeah. On the other hand, there is a counter-example which is that in the Biden administration when they set up the AI Safety Institute and it included a mandatory requirement for all the frontier labs to submit their models to this institute for review. The institute didn't have a veto, which it should have had in my view, but still it was mandatory to submit. And I've spoken to the Biden people who created that executive order and they say that there was zero resistance from the labs. All the labs were more in the mode of saying, "Look, you need to be aware something's coming down the pike, it's very powerful, you need to get ahead of it." And when they did get ahead of it or they started to at least move on it, there wasn't resistance. That's what the Biden people say. So I think it depends. It's a bit like with Google and DeepMind and the National Health Service in Britain. There can be good instances of technology company behavior. And again if we paint, this is true of anything, like if you paint the Chinese as uniformly bad you can never collaborate with them on anything and that's actually a bad outcome in itself. Same with the tech companies. Mention China. In a US context with and the group of companies that you're writing about in your book. I could totally see how you have a kind of circle of trust among those companies and shared incentives. But the thing that I think got in the way then and gets in the way often in practice is this fear of China, the outsider which is not necessarily part of the system. And if you want to be not applying the brakes, if you want to not have these kind of controls, all you need to say is, "Well, China's going to get ahead of us." Yeah. So what I think about that is that the West really needs to remember what happened in the Cold War. What happened in the Cold War in the 1950s was you had simultaneously moments of extreme tension. The Hungarian uprising, Soviet tanks going into Hungary. You have the Suez Canal crisis at the same time which prompted the Soviets to actually threaten the West with nuclear attack because the Suez intervention was viewed as imperialist by the Russians. And simultaneously with those events you had the creation of the the IAEA, the Institute for Atomic Energy, I'm forgetting the... but basically counting all the nuclear material and trying to prevent the nuclear material from going into loose nukes. Then in the 1960s you get the Cuban Missile Crisis, enormously tense moment, and six years later you get the Nuclear Non-Proliferation Treaty. So you can do what Reagan said, which is sort of trust but verify, do arms control even if there is competition with China. And when I went to China in March because my book came out there first and spent eight days talking to AI leaders both in academia and in the private sector, it was stunning to me how often they raised the subject of safety spontaneously. They would say, "These agents are becoming powerful, we have a window of opportunity before they are too powerful to make them aligned with human incentives. The government should be warning people about dangers with these models." And in fact while I was there, Open-Claude, this open-source agent which, you know, is not a good thing to put in your computer because it can do whatever it wants with all of the data. That was being downloaded by Chinese mums and pups onto their laptops and the government wanted them not to. So the idea that China doesn't care about safety is wrong. There is a debate there and they love regulating the internet. That's what they're very good at in China. So why wouldn't we talk to them and say, "Look, we're going to probably compete on a sort of grand geostrategic military level. But we don't want this technology leaking out all over the world and being used by terrorists, criminals, non-state actors to crash the web and do cyber hacking and build a bioweapon." I mean, nobody wants that. You talked about the importance of trust earlier. I guess the question is have we got to a point with China where there is enough trust? I mean the comparison with Russia is an interesting one. That was, you could say in a sense, that was a semi-stable equilibrium. The trust and verify was one of the things that made it stable. But it was an established competition of superpowers. China is a much more in flux. We've had administrations have quite different approaches to controlling or competing or working with China. A lot of people will even listen to what you've said about your trip and say, "Well, yes, they discourage their own people from using these things just as they have much tougher controls on social media with their own population and they don't tend to be so worried when they export all these products to the rest of the world." So do you think there's trust there? I think at a high level what we should first get on the table is that the alternative to not even trying with China is that we just resign ourselves to having AI go viral all over the world with zero controls. The fact is China has cutting-edge AI labs; they're only six months behind the frontier in the US. And if they get a really powerful model that can do cyber hacking for example, and they put it in their systems and their systems are open source, it's game over. This is just going to proliferate all around the world. So that is a very bad outcome. So let's try and be creative about at least trying with China to talk to them. And I think the notion that it was easier in the Cold War, not really. I mean, look, Khrushchev was this sort of mercurial unpredictable semi-stable leader and the Russians would go to the UN and take their shoes off and bang them on the table. It was not an easy relationship in the 60s, right? And yet there was the Nuclear Non-Proliferation Treaty. So I really don't think we should give up. Okay, so the latest example we've had and we discussed a while back on Trumponomics is Mithos. And you know, there's obviously been a care in the way that it's been introduced, controls on who can have it. I know you feel strongly about the difference, the sort of safety differences between open-source and closed-source technologies. Talk us through how some of the stuff we've just been talking about, how that's been illuminated or not by the example of Mithos. Yeah, so I think what's happened here is that whilst there was progress towards some AI safety regulation by governments in 2023 and 2024, with national AI safety institutes being set up in multiple countries, a meeting of all those institutes in San Francisco in the fall of 2024. Once President Trump came into office, the whole system shifted towards acceleration and competition and the momentum for regulation died, even though the technology itself was speeding ahead. So now we find ourselves in 2026 and what we all knew was going to happen has happened. There is a model that if in the wrong hands could be used to basically steal everybody's money from their online bank accounts, crash internet systems that control dams and so forth. It's a pretty scary prospect. And there is no government mechanism to stop that from leaking out. And there's no international mechanism because you need to do it internationally because this will be copied by the Chinese at some point. So it's a big problem. So Anthropic's response to this is to say, "There's no governmental governance, so we're going to do private governance." We, Anthropic, a five-year-old startup in San Francisco, are going to arrogate unto ourselves the power to say these are the 40 entities that will get access to this technology and the rest of you cyber peons, you're outside the castle, outside our protection and you can just you know stuff it. I don't think that's a sustainable position for a private company to be in, to be the arbiter of who is safe on the internet and who isn't. But it's faute de mieux. It's like there is no governance from the government; we're left with this private solution which is not sustainable. So I think what this is a wake-up call in terms of our earlier conversation. It's we don't have an alternative but to try and do something with China because they have these open-source labs which are putting this AI into everybody's hands in a way that is totally not controlled. So it's not theoretical now that we might have an AI that's dangerous. We have one that's dangerous. So I think Mithos is a wake-up call and looking back at the history of AI in my book, there are other moments when private labs thought that they could do governance themselves. OpenAI thought that it had a kind of for-profit non-profit capitalist hybrid that would make it all safe. It didn't work. DeepMind tried the same thing with Google. It didn't work. Basically individual labs just can't deliver governance. It needs to be government. If you think that the open-source putting and just putting open-source technology into people's hands is probably the single most dangerous thing around at this level of technology and China is still doing that despite as you say being so concerned about safety. Doesn't that suggest that there's the same dynamic operating in China? That or despite much talk and concern about safety, they are still hardwired to be moving ahead. Yeah, I think there's a couple of things there. I mean, one is that any lab that is not right at the frontier can't compete by telling customers, "We've got the best technology." So they compete by saying, "My technology's more available to you. You can download it into your server and do whatever you want with it." And that's what, you know, Meta does in the US; they're open-source. That's what Mistral does in France; they are open-source. And that's what all the Chinese labs do because all of these players have in common the fact that they are not one of the big three: Anthropic, OpenAI, Google DeepMind. And so this is just what followers do. And to stop them from doing that, again you need some government action. And yes, the Chinese do have a sort of race mentality, they do want to build AI as fast as possible because they're frightened of falling behind the West. And that's just the normal natural obvious thing for them to do in the face of an American leadership which is also racing. You're going to need both sides to slow down. But my point about the discussion in China which I came in to touch with when I was there is that there is at least a discussion and openness to think about safety. The door is open just a crack, and so it's up to American negotiators to go there and try and give it a nudge and open it more. It's very striking to me the conversations about AI safety, the Bletchley summit that Rishi Sunak had. There was a lot of focus on killer robots, existential threats, the sort of much darker potential scenarios, the elimination of humanity, all of those things. And there was a general feeling in the industry and even among some policymakers that the focus on that was to some extent counter-productive because it was distracting from the sort of short-term things that actually might cause us more harm: the impact of chatbots on children, all of those kind of things. It feels like we've now swung completely in favor of thinking about those things, those kind of social harms. In fact even today we had a devastating story about AI-generated child porn and how that's making it extremely difficult for investigators to really to identify the true abuse of children in the footage. And just as well we get very depressed reading it. But it's sort of ironic to me that just as we've been focused on those social harms, actually those slightly more existential harms have become, or the risks have become, much greater. Yeah. I mean I think that the existential risks are still there. And when I talk to Demis Hassabis as I did again this week, he still worries about that stuff. Both and by existential what I really mean is two things. First is that bad guys get the technology and do something really catastrophic with it: build a bioweapon, do an enormous cyberattack which floods a city by hacking a dam or something. Second, the machine itself goes rogue and starts attacking humans. And I always thought while I was writing this book that that second thing was science fiction nonsense. You know, the machines will be more intelligent than humans, but they don't have a motive to attack humans because they're not evolved to survive. Unlike humans, we were always watching out for the lion on the savannah because we want to pass on our DNA. Machines don't have DNA. But then I had this rather disturbing conversation with the computer scientist Geoffrey Hinton. I went up to Toronto and sat in his kitchen for two hours and he said to me, "Look, suppose you have an AI which is very powerful but you're worried that the enemy AI will attack it. What are you going to do? You're going to tell your AI to defend itself. If you see the attack coming, counter-attack. Make sure you don't get killed." You've just given it a survival instinct. So now are you so comforted, Sebastian? Do you think that AI won't have a survival instinct? I think this existential stuff is real. It can be addressed by engineering what they call alignment into the models where you're basically giving instructions to the system not to turn against humans. And that's an engineering challenge which I think probably is solvable. But you need time to solve it. And the thing about alignment is that what we really care about is aligning not necessarily today's model but the harder, the tougher, more dangerous, more powerful one which we might have in six months or a year. And you can't invent the alignment of the future model until you've got it in the lab and now you're trying to align it. So the faster that progress goes, the less likely we are to do alignment. And that's another reason why we need government controls. The government we have currently, as you pointed out, the US administration—and obviously it's the US government that's going to have the biggest influence by definition on this at the moment—is one that is not minded towards safety, is very focused on the arms race aspect with China. Given what you've been saying about the pace of change, the fact that we already have potentially enormously damaging instruments that could be tomorrow in the wrong people's hands. Is it going to really matter a lot to all of us that this administration with those instincts was in power for at least the next two years? Yeah. I think there's a real danger that will turn out to be decisive. On the upside, it's interesting that with Mithos, the Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent immediately worried that this was going to be catastrophic for the stability of the banking system because bank accounts would be hacked. And he called in the heads of the big banks and told them to take it seriously. And he apparently inside the administration favored a nationalization of Mithos because he thought it was so scary. So I think it's possible that as these models become more threatening, the Trump administration which is flexible, one could say, might do the kind of U-turn it's done on the notion that you shouldn't intervene in other countries and start wars. It clearly forgot that principle and so perhaps it will change on AI as well. Even the instincts that we're seeing on that seem to be much more about protecting American banks and maybe UK banks. But to your point about Anthropic really controlling who has this, is it really realistic that you can be just picking and choosing countries that get this defense? I don't think it's realistic for Anthropic to continue to do this. Therefore I think we have to hope that the sheer evidence of the disruptive power of the models eventually gets the US government to change its position and then we could see a sort of norm cascade where other countries also change. Well, you say in your book that you start off as an optimist on these things and I hope that you've still managed to sustain a little bit of optimism. But Sebastian, thank you so much. So nice to be with you Stephanie. Thanks for listening to Trumponomics from Bloomberg. It was hosted by me, Stephanie Flanders. I was joined by Sebastian Mallaby, senior fellow at the Council on Foreign Relations and author of The Infinity Machine: Demis Hassabis, DeepMind and the Quest for Superintelligence. Trumponomics was produced by Summer Sadi and Moses Andam with help from Amy Keen. Sound design was by Blake Maples and Kelly Gary. To help others find this show and enjoy it, please rate and review it highly wherever you listen.

The year's largest IPO — Cerebras joins the hottest trade in AI — 5/14/2026

CNBC Television(3.4M) ·53:22 ·6.3K views · 2026-05-14
Bob Komin (CFO, Cerebras) Ram Parameswaran (Octahedron Capital)
Cerebras’ explosive 70%+ IPO debut validates massive public market appetite for AI infrastructure, signaling a shift toward valuing specialized "pure-play" hardware companies on multi-year revenue projections rather than immediate earnings.
TL;DR Cerebras’ explosive 70%+ IPO debut validates massive public market appetite for AI infrastructure, signaling a shift toward valuing specialized "pure-play" hardware companies on multi-year revenue projections rather than immediate earnings. Record-Breaking Debut: Cerebras...

TL;DR

Cerebras’ explosive 70%+ IPO debut validates massive public market appetite for AI infrastructure, signaling a shift toward valuing specialized "pure-play" hardware companies on multi-year revenue projections rather than immediate earnings.

The Cerebras IPO: Market Performance and Valuation

  • Record-Breaking Debut: Cerebras (trading on Nasdaq) opened as the largest semiconductor IPO in history, with the stock popping over 70% on its first day.
      • Investor Demand: The offering was more than 20 times oversubscribed, reflecting what CFO Bob Komin described as a "tricky balance" in pricing despite leaving money on the table.
  • Aggressive Valuation: The stock is trading at roughly 150x sales, significantly higher than Nvidia’s ~28x earnings multiple.
      • Forward-Looking Multiples: Komin argues the market is valuing the company on 2028 revenue projections; when discounted back, he claims the valuation is in line with or at a discount to industry leaders like Nvidia.
  • The "Obvious Trade": Ram Parameswaran (Octahedron Capital) notes that semiconductors are currently the only "working" sector in tech, as investors move "bottleneck by bottleneck" to find AI infrastructure plays.

Growth Drivers and Supply Chain Constraints

  • Insatiable Compute Demand: Cerebras reports being sold out through 2027, with the sales team currently signing customers for capacity that won't be available until next year.
      • Manufacturing Ramp: To meet demand, the company must ramp manufacturing 10 times this year and another 10 times next year.
  • Infrastructure Bottlenecks: Growth is increasingly threatened by external factors such as power moratoriums in Virginia, Georgia, and Ireland.
      • Data Center Availability: While hyperscalers require gigawatt-scale facilities, Cerebras focuses on a level below that, though Komin admits grid constraints remain a significant challenge to the roadmap.
  • The "Durability" Thesis: Parameswaran suggests the typical three-year semiconductor cycle has been elongated to at least 2028 due to the generative AI super-cycle.

Customer Concentration and Competitive Strategy

  • The OpenAI Factor: Cerebras is highly concentrated, with OpenAI representing a $20 billion backlog.
      • Sticky Agreements: To mitigate risk, Cerebras secured a $1 billion upfront payment from OpenAI and structured the deal around specific performance obligations for 750 megawatts of compute.
  • Diversification Pipeline: The company is looking to move beyond OpenAI and G42 by targeting hyperscalers (e.g., AWS) and other frontier labs to normalize customer concentration over time.
  • SambaNova’s Alternative Path: Direct competitor SambaNova is focusing on "Sovereign AI" and enterprise on-premise deployments.
      • Efficiency Gains: CEO Rodrigo Liang highlights a power efficiency advantage, claiming SambaNova racks run at 10 kilowatts compared to 100 kilowatts for some competitors, allowing for deployment in existing data centers.

The Financialization of Compute

  • The Launch of Compute Futures: In a move to stabilize the market, CME Group and Silicon Data are launching a futures market for compute capacity.
  • Managing Volatility: Carmen Li (Silicon Data) compares compute to jet fuel for airlines.
      • Risk Transfer: For the AI economy to mature, buyers (AI labs) and sellers (GPU clouds) need the ability to hedge against price volatility and supply shocks.
  • Price Discovery: While compute usually gets cheaper over time, supply constraints for new chips (like the Blackwell B200) can cause temporary price spikes, necessitating benchmark indices for A100, H100, and B200 chips.

Notable Quotes

"Investors largely told us that they saw that the architecture the company has built is just customized for low-latency, high-speed inference... producing tokens has become a huge focus for everyone."

— Bob Komin, CFO of Cerebras

"Right now, only one thing is working in all of technology, which is semiconductors. There is no bid for almost anything else... It's what I call the obvious trade."

— Ram Parameswaran, Founder of Octahedron Capital

"How can you run a business without being able to hedge the biggest raw material cost of your operation? You just can't... Similarly, the biggest line item for AI companies will be the GPU cost or token cost."

— Carmen Li, CEO of Silicon Data

There's one hour left of trading today and Cerebras is up more than 70 percent on its first day as a public company. It's a massive pop for what was already compute demand running above every forecast. We hear it all the time that the backlog is real and the biggest customers are the biggest names in AI. That is all true. But the bears point to the 70 percent run this year and that most of this stock isn't even trading yet. Insiders do own almost all of it and they're locked up, so when that starts to expire this summer, a flood of new shares hits the market. History says that's when IPOs, especially blockbuster IPOs like this one, come back down to earth a little. So it all raises the big question out of this IPO: Is this rational behavior we're seeing in the public markets, or is it frothy? OpenAI, SpaceX, Anthropic, they're all lining up to go public. Every IPO behind this one is watching how it trades in the weeks and the months ahead. Today on the live stream, we have this trade surrounded. We have the CFO of Cerebras on his company's first day as a public stock; it's still trading, still up more than 70 percent. We've also got a hedge fund manager who has to decide if this works. We've got the CEO of the competitor still on the outside looking in, that's SambaNova's founder and CEO, and the woman who's building the first futures market for compute itself, on what this all means and where this market that is on fire is going. Let's start at the natural place with the company. Bob Komin is the CFO of Cerebras. He just watched his stock double on its first day as a public company. Bob, welcome to the show. How are you feeling? I'm feeling fantastic. It's been an incredible few weeks and today is just the cherry on top. Are you watching the stock price right now or are you trying to already sort of put that aside, or will you continue to look at it? I already have it. It's already off on the side. Me too. It is 73.5 percent higher, at 321. So we'll—I'm sure we'll be looking at it right until the market closes and in the after hours. So let me ask you: How did you and the bankers decide to land where you did in terms of price? We saw an almost doubling this morning. There's always that question: Did you leave money on the table? It's always a very tricky balance and I think in this case, it was a particularly difficult thing to try to peg. We just had so much demand. We were over 20 times oversubscribed. And being in the right place, with the right product, in a market that's moving as fast as AI compute is, it was very challenging. You always just do the best job that you can, looking at the book, looking at where demand falls off, and then you have to get it out to the marketplace and see what people think. We knew that many people had dramatically lower allocations than what they would have liked to have received, and we thought that there would be, you know, upside to the stock today. We'll see where it lands, but I think you just do the best job that you can. Certainly. I spoke to a few of those that were holding out and hoping for allocation right up until yesterday. So what did the market tell you? Specifically, what did investors tell you on the roadshow about why they wanted in, and how were you thinking about the retail investor? Investors largely told us that they saw that the architecture that the company has built is just customized for low latency, high-speed inference. Over the last six months in particular, as AI demand has gone through the roof and producing tokens has become a huge focus for everyone, the interactive experience that users can have with fast inference has been something that is not only valuable, but something that makes people stay and use AI more. You get better answers when you can utilize the speed, and I think investors really believe that that's the direction that we're going in. You serve—go ahead, sorry Bob. Finish that thought. Nope. No. Go ahead, please. Well, you serve a really sort of interesting specific part of the market, right? These smaller or more specialized models, which it feels like has been growing in popularity, especially as AI prices rise. What are you seeing on your end in terms of compute demand and specifically for the kind of models that you guys serve? So Deirdre, I think I'm really happy that you brought that up because actually that's one of the biggest misconceptions. We serve all models and there is no limit to the size of the models that we can serve. Today, we're serving trillion-parameter models. We serve Qwen2, and we're serving trillion-parameter models that are internal for OpenAI today. So we actually can be used for all types of models, open or closed, at all size. Okay. I'm glad you bring that up. Are you being used for any of the frontier models right now? We are. We are currently running OpenAI o1-preview and o1-mini with them. These have not been released yet to the public. That's coming very soon. So our teams have been working incredibly close together on all of the models right at the state of the art, right at the edge. More broadly, what are you seeing in terms of compute demand and how does it compare to what you were modeling say six months ago? It feels like on CNBC we can't stop talking about compute constraints, tokenomics, and everyone just trying to get their hands on as much compute as they can. For our fast inference product, we are—there's so much demand that our biggest challenge is actually trying to supply it. So we are adding as much manufacturing and data center capacity as we possibly can as fast as we can, and we're still sold out into 2027. Our sales team is signing people up for capacity that we won't have available until next year. That's what our real focus is. We don't see an end to the demand. We are trying to catch up with it. Okay, well let me talk a little bit about the skeptics' argument, right? They look at that backlog and it's strong, right? But it's also concentrated. One customer, 20 billion dollars of it, maybe some concentration risk. What do they have wrong here? I don't think they have anything wrong, but the fact is, you know, when you have big customers and you're starting from a small scale, they are going to be a lot of your revenue and a high percentage of it. So when we started, people made comments about the fact that our partner G42, which was doing over a billion dollars of business with us over a few years, we were very concentrated with them, but we were starting off of a very small base. Now we have an over 20 billion dollar customer. Of course, they're going to be a high percentage of our revenue. But the second step out was to get a foundation model lab using our hardware, and then the third step is our first hyperscaler customer with AWS and we haven't even gotten started with them. When we have all three going, the customer concentration will change and, you know, how do you build up a big company? You start with one large customer, add another, add another, and we're on that trajectory right now. And we also have many other customers that are smaller. Right. And that's fair. Though the AWS still needs the ink to dry and be sorted through and everything. So right now you are highly dependent on OpenAI. What happens if their roadmap changes and as CFO Bob, how are you preparing or hedging for that? So there were several things that we did. It was a unique deal for OpenAI. They actually provided a 1 billion dollar upfront payment when they signed with us, which they've not done with other compute vendors that they've had. We are delivering it in tranches to them so we have a lot of visibility as we add the capacity into our data center. The nature of the contract is as long as we're upholding our end and meeting our performance obligations, OpenAI actually will continue to buy the 750 megawatts that's committed to us. I think we tried to make the arrangement much stickier and I think we have a great start with them. We got them going on our hardware in only a couple months after we started the deal, which is exactly what they wanted. And I think that with the amount of demand that they have, the more compute that they have access to, the more they will buy. So they're a fantastic customer. That's what they look like right now and that's great that you have visibility and you made the deal sticky, but what does the actual financial model look like if they do scale back? Well, if they scale back, we have so much other demand that we're deferring today that we would just replace them. We would be able to accelerate other customers that are in our pipeline that would like to get access to our compute resources even sooner. Are you having talks with any other frontier labs or hyperscalers? We talk to everybody. Are you getting closer to more deals? There will be, I'm sure you will see us announce other deals over the year, but again, we have such a huge commitment here at the start. We have to ramp manufacturing about 10 times this year. We're going to do that again next year. So there is a limit to how fast we can actually take on large new customers. Right. A lot of things have to come in place before you actually start seeing that revenue. Do you know when that revenue starts showing up? We have, I mean, we're recognizing revenue right now on the OpenAI deal. We continue to have significant revenue coming from existing customers like G42, who's buying hundreds of millions of dollars from us per year. And then with the AWS deal, that probably will be later this year and into next year when it will start to ramp. Great. Still looking at the stock. You're at almost 80 percent up and so the market is essentially valuing you at, I think if you double, it's around 150 times sales. Nvidia, right, who has been the leader obviously, a legacy chipmaker, is trading at around 28 times earning. How do you explain that gap to a regular investor that's watching this? I think we're getting paid—I think what investors told us is they're actually valuing us on 2028 revenues. If you look at us relative to Nvidia, out a couple of years and discount us back, we're probably trading in line or maybe even at a discount to them. You look at where we have been when we were smaller, then the numbers look much higher. Okay, but still, against a giant like Nvidia. Yes. Nvidia is, you know, they're huge. We're getting to our first billion dollars of revenue. They're doing 60 billion a quarter. We think that the market is telling us that they believe that the platform that we've built and the competitive advantage that we have in our part of the market is significant and that we will add significant large customers over the next few years. Bob, you talked about ramping up on manufacturing. Really the bull case depends on hyperscalers being able to continue to build data centers and the labs as well. But we are seeing these power moratoriums in places like Virginia, Georgia, Ireland, you know, several jurisdictions that are slowing these new approvals that you need to actually build these data centers. How exposed is your roadmap to grid constraints? You know, we're still not at the scale of the hyperscalers. They tend to look for gigawatt size facilities. We tend to be at a level below them. I think there is a ton of demand. There's no question there's a ton of demand for both power and for data center resources around the world. We're out there. We have a strong pipeline that we believe will be able to support the growth plan that we have for the next couple of years. We're working hard at it. We look across the globe for data centers and we think it is a challenge, but we think we're up to it. How are you thinking of geopolitics in this moment, since a lot of your business is overseas? You know, I think we have to watch it closely and be dynamic. We do have a large Middle Eastern customer. Right now, the bulk of what we're doing is to serve the US market. I think we will just have to see where it—you know, it's very hard to predict. So I think we're doing the best that we can to monitor it and to diversify in countries around the world. And finally Bob, last question. This is not your first IPO. You were CFO and brought Sunrun public. How does this compare today? I think compared to all IPOs, this is a very unusual one at the size that it is. There are very few that are 5-6 billion dollars in size, certainly at this kind of market cap. I think we're the largest semiconductor IPO ever. Sunrun had a completely different feel. This one, the momentum in the industry, a pure-play AI compute company the market hasn't seen, so everybody was paying attention. The order book got full very early and there was momentum all the way till the end. And I'd have to say that in my previous experience, I wasn't able to get the book 20-plus times oversubscribed. Right. Not an easy feat. Bob Komin, thank you so much. Congratulations on just a massive milestone and what looks to be a very successful debut, 45 minutes left in the trading day. Thank you, Bob. Thank you very much. Thanks for having me. So we just heard from the company, let's get to the trade. Ram Parameswaran is the founder of Octahedron Capital, a tech-focused hedge fund. Before that, he was at Altimeter where he worked on some of the largest AI investments of the last decade. He got allocation in the Cerebras IPO, which I understand Ram was no small feat given how highly in demand it was. You just heard Bob, 20 times oversubscribed. I think you're on mute, Ram. It happens. There we go. I've got you. Yes. So first, thank you Bob for allocation. That helped. No, listen, I think it's kudos to this team. You know, I've followed Andrew for years at this point and I know it wasn't an easy journey. Lots of ups and downs along the way. A lot of naysayers along the way. Nobody believed their architecture. You know, Bob talked about single customer concentration two years ago when they had a pause in their previous IPO. So, honestly, just a testament of fortitude and resilience. So really well done, guys. So Ram, make the case then for why this multiple is rational, why you wanted to buy in, and I guess that's before we got today's first day of trade. Why was it rational before it actually started trading? Yeah. It's a good question because right now, only one thing is working in all of technology, which is semiconductors. There is no bid for almost anything else. I mean, as you know software's in all sorts of trouble, Internet is not really working, and payments is a bit of a disaster as well. So it's what I call the obvious trade. And the obvious trade started with Nvidia two years ago. That's now become the consensus trade right now. Investors are going sector by sector, bottleneck by bottleneck, company by company, to find new places to invest in. And so when you have this crazy hyperscale growth we're going through right now, because as Bob said the demand is infinite, and the ROIC for these end enterprise and consumer products are very real. You can see them in the numbers. There seems to be insatiable appetite for content. So now think about it. A typical semiconductor cycle is three years. As you know, Deirdre, we've talked about it before, we think this cycle is elongated till say 2028. So now you run the numbers out to 2028. Bob said that they are cheaper on an EV-to-sales basis looking out two years. And obviously Bob is being very conservative in his estimates because the reality is Cerebras is kind of what I think of as an N-of-one company. They still have a lot of execution ahead and their architecture still needs to scale up and it's going to be a very bumpy ride. But the reality is these guys have found a niche in building the fastest inference in the world. We talked about G42, we talked about OpenAI. I am reasonably confident every frontier lab will work with them eventually as well as every single hyperscaler will install them in their facilities. And so you look out to 2028, that multiple he talks about could be actually half. But there's going to be a lot of bumpiness ahead. It's not going to be a smooth path ahead. Right. So you're saying sort of like the traditional cyclical cycle isn't applying in this case because AI is so generational. But I guess Ram, let me push back a little bit. How do we—this rests on the assumption that demand is going to be up and to the right for many years in the future. How do we know this? Yes, we're compute constrained right now, but you know like Dario Amodei, the CEO of Anthropic, talks about this cone of uncertainty, and if you are wrong even by a year or two that can be disastrous. How certain are you that demand is going to follow this straight up and to the right path and that we are building for that? It's such a great question because I used to be a semiconductors engineer back in the day and I ran away from the industry because the industry was a very messy industry. Lots of bad behavior, lots of poor incentive structures. Things have changed quite dramatically from a sector rationality perspective. Number one, the only thing we focus on in our research is the durability of this potential trend. And right now we have reasonable conviction that there is no demand slowdown at least till 2028. So you're right, I could not tell you with a straight face that demand goes up and to the right till 2030 or 2031, but now we have the courage and the confidence to look out let's say one and a half to two years. What are the points of evidence for this? Number one, think about memory. We have long-term agreements across the board. There are take-or-pay agreements so bad behaviors are thwarted. Bob just told you that well, if OpenAI stops their demand, there is a line of people waiting to take the excess capacity from them. It's reasonably confident looking at the supply chain, looking at the channels, looking at the long-term agreements, and frankly looking at the way their structures are being built. In fact, in certain cases for example in packaging, the end customer is providing working capital financing. It's unheard of in semiconductors. So you're right, I can't tell you what happens in 29 or 30, but it looks like at least sitting today in the middle of '26, '27 is in the bag and '28 is potentially in the bag. If those two things were to happen, I think the semiconductor trade keeps going on because the multiples are not even that extended for the most part in most companies. Maybe in Cerebras's case it looks artificially expensive, but guess what, what if they sign three big contracts next quarter? I'm sure they have it in the hopper already, and the market's not ready for that. It's very hard to make a valuation case for sure in the short term, but I think the way we thought about it is: take a three-year view, it's still reasonable. Okay. So you're a buyer then at $185 a share? Are you a buyer now at 325, 400? Will you look to pick up more? Generally the way these things work is when you have such an incredible day one, there should be a bit of a pullback, you know, day two, day three, day four after the greenshoe expiration. It's all going to be price dependent and so I think the interesting part about every single technology company since the history of capitalism is that they always touch their IPO price. Every company has. So I think at some point we will have a chance to pick it up again cheaper. How much cheaper, I could not tell you. We're also in a super cycle right now. So maybe it never happens for a while. Right. So markets will not be a straight line up. That's going to be a little more volatile, which is always the case. I mean we've talked about these blockbuster IPOs and huge pops at the start. So let me expand this conversation a little bit then Ram. I know semis is consensus, up 70 percent year-to-date. Where else are you looking in AI? Does value start accruing elsewhere? Are you looking at Megacap? Are you looking at any software names that have been beaten down? The reality is for 18 to 24 months people only played the GPU trade. There are always interesting opportunities. Even within semiconductors, one of our big calls this year in January was Intel and that's done really well for us. So we identified the CPU backlog so we owned Intel and AMD as an example. Right now we think within semiconductors the most critical component is power semiconductors, so we have exposure there. We're just working our way through how does the data center get built, what is the fab capacity out there being created, and then work your way through where are the supply chain bottlenecks. We look for where the market hasn't figured out that there's going to be a massive dislocation on earnings looking out one and two years. It's a pretty disciplined process we run. But if you leave semiconductors aside for a second—my point is there's still alpha to be created in semiconductors. I think Nvidia, the best covered company in this space, is still quite undervalued even at 230 dollars a share because its earnings are going to be quite high next year. But if you move away from that, we made a counter-consensus bet in February that we called the bottom in software and specifically in infrastructure software. That's also worked reasonably well for us because if you take infrastructure software, the first theme you want to leg into I think is security. Now, the entire security companies got demolished the day the Mistral model was out by Anthropic. But what people don't realize is the amount of patching being done in Fortune 500 companies is unprecedented. Which means there's going to be this massive pull-forward demand for security software over the next say, one year. We are big investors in a company called Databricks and Databricks is seeing massive acceleration in revenues as well. Now if Databricks is seeing that revenue acceleration, I think it's reasonable to assume that Snowflake will see equal acceleration. The infrastructure database providers will see acceleration. So I don't think it's a case of: you buy semis and go to sleep. I think you got to be very paranoid in semis. But for the first time in years I'm just looking at the rest of our book that's not really working this year, but I'm just really happy because I see a cornucopia of interesting ideas if you're willing to wait one or two years. The key is stock picking and picking incredible management teams that'll work their way through the chasm of pain. But on the other side of it, great management teams and great business models always win out. So I'm quite optimistic actually. Right. Ram, I love that you gave us a public and a private name. Very useful, like a Databricks and a Snowflake. You mentioned supply chain bottlenecks; I asked Bob about this as well. Power data center moratoriums are showing up in multiple states and that is a bottleneck for actually building these data centers to put these chips in. So how are you thinking about that as an investor in the AI infrastructure trade? Yeah, this is very difficult. I think it's going to be painful, it's going to be slow. But again, incentives matter, right? I think in certain states they'll be more open to creating electricity grids and infrastructure grids. And so I assume that the capital markets will work themselves out to figuring out locations where these moratoriums are not available. Remember the nice thing about AI is you don't need absolute perfect real-time latency. You can actually have your data center a little far away from everything. And so I think it's going to naturally go towards states in America where they are very pro-business and pro-data center creation and the states that don't will get left behind. And so I just think a lot of capital moves in this space. I guess but that solves one problem, you know, at the local level fighting back, but this is now becoming like a federal issue. Politicians are taking sides in Washington. Are you optimistic that they'll even be able to be built in further away places, aside from local municipalities? Right, it's a great question. So one, we don't know and this is not something we pay attention to so I want to be very honest with you on this one. What I do think is our mapping of total coverage of wattage; we have enough capacity going on till 2028. Beyond that, we just don't know. I think typically what'll happen is if your demand is infinite and supply is constrained, what effectively happens is if there is ROI to the end customer, you have the net effect of demand being smoothed out for multiple years. And maybe that in itself is a good thing because we sometimes pray too much at the altar of hypergrowth, and me personally, I prefer durable growth. So I think between the supply constraints on just capacity of semiconductors and components, the long-term supply non-availability of energy, I think what may actually end up happening is this thing that has gone on incredibly for the last 24 months may actually have a sustainable growth era for longer than 2028, maybe 29 and 30 even. That's the way I kind of put it together. Again, it's such a fast-moving industry we've just got to keep our—we got to be super sharp and super paranoid. So that's just the way we are. Yep. That's fair. Okay Ram, last question for you. I was careful to call Cerebras the biggest IPO of the year so far. Of course, we're waiting on the SpaceX S-1, OpenAI, Anthropic; they're in the IPO pipeline. So if you could only have allocation in one of those three names, which one is it and why? Man, you put me on the spot here. The correct answer is we want allocation in everything. I hope everyone's listening to this live stream. So no, the reality is it's a rising tide that lifts all boats. We don't really cover SpaceX so we don't know much in this space. But honestly, I would love to invest in both Anthropic and OpenAI. I think it's an "and" story, not "or". We'll get allocation in actually one of them, I'm pretty confident about it, in the other one we don't know. But the reality is for people who don't get allocation I would say number one, every stock gets back to its IPO price. I used to also be very anxious back in the day when we didn't get big allocations but it doesn't really matter. There will always be a chance to buy it again if you're patient. Number two is if you don't have the capacity to get into these IPOs, well you know just buy Google. I think that just—we made a big counter-consensus bet on Google two and a half years ago when every talking head said that Google was dead and we nailed the thesis dead to rights and we basically predicted search acceleration. And I think that's the one to buy because they have the cheapest cost of tokens, they have the longest capacity structures, they have the best—they keep getting better and better models every single time, the best talent density. I mean half of OpenAI and Anthropic comes from DeepMind. And so I think the easy way to play this through the public markets is buy more Google stock. Now it's a little expensive here at 25 times forward earnings, even under my bullish assumptions, but you know on a pullback buy Google and you can clip frankly 20-25 percent in my opinion. All right. I'm not going to put you on the spot and make you choose between the three or even the two because you gave a really good answer for what you can buy in the public markets already. I guess last on Google, what are you thinking about the other public players: Meta, Microsoft, Amazon, Apple maybe? Yeah, I think Microsoft is in a bit of a funk right now. I think they need to get their products out there. But the reality is I was—my wife, who's a pretty prolific venture capitalist, speaking to a very large public group out in New York, and she said how are you guys using AI? And they said well we're all forced to use Copilot. So many of us on the coasts who are kind of bleeding-edge users of different kinds of AI—the reality is distribution matters. So I think nothing is a zero-sum game. I think all these things go up together. Amazon was dead for a long time and guess what, they signed this Anthropic deal and they showed acceleration of AWS. I think it's really incumbent on Satya to kind of figure out his next play in the AI space. He was very early to the curve with this big OpenAI investment, he's taken a bit of a step back, but I think Microsoft will be fine in the longer term. The big bet for us really is actually Meta. I think Meta has finally, based on our research, got their act together now. The AI team there is run by two incredibly talented individuals in Nat Friedman and the other gentleman there.
