And I think they will come up with surprisingly good models going forward. Who's that? Alexander Wang and who else? No. Alexander Wang runs Scale, but Nat and Daniel Gross—I've just blanked for a second. So Nat and Daniel Gross, extraordinarily talented managers, and I would argue that they have the same kind of qualities of being able to build businesses that we see also at Google for example. And are you ever going to count out Zuck? Ever? You just can't do that. And the reality is if you look at Meta's numbers, because of their crazy personalization because of AI both on the ad side and the consumer side, you have over the next let's say 18 months this very interesting situation where their revenues will continue to accelerate from 30 percent onwards. Which means they're seeing some really incredible ROIC on their Capex spend even though their Capex spend is the most inefficient amongst everyone else because they don't have the capacity to make chips. So I think Meta is highly understated here and the contrarian bet again that we're making in our portfolio is actually Meta; that's our biggest position right now. Right. Well we covered a lot of ground, Ram. Thank you. Super useful insights from Cerebras to the public plays to the infrastructure trade. Thanks so much. We'll have you on again soon I hope, for sure. Take care. Bye now. All right. Let's get to a company now on the outside looking in. SambaNova was founded the same year as Cerebras with the same goal actually: build a chip that challenges Nvidia's dominance. Today one is public and worth nearly 70 billion dollars, the other just closed a fresh round to stay private. One of the big questions coming out of this IPO is what happens next in the rest of this category. We talked a little bit about it but let's get to someone actually building here. Rodrigo Liang is the co-founder and CEO of SambaNova. Rodrigo, welcome. It's good to see you again. Yeah. Good to see you, Deirdre. Are you watching this trade as closely as everyone else's debut? And what does it mean for you guys, you know, Cerebras going public at this kind of valuation and with this kind of appetite? You know my phone's been ringing off the hook and people texting. Impressive and so first of all congratulations to the entire Cerebras team. It's a great day for the company and also for our industry. I think it shows that there's a huge appetite in the market for additional players in this space, a space that's been so dominated by Nvidia. And so companies like Cerebras, like ours, trying to come in and offer alternate solutions—I think it looks like the investor community is very excited about that. What are people texting you and calling you about? When are you going public? Well the number keeps going up as far as the share price and so people want to make sure that I saw it. But no, I think it's just incredible. Just incredible. We're living in a unique time where the market is just so excited about chips. I've been doing chips for 30 years and I've never seen anything like this where it seems like everybody wants to learn more about chips, learn more about semiconductors, and they understand the level of chip design that I've never seen in my career. Okay, so how does this, or does it change the calculus on your part of going public? Or does staying private make more sense for you right now, if so why? Well, look, I think for us it's always been directionally something that we've considered and I think you look at this as a validation of the market and the validation of what we've been building. As you mentioned we started our company just shortly after Cerebras so we've been on this journey together for the last seven-eight years and you know it's a market that's been so strongly controlled by Nvidia. But as we've now moved into inference and you're starting to see people open up for other alternative architectures, we're now seeing the business pick up. Cerebras is talking about theirs; we certainly as a private company don't talk about us but we've seen significant growth in our business as well. Really where I think this indicates to us is this stepping stone towards what we see people wanting to talk about with premium inference, right? Which is this idea that I know Jensen's talked about before and others have discussed about the marrying of inference of the largest models with speed. You want both of those things because in inference the battleground for large models is really where you're going to see a lot of interest as a next step for our industry. Right, and certainly more competition. A big part of the Cerebras pitch to investors was this OpenAI partnership that I was talking to Bob about. Correct me if I'm wrong Rodrigo, but SambaNova doesn't have a frontier lab or a major American hyperscaler as a customer. So is that a choice or are those deals not there for you? No, but we're having a lot of discussion. It's correct, we don't currently have them as a customer, but we're actually having a lot of discussions with them. Two things. As you know, you and I talked about SambaNova for quite a while; our strategy initially has been really focused on a broader base of customers through the enterprise. These are—you see a lot of our activities globally with enterprises, with banks and sovereigns. And so and more recently we announced our SN50 product that's really much more focused around large-scale cloud deployments, which is really where these frontier labs and these large cloud customers really want to go because of the size of the model and the concurrency they want. And so we're really excited with the SN50 that we recently announced that we're able to really engage deeply in those conversations. And as you know the large models when you get to trillion, two trillion, three trillion parameter models, they're just significantly more complex than say the Codex models that Cerebras is running for OpenAI. When we actually go into these larger models, we have to make sure that we can actually run these really large models at a much smaller power footprint just because the deployment is so large. Bob actually corrected me because I also thought he wasn't serving the really large frontier models. He says that he is already right now. Is that something you guys can do also or will be able to soon? Yeah, I mean we're demonstrating that today with even some of the large open-source models that are already trillion parameter models. As DeepSeek V4 came out as 1.5 trillion, you've got Kimi, some of these other models that are larger than a trillion already and moving to multi-trillion. We support that already. If people go to SambaNova Cloud you can see all those models that are in there. We run today on SN40; we already run five-trillion parameter models in a single rack, and then with the 50s we can go 10 times bigger than that. And so that's what people are starting to get excited about because you can get to this infrastructure that isn't bounded by the parameter size. Got it. Rodrigo, what are you seeing in terms of demand? We got numbers obviously in this IPO; they had to release an S-1 with all their financials. How do you characterize your backlog and durability of demand that you're seeing? You know, I mean, tremendous results from Cerebras—again with OpenAI coming in as a big part of that, it's just fantastic. It shows how much demand there is from these frontier models. For us, we have taken a much broader customer base, you know we go after dozens and dozens of customers. And so with all this excitement and interest, when we go out there and talk to people, the enterprise is yet to start. We just think that that's going to be a huge part of the market still. Enterprises are going to have a big portion of their workloads on clouds but still a big part of AI workloads that they're going actually have to keep private and secure. And so those workloads are just getting going and we're really well suited for that and then with the SN50 that's coming, we will also be competing for the large models that go into the hyperscale clouds. So you're saying that you guys are going to serve enterprise through on-prem AI deployment. Is that right? And where is that moving? We spoke to Aidan Gomez a few weeks ago at Cohere—that's what he's aiming to do with less GPUs. What does that look like? Do you think that more companies are going to go that route? Well the first step we're seeing now is with sovereign. And so sovereign clouds, you see a lot of nations actually investing in local entities to build kind of the second tier—not hyperscale clouds like the Amazons and Googles, but one tier lower for their domestic use cases. The challenge they have is around power and energy. And so they don't have the two, three, or four gigawatts of data center capacity, so they're trying to find solutions that are much more power efficient and still deploy this infrastructure. And so SambaNova, because our power is so much lower—it's 10 kilowatts per rack when Nvidia racks are a hundred—they're able to then deploy into existing data centers without having to wait for new data centers to be built and approvals and all of that. And so once they have the sovereign cloud, then you can allow local enterprises to use it and know that their data will be secure within that infrastructure. That's certainly something that a lot of countries actually have a requirement for because the data for certain countries has to remain in-country. Right, and I know earlier this year you signed a partnership with Intel. How do you think about this market? Would you rather go public or do you think that being acquired is an option for a SambaNova too? How do you just think of that tradeoff especially with Cerebras now going crazy on the public markets? Right. I mean certainly investors look at the Cerebras market and think hey this is something that we certainly should explore as the business grows. But our focus really is around building the business. With Intel as a partner, I think we've got a great partner that we can go to market with. As we see the AI solutions, we think that there is a strong connection between the AI infrastructure and the systems of record. And so as these functions are now tying together between kind of what the traditional applications run—databases and other SaaS applications—and you've got the AI agents trying to connect them, the partnership we signed with Intel is going to allow us to build this really tight interconnect between the technologies that then deliver complete solutions. So it's not just running an LLM or running a model, but it's really providing enterprise-grade solutions that tie into existing systems of record that people are already running in an enterprise to AI solutions that are going to be transformative for the business. Well Rodrigo, it is great to get your insights on a day like today. Go return those texts, answer those phone calls, I know your phone's blowing up. Thanks for joining us. We'll talk to you again soon for sure. Talk soon, Deirdre. Take care. Okay, we've talked about the company, the trade, the competition. Now let's talk about what is under all of it. We've been doing it anyway but we're going to get at this even more directly: Compute. Compute constraints, compute demand. Cerebras, Nvidia, SambaNova, all of them are selling the same underlying thing: compute capacity. And this week for the first time ever, compute is getting its own futures market. The CME Group and Silicon Data announced plans to launch it just a few days ago. Carmen Li is the CEO of Silicon Data. She is building the pricing index that goes beneath it. Carmen, welcome to the show. Thank you for waking up early in Asia. How's it going? Thank you. This is great. This is great, yeah. I'm excited to be here. Okay, so this is all early; it's pending regulatory approval but in plain English, because this is going to be such a new asset class, what are you actually trying to build here? It's a great question. So take a step back: Silicon Data is the market intelligence provider building financial infrastructure for the AI economy. We published the world's first GPU indices in 2025. So that tracks A100, H100, B200 near cloud on-demand per GPU per hour rental rate—and that's a mouthful. The goal for any financial instrument is for risk management, risk transfer, and price discovery. The way we think about compute is it is on track to become the world's largest commodity by dollars spent. There's a way people need it for risk management, for price discovery, that should be closely linked to the spot market. How people are spending money on compute today is usually on rental reserve contracts. Per GPU per hour; that's become the default unit of choice. That's why we create A100, H100, B200; those three are Nvidia popular chip families. Right. Carmen, who's actually using that data today and what's it telling you about where compute prices are headed? Yeah, it is a great question. When we started publishing those GPU indices last May at Bloomberg and Refinitiv terminals, everyone was saying that the GPU price, the rental price, is going to always go down—what is the point of indices for a commodity that is always just going to go down in price? Intuitively speaking, compute costs should come down. However, I think people forget it's a cyclical industry where supply-demand plays a huge role. As the cycle goes up in demand, supply constraint follows. And then compute is not a simple product. I was always telling reporters that I wish we could have a bubble easily, right? If you think about bringing any unit of compute online, you have to have the stars aligned for every single thing, right? From energy, to colocation, to the server itself, to the actual software component. It's not that easy to build compute live. It's not something we can just say, hey, snap a finger and compute will be on line. So it's actually really hard to form a very big bubble. So that translates to whenever demand spikes, it doesn't mean the supply can catch up very quickly. So then you see the cyclical price behavior. The price we have seen usually when new chips come out, the price usually comes pretty high as the supply is restrained. The price should come down gradually as more supply comes into the ecosystem, and then it normalizes. However, this is not the pattern we see for B200 per se. B200 opened high-ish relative to H100 families, and then did come down but the slope of the price coming down is a lot less steep than what we have seen for A100 and H100 families. And then the price we see actually came back up. That's a demonstration of a supply constraint. And geopolitics plays a role like it does in other commodity futures, certainly. So what I hear you saying Carmen is, you know, compute can be volatile. But everything we've heard so far in the last 45 minutes that we've been on this live stream is like demand is totally insatiable, it's only going one way. What you're looking at is more volatility. Is that a right way of looking at it? Is the market prepared for that? Is that what we're seeing sort of just in terms of public equities? So a few things. I think number one, I think compute as an industry is definitely going through the cycle of increasing demand, and increasing supply ideally. However, it's not in the same pace. So volatility is going to be a critical risk component for everyone to manage. Think about from the compute buyer's point of view; obviously if you are consuming compute, you don't want your costs to just go up at any given point as unmanageable. So you want to hedge your cost. If you are a supplier of GPUs, the near clouds, the infrastructure providers—ideally you want to make sure your revenue, which is your reserve rate or your rental lease rate, should be stable. Meaning you want that revenue to be hedged. If you are the banks, the insurance companies, or the financiers of those underlying infrastructure, you also want to benchmark, risk manage, and hedge your positions ideally live for intraday. So all of those things will really drive into a benchmark index. So Carmen is it a way for companies, as you say, anyone really to hedge against demand too? Is that what it gets at underneath too? Does it get to that demand question if it's not as strong, is that a way to bet that maybe AI demand isn't just going up and to the right? So again, the supply and demand, right? Assume the demand is not going up as fast. But then at the same time if supply is not going up as fast either, then you reach equilibrium in some way. But everyone's building for neverending supply, it feels like. Everyone's building, but that doesn't mean everything's online at the same time or at the pace that people wanted to. As I said, you need everything to be aligned, which is not the easiest thing to do given how complex the product is. That's really fascinating. Okay, so right now is compute getting more expensive or cheaper? I think you're going to say it's getting cheaper, right, we're getting more efficient. Well, we have defined a unit. If you look at the per token per intelligence at the end, maybe it's getting cheaper. We actually have indices called LM Expenditure Indices, so that tracks usage-weighted LM token pricing across the major consumption of open-source, closed-source, and open-weights models. The prices haven't come down just because of the supposedly token cost coming down—that doesn't mean people always use the cheapest option. Context windows get larger, and input token prices versus the output token prices are different. If you use more input tokens, then things can get expensive depending on which model you use. So again it's not a simple question. In the longer term if we take a hundred steps back, the price all should come down. However, no one can wait for a hundred years. People have inter-annual, monthly, and quarterly risk exposure to hedge. That's why financial products and financial infrastructure makes a lot of sense. Right. I don't know if this is something you can answer, but Jasmine and I are looking at this pricing and we don't have the complete tools. But if you even look at the last earnings season, there's a lot of companies talking about how they're blowing through their budgets or AI pricing is sort of eating away at their margins. How could compute futures help them or let them hedge? And are you seeing the same thing? Exactly. So I think a lot of people voice this; we engage with industry participants constantly. One of the biggest concerns not only about price going up or down is because of the unmanageable nature, the volatility. Think about today if American Airlines said that hey I cannot hedge jet fuel prices—I mean they can't really run a business. Fuel prices are a big component of their cost structure. So how can you run a business without being able to hedge the biggest raw material cost of your operation? You just can't; they can't price tickets. So similarly we copy-paste that to the compute economy. The biggest line item for the AI companies will be the GPU cost or token cost; they have to hedge that. If you are the naturally long GPU participants, the near clouds, or the infrastructure providers, you need to hedge your revenue ideally, then you have consistent margin forecasting. The volatility is what the problem is; it's not directional. If you know for sure it's going to go up, there's ways to do so; there's arrangements. But because you just have so many unknowns, the only thing you can do right now is doing super long-term, long-term contracts. You can lock in a five-year long contract, but how much flexibility can that really give you as an industry, as a business, as a company? Right? So it's not the most efficient way for asset, for capital allocation. Well, you explained it well. It's a fascinating development and what it says, too, about where we are in AI and the cycle and, and what's ahead. Carmen, thanks again for waking up early to join us. Safe travels back to the US and thanks again for being on. Thank you so much. That'll do it for this live stream. A quick check of Cerebras, which is still trading up about 71 percent—316 dollars and change. Interesting, very strong debut. See where it goes in the weeks and months ahead. Thank you again for tuning in. Thank you to Jasmine and Denise on the production side, Morgan in the control room, and Robert and Evan behind the cameras. We'll see you guys all next week. Thanks again.

Cerebras Goes Public in Year's Biggest IPO | Bloomberg Tech

Bloomberg Podcasts(483.0K) ·44:07 ·489 views · 2026-05-14
Ed Ludlow (Host, Bloomberg Technology)
Cisco and Cerebras dominate the tech landscape as the AI infrastructure build-out accelerates, while high-stakes diplomacy in Beijing sees U.S.
TL;DR Cisco and Cerebras dominate the tech landscape as the AI infrastructure build-out accelerates, while high-stakes diplomacy in Beijing sees U.S. tech titans navigating complex geopolitical tensions between President Trump and Xi Jinping. Record Growth: Cisco shares jumped...

TL;DR

Cisco and Cerebras dominate the tech landscape as the AI infrastructure build-out accelerates, while high-stakes diplomacy in Beijing sees U.S. tech titans navigating complex geopolitical tensions between President Trump and Xi Jinping.

Cisco’s AI Pivot Drives Market Optimism

  • Record Growth: Cisco shares jumped 15%, tracking for their best performance since 2011, following a massive revision in AI-related demand.
      • Hyperscaler Forecast: The company raised its calendar year 2026 forecast for hyperscaler orders to $9 billion, up significantly from the previous $5 billion estimate.
      • Revenue Translation: Roughly $4 billion of that demand is expected to translate into actual revenue within the current fiscal year.
  • Strategic Realignment: To prioritize AI infrastructure, Cisco is cutting approximately 4,000 roles.
      • Market Positioning: Analysts note that Cisco is successfully transitioning from a legacy networking firm into a core player in the AI data center build-out, providing the essential "cables, switches, and routers" for the sector.

Cerebras Systems’ Record-Breaking IPO

  • Largest Tech Listing of the Year: AI chipmaker Cerebras priced its IPO at $185 per share, raising $5.55 billion.
      • Market Enthusiasm: Initial trading indications reached as high as $350–$400 per share, reflecting a massive oversubscription (over 25x).
      • Valuation Potential: With Nvidia valued in the trillions, buy-side analysts suggest Cerebras—currently valued near $80 billion—could realistically target a $100 billion market cap.
  • Competitive Edge: CEO Andrew Feldman highlighted the company’s "wafer-scale" technology, where the chip is the size of a dinner plate rather than a postage stamp.
      • Performance Metrics: Feldman claims Cerebras is 15-20 times faster than competitors in AI inference and offers a superior "dollar per token" ratio.
      • Major Partnerships: The company recently announced a $20 billion deal with OpenAI for 750 megawatts of compute and a binding term sheet with AWS to deploy equipment in their data centers.

Geopolitics and Tech Diplomacy in Beijing

  • The Xi-Trump Summit: President Trump and a delegation of CEOs (including Elon Musk, Tim Cook, and Jensen Huang) met with Xi Jinping to stabilize US-China ties.
      • Taiwan Warning: President Xi warned that Taiwan remains a "highly dangerous situation" that could derail the bilateral relationship, though U.S. officials like Marco Rubio downplayed changes to the status quo.
      • Open Door Policy: Xi told tech executives that "China's door to the outside world will only open wider," signaling a desire for continued U.S. tech investment despite security concerns.
  • Regional Conflicts: Trump reported that Xi pledged not to supply weapons to Iran and expressed a willingness to help bring Iran to the negotiating table to secure trade routes like the Strait of Hormuz.

AI Integration in Hardware and Infrastructure

  • Ericsson and Global Competition: CEO Börje Ekholm noted that while Huawei is their primary benchmark, Ericsson is focusing on lead markets like the U.S., Japan, and India to combat China's scale.
      • Physical AI: Ekholm predicts AI will move into the physical and industrial worlds, requiring high-speed terrestrial cellular networks to manage latency for edge computing.
  • Oura’s Predictive Health: CEO Tom Hale discussed using AI for short-term health predictions (illness and cycles) and long-term behavioral changes.
      • Manufacturing Shift: Like Ericsson, Oura is moving toward U.S.-based manufacturing to mitigate tariff risks and supply chain disturbances.
  • Alphabet’s Global Debt Strategy: Google’s parent company is tapping the Japanese Yen bond market for the first time to fund its massive CapEx requirements, part of a broader trend of Big Tech diversifying debt across international markets.
"This chip is the size of a dinner plate while traditional chips are the size of a postage stamp... You don’t just get 15 or 20 times faster than the competition because you built a good chip. It’s a collection of things—packaging and system design—that enable that performance."

— Andrew Feldman, CEO of Cerebras Systems

"For the rest of the world to compete with China, you need to lead on technology... unless you're on the same development curve as they are in China, we're not going to be able to bring those solutions globally."

— Börje Ekholm, CEO of Ericsson

"China's efforts here in Beijing are aimed at stabilizing ties between the U.S. and China rather than overhauling them... Xi Jinping basically threatened that Taiwan could derail the U.S.-China relationship."

— Tyler Kendall, Bloomberg News Reporter

Bloomberg Audio Studios. Podcasts, Radio, News. Bloomberg Tech is live from coast to coast with Caroline Hyde in New York and Ed Ludlow in San Francisco. This is Bloomberg Tech. Coming up, Cisco shares jump the most since 2011 after the company's earnings results; we'll break them down. Plus, China's Xi Jinping gives a warning to President Trump on Taiwan but tells US tech CEOs China will open up more. And AI chipmaker Cerebras raises five point five five billion dollars in its US IPO, seizing on the surging demand for semiconductors. We'll be joined by the CEO later this hour. Cisco is our top story and it is actually rising with the rest of the market. We're up fifteen percent, on track for our biggest jump since 2011. After the open, we hit seventeen percent, putting Cisco on track for its best day since 2002. It's a pretty simple story: hyperscaler demand. For calendar year 26, they are looking at nine billion dollars, up from a previous forecast of five billion dollars. Four billion of that will translate into actual revenue and sales on the income statement in this year. There are many more threads, but Cisco is finding its place in this AI infrastructure story. Let's get out to Bloomberg Equities reporter Ryan Vlastelica. And I think Ryan, the best place to start is: what has the sell side reaction been to this? What are the analysts saying? The main thread of their enthusiasm for Cisco has been this morning. Analysts are very positive and very optimistic that Cisco will be able to continue kind of putting up these numbers that we saw this past quarter. There is a lot of optimism that Cisco is now really part of the AI infrastructure firmament. It showed very strong demand, as you said, from the hyperscalers. It's pointing to very strong growth going forward. I think there's been a growing realization this year that the sort of optical and networking companies are a huge part of the whole data center build-out. And obviously, we have seen no end of demand and growth for data centers related to AI. Cisco's really well-positioned in that. It's a legacy company; it has a wide product suite that people are using here. It seems very well-positioned and it does seem like analysts are really sort of seeing additional confirmation of this trend. Ryan, there's so much to pack into Bloomberg Tech today. People are saying, "Why are you starting on Cisco?" The markets team is looking at the equities market widely, and Cisco is a big factor in kind of the feel-good that's out there. Networking is your cables, your switches, your routers; that all goes into real data centers, right? But the other part of the story is Cisco's discipline. They're cutting about four thousand roles. How did the company kind of spin that as a positive in that sort of feel-good around AI in the market today? Well, they talked about how they are really focusing and prioritizing AI. You mentioned the job cuts; it does seem like all of their investments are really geared toward this end market in particular. So, there is a lot of optimism about that. And I would just say, as I'm sure you remember, Cisco was one of the major companies of the dot com era build-out. Now, it saw huge growth at that time and it only recently surpassed its dot com era high. So there's a certain amount of poetic justice, I guess, in the fact that AI is the thing that is finally bringing it back to record levels. It does seem like it is now becoming a real part of this technology build-out, just as it was in the internet tech build-out. Ryan, you and I were not doing this in the dot com era, but in the here and now, Cisco is a big story. Bloomberg's Ryan Vlastelica on the equities desk, thank you so much. Another stock we're watching is Klarna. Looking at shares also up fifteen, sixteen percent. The company posted just one million dollars in first quarter net income, but it beat estimates, swinging from a ninety-nine million dollar loss a year ago. This is in the fintech space, right? Revenue jumping forty-four percent to one billion dollars, driven by higher interest income, growth in debit card sign-ups, and partnership fees. It's another one that we had to fit in in a very busy Thursday morning. Today, all eyes are on Cerebras. The company is set to begin trading after pricing its IPO at a hundred and eighty-five dollars a share, raising five point five five billion dollars, the biggest US IPO of the year so far. It has been mayhem in the market so far. Shares were indicated at one point to open at four hundred dollars a share. Four hundred, having priced at 185. I think we're now back down at three hundred and fifty dollars a share as of one minute past eight Pacific time, one minute past eleven AM Eastern time. Bloomberg's Bailey Lipschultz has been my partner in tracking the chaos of this IPO for a good few days now. Let's start with the math, yeah? The indicated opening price is well beyond a hundred and eighty-five dollars a share. What's that telling us? It tells us that there's untapped demand, Ed. When you look at a deal like this more than twenty times oversubscribed—we reported before it even launched—there was ten billion dollars of demand. So these are huge numbers. This is the hottest stock, at least from an IPO perspective, this year bar none. The big question, Ed, you asked me—you know, look at the market cap of where this could open—if we're getting closer to eighty billion dollars. Talking to folks on the buy side, they're still saying, you know, if Nvidia is worth five trillion dollars, who's to say that this company doesn't easily trade closer to a hundred billion? I don't know what you've been hearing about that from your side, though. Yes, so exactly, that brings us back to: what is Cerebras? What does it do? Well, it is a full-stack vertically integrated supercomputer maker, right? Nvidia makes largely the chips but the server trays these days. It doesn't build its own servers; Dell does that, Supermicro does that. Cerebras does the full-send package. But even at a hundred billion dollars, it's a drop in the ocean. Why is the market so enthusiastic about its technology? Like, what else do we know about this company's financials or its customer backlog? The big thing initially when they tried to go public two years ago was concentration risk. So, G42 was pretty much their only customer. They've expanded that; now you have OpenAI and others. And the big pitch, Ed, at least when I talk to folks on the buy side, is how broad-based can that be? If we're dealing with a market, especially as it relates to building some of the compute and everything that goes into building out the AI future, if this is just the start and we're in early innings, what does this ultimately look like, call it five years from now? And the big question is back to your point earlier; if this is just a drop in the bucket, there's a pretty much massive potential as it relates to going forward. Though again, all things considered, we've seen the semiconductor index up what? Thirty percent since they started this process? Right. I would say, indicated opening prices—they're on the Bloomberg Terminal—they're not always right. Who knows what will happen when trade starts, but Bloomberg's Bailey Lipschultz, please don't go far. Stay with Bloomberg Tech because coming up later this hour, we will speak with Cerebras CEO Andrew Feldman, and if you're on the socials, hit me up; I welcome your questions. Now coming up, China's Xi Jinping gives a warning to President Trump on Taiwan. We're going to have those details next. This is Bloomberg Tech. One of the most important summits in human history. And the two presidents, President Xi and President Trump, have such a wonderful relationship. This is an incredible opportunity for us to rely on their relationships to build a much, much better partnership. Yeah. Did you get any specific customers for your chips, for the H200? We didn't really talk about that. I'm here to support the president and to represent the United States. That was Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang speaking to reporters in Beijing and telling Bloomberg the company hasn't really discussed the H200 chips during the president's trip to China. He's there to support President Trump. What was discussed, though, is Taiwan. Xi Jinping, through state news agency, called it a quote, "highly dangerous situation" for the world's biggest economies. Let's get out to Bloomberg's Tyler Kendall who joins us from Beijing. There are many threads; we also have the breaking news of the last fifteen minutes, the president saying that Iran was discussed and that there is some sort of agreement there. What do we need to know? What's the latest from China? Well Ed, at this point, as you're saying, there are a lot of different threads that we could pull on here. I can say that the overarching theme appears to be that the efforts here in Beijing are aimed at stabilizing ties between the US and China rather than overhauling them. If we start with the latest news, President Trump is out saying that Xi Jinping has pledged not to supply Iran with any weapons and that it's going to help get Iran to the negotiating table. Perhaps this will help make some progress when it comes to what has been gridlock in those ongoing discussions, as well as a reopening in the Strait of Hormuz. Though we would be remiss if we didn't point out that from the Chinese readout, we didn't really get any indications that China is willing to go further in terms of helping to ease that conflict, saying instead that they had extensive dialogue about the situation in the Middle East but not really giving us any firmer details there. Another point where there appeared to be a mismatch in terms of the public readouts from what happened behind closed doors was, as you mentioned, that issue related to Taiwan, as Chinese President Xi Jinping basically threatened that Taiwan could derail the US-China relationship. That was not mentioned when it came to the US readout in this. And in fact, we just heard from Secretary of State Marco Rubio, who appeared to downplay that the concerns were raised after we really saw US officials in recent days signal that there would not be a change when it comes to US policy regarding Taiwan. Now we can say, though, that there was a more optimistic tone reached when it came to the economic issues. That includes the fact that Chinese President Xi Jinping did appear to signal a move towards greater openness, telling tech execs in the room—including Elon Musk, Tim Cook, and Jensen Huang, who traveled with President Trump—in part quote that "China's door to the outside world will only open wider," though despite the messaging, we haven't really seen tangible signs yet that any steps have been taken. But Chinese state media did report that these executives told Xi Jinping directly that they highly value the Chinese market. And Ed, as you well know, this US business delegation includes these companies that already have high exposure to the Chinese market, but also companies like Micron which have seen some of their products barred over cybersecurity concerns here in China. We can say at this point there's still a lot that we are waiting for: potential purchase agreements when it comes to agriculture, Boeing aircraft, or energy products from the US. But the dialogue does continue; these pair of leaders do have bilateral meetings going into tomorrow as well. Bloomberg's Tyler Kendall with everything you need to know out of China so far. Thank you very much. And let's get the broader picture with Michelle Giuda, CEO of The Krach Institute for Tech Diplomacy at Purdue. She also served as assistant secretary of state for global public affairs under the first Trump administration. You understand how this works and you understand what's at stake. And like here on Bloomberg Tech, right, I want to start with Taiwan because for a very long time you've been coming on this program and we've been talking about Taiwan in the context of America's dependence on that nation for semiconductor supply, right? I'm literally talking about TSMC and the manufacturing footprint in Taiwan—apologies. Have we learned anything new here on Taiwan? What did you make of President Xi's statement? Nothing's new here. I think the position that China has taken on Taiwan being really important to them when it comes to US-China relations has been the case for quite some time. We know that General Secretary Xi Jinping has been pressuring the president on arms sales to Taiwan for a while. So that's not new at all. And neither is the United States' position on maintaining the status quo. As Secretary Rubio said, there's been no change on the US position here with regards to Taiwan. But as you had mentioned, you know, the ties between Taiwan and the United States are strong, they run deep, semiconductors are a critical piece of that, and there's been a lot of collaboration with TSMC building out here in the United States. Ultimately, the status quo hasn't changed. A red headline on the Bloomberg Terminal just before we came on air: President Trump saying that President Xi offered help on Iran and pledged not to send weapons to Iran. The president had said that it wouldn't be an important topic of conversation, but actually, if you look at what markets are saying and what people are saying on socials, it's a key issue. It's especially key on how it would have a bearing on the Strait of Hormuz. It's been one of several key issues. I think it's not surprising that they talked about Iran. It was looming over this entire thirty-six hour summit. The fact that you mentioned this breaking news that President Xi is going to stop supporting the Iranian regime is a positive step. And I think it's one that the United States would welcome. So again, not surprising. And I think overall the big picture on this is, you know, this is a thirty-six hour summit on all of these agenda items. Ultimately, these are micro movements in a much bigger picture here of the United States and its long-term view to achieve the golden age of America and retain our leadership across the world. China's on its own path to China rejuvenation, and the big question is: who gets there first? And do the thirty-six hours in this summit help the United States move incrementally faster toward our vision? I just want to disclose to the Bloomberg Tech audience that President Trump's comments about Iran were part of a Fox News interview, a clip that Bloomberg reviewed. Michelle, would you kindly draw on your experience from participating in the first Trump administration? What does it take to put a delegation like this together that includes Elon Musk, Jensen Huang—picking them up in Alaska for the refuel stop on Air Force One and taking the world's most important tech CEOs to China as part of a high-stakes meeting? Yeah, these things take a lot of preparation historically, especially in terms of what you want the meetings to be about and the readouts coming out of these meetings. However, I will say this: this type of delegation is unprecedented. And I think if you look at how the president has been agile and maybe non-traditional in his work, so too are these tech CEOs. They move fast and they're disruptive, and it's easy to hop on a plane in Alaska and go to China. So I'd say, you know, there's a lot of planning. At the same time, with this administration and these types of disruptive tech leaders, there's also a lot of room for ad-libbing and improvisation, and I think that's what we're seeing here. Michelle Giuda, thank you very much. Now coming up, we're going to get out to Napa Valley where our own Caroline Hyde is set to speak with the CEO of Ericsson on the sidelines of the Spark Global Leadership Summit. It's coming up next. This is Bloomberg Tech. Nuclear power is trying to make a comeback, but the resources required are in short supply. From uranium mining to enrichment, the supply chain's being reshaped by geopolitics, lobbyists, rising energy demand, and new technologies. That's the focus of Bloomberg Primer this week. Listen to this: Global investment in nuclear energy since 2020 is estimated to be around three hundred billion dollars with ambitious plans in Europe, North America, and Asia to revive or kickstart their nuclear energy sectors. Every step of the supply chain, from mining to enrichment to building reactors, is highly regulated and controlled because a single misstep could be catastrophic and erode the public trust nuclear has worked decades to regain. It's a real shift from when I came of age and everyone was worried about nuclear war. The process of enriching uranium for nuclear fuel is the same technology that they use for enriching uranium to make nuclear weapons. Catch the full episode of Primer on bloomberg.com and YouTube. Let's go over to the Spark Global Leadership Summit in Napa where our own Caroline Hyde is standing by. Caro. It is a tough life being hosted by Concello here in Napa Valley. And I'm very pleased to say I'm joined by a giant in telecoms. Based in Sweden, Ericsson is a global company, and Börje Ekholm is here with me, who is the CEO. Börje, tell me about the China exposure you have. You have supply chain there. You have the consumer. And competition is really what you have from China. What did you make of the relationship between US and China that we just saw overnight? Thanks for having me. That's a great question to ask, very hard to answer actually. We like to do that in journalism. Oh, I know, I know. But I do think there is—you know, China started out—I often look at the old telecom movie from China, right? It actually started out as being a prioritized sector in China. So they had almost a hundred vendors if you go back thirty years. They consolidated into really two that became fierce competitors over a decade. Huawei being one key one. Huawei, of course, being the biggest. They started out being, of course, low-cost and fairly simple. Today, they're of course a phenomenal competitor and it's the one that we benchmark ourselves with. We say that's the one we need to beat, actually. We need to win on technology; we need to win on cost position. So for us, the competition with China is front and center and has been. I do believe that for the rest of the world to compete with China, you need to lead on technology. But you've been outspoken in the past, sort of pushing against this idea of a focus on your own country and banning. In Sweden, they pushed out Chinese competition and you actually at the time said that's the wrong thing to do. In the US, your key markets now are the US, Japan, and other nations. How do you think about China being excluded, and should they be? You know, the way to think about about China is actually multi-pronged. So if you take telecom again, it's a scale game. They have scale in their domestic market; that's a market you need to be in. But more importantly, what was also clear is that given the development in China, economically and use case-wise, you saw data consumption grow much faster in China than anywhere else in the world. That led them to lead on or demand certain technologies ahead of the rest of the world. So unless you're on the same development curve as they are in China, we're not going to be able to bring those solutions globally. If we take in our case, we have what's called massive MIMO. Basically, it's a way to increase capacity in the radio network. They needed that earlier than in the rest of the world, and therefore if you're not there when that happens, you don't develop the technologies at the same pace as they do in China. I think it's a much more interdependent world. You need to think of the world as being interdependent. And you're dependent on geopolitics. Tariffs, inflationary pressures, the Strait of Hormuz is again front and center today. How are you managing that? You know, we actually took a decision a number of years ago now to say that we have three home markets. So that was recognizing the US because it's a front-runner market. Big. India in those days was big; today it's starting also to be a front-runner on technology. Japan is very big in telecom and also an early adopter of telecom. So we said those are markets we need to win in to make sure that we combat the scale of China. So we did that. Then we said we need a flexible supply chain in order to manage any disturbances. So we built a factory here in the US. How big is your manufacturing now here in the US? We manufacture a large portion of what's supplied to the US here. So we built that in 2020, commissioned it about 2020. So we were ahead of the really supply constraints in that sense and the tariff issues. We've been able to use that manufacturing footprint and R&D footprint to actually manage a bit of the geopolitical exposure. Now we look at the markets today and the extraordinary ramp high on the back of AI. AI is the excitement in your area as well. Look, you've got competition with Nokia, who has a strong relationship with Nvidia, and they've gone big into data centers. Briefly, how are you thinking about the AI opportunity? We think of the AI opportunity as a massive opportunity for us for the simple reason that AI will ultimately move out to the physical world—call it industrial AI or physical AI. And that's going to require inference at the edge of the network to manage the latency requirements, etc., and then connectivity will be needed. So we will start to enter a world where everything has to be connected. When everything is connected, you can't connect them with wires. It just simply doesn't work. You're going to need the terrestrial cellular network to provide the backbone to scale AI. So I think of AI—yes, it's going to fundamentally change the economy, it's going to be a driver of productivity. It's hugely positive. And for us, it will be a benefit because we will see new types of traffic coming in the network. Börje Ekholm, I could talk to you more; I'm luckily going to be talking to you in a panel a little bit later here at the Spark Leadership Summit hosted by Concello. Ed, back to you. Okay, more interviews to come out of the Spark Summit throughout the afternoon. Tune in for a conversation with OpenAI CFO Sarah Friar. Welcome back to Bloomberg Tech. To our TV viewers and radio listeners around the world, we're joined by Andrew Feldman, Cerebras CEO. Cerebras priced this IPO, Andrew, a hundred and eighty-five dollars a share—above the top end of the marketing range. I'm looking at the Bloomberg Terminal. Shares are indicated to open at three hundred and fifty dollars a share. Your reaction to that? Pretty good day, huh? How you feel? I mean, did you see this coming? There's some mechanics to it, right? But you know, I know you, and let's be honest, you like to talk about what's happening in AI, the big focus on inference, and Cerebras' place in it. But this is the biggest IPO of the year so far. What does it mean to you and your employees and your backers? It's the biggest tech—one of the biggest tech IPOs in history—and it's the biggest semi IPO in history. We couldn't be more proud. This is the culmination of a decade of work, of countless late nights and long weekends. We are enormously proud and excited and ready to get back to work and start working on the next great thing. Again, the market is indicating shares to open at three hundred and fifty dollars a share. Let's see where we're at when trading starts. But you're being priced in as a major player in this field now. What evidence would you point to that you are, in fact, a major player? A lot of focus is on the concentration in the relationship with OpenAI. Tell me about some of the other frontier labs or other names that you have some concrete talks with. Sure. In the last four months, we announced a deal with OpenAI that's north of twenty billion dollars for seven hundred and fifty megawatts of compute. We also announced a major engagement with AWS where our equipment would be deployed in their data centers. I think those are obviously the largest, but there are dozens of others that are in what used to be a big deal in the ten to fifty million dollar category. And so there's just an extraordinary demand right now for fast inference. We're the fastest, not by a little bit, but by more than an order of magnitude. You know, we're fifteen times faster than the next nearest competitor. And as AI has become useful, everybody wants it to be fast. Nobody wants to wait. On that performance, there is a lot of emphasis on the dollar per token, right? That is the metric that the field cares about. But that's on paper. You know, you just talked about the AWS relationship as an engagement. When does the word "engagement" end and real revenue start to show for you from those relationships? Sure, we signed a binding term sheet with AWS as described in our S1, and we're working through the master agreement. I think in dealing with organizations of that size, it takes a little time to dot all the i's and cross the t's. But we are extremely confident that they will be an enormous channel for us and a partner in delivering our technology to large enterprises and medium-sized enterprises around the world. I think they are one of the preferred cloud providers for just about every enterprise on earth. An opportunity to have your solution embedded in their offering as part of their bedrock offering—that's a huge win for us. We're live on Bloomberg Television and Radio. This is a Bloomberg Tech takeover, and we're speaking to Andrew Feldman, the CEO of Cerebras, whose company just priced the IPO at a hundred and eighty-five dollars a share and is indicated right now to start trade at three hundred and fifty dollars a share. Andrew, I think you know this about me: whenever we have a big moment to have a conversation like this, I always go to the Bloomberg Tech audience. What do they want to know? And actually, the first question is: what was your attitude toward retail investors? Why did you not do more for the retail investor in this big moment for your company? We were more than 25X oversubscribed. There were a lot of hard decisions that needed to be made, and nobody got what they wanted. And you know, we did our best. It's about all you can do when there's twenty-five times more demand for your stock at the institutional level and at the retail level than there are shares to be sold. So we're really proud of the way we chose to do it; we thought we did it with integrity. And I think we are very comfortable with the ending allocation. Full-stack, fully vertically integrated—and what I mean by that is you literally build the supercomputer top to tail, right? So Nvidia will do the tray, not just GPU, but then Dell or Supermicro will assemble it. Dell's margins are low teens. Nvidia's margins are mid-seventies. Your margins are forty, forty-one percent. I'm trying to help the audience understand why that strategy of owning everything top to tail is going to pay off in the long run. And I would say the other question I get for you is: what's the future outlook for margins based on your plans? Well, I think a couple of things. I think we have obviously opportunities to improve our cost structure. You know, we did half a billion last year in sales; that means we put two hundred and fifty million in the supply chain. Obviously, that's not an efficient spot. As we grow, we will have more leverage in the supply chain and our cost of goods will come down. I think we have an opportunity to increase prices. I think the demand for fast inference is overwhelming this minute. And so I think in the long run, we'll be really proud of our gross margins and where they will sort of wash out as we hit scale. I've got a question for you from a terminal client via IB. Everyone's tuned in right now, Andrew; they want the detail. And so one way that people look at it is that you know, this is custom silicon, but Cerebras isn't a chip per se—it's like wafer level. Is there a reason why you can't just sell that versus the whole server? Yeah, that's a good question. For the entire seventy-year history of the compute industry, every previous effort had failed to build a chip of this size. So for your audience, this chip is the size of a dinner plate, while traditional chips are the size of a postage stamp. And not only had everybody failed until we succeeded, but several tried to copy us and have since failed as well. Part of what we were capable of doing was that we were able to use not just the chip, but also our expertise in packaging and in system design to solve some of the problems and build a truly compelling solution, right? You don't just get fifteen or eighteen or twenty times faster than the competition because you built a good chip. That's one of a collection of different things that enable that sort of performance. You can build a great chip and the system vendor, the ODM, can nibble away at your performance by not delivering the right amount of power or the right amount of IO, right? There's a reason why Nvidia then sought to control the IO, the NVLink, because they didn't want others to nibble away at their performance. So by building the system, we're able to optimize all parts. I mean, you could ask the same thing about Porsche: you know, why don't you sell just engines, right? It turns out that a 911 is a beautiful car because of the engine and everything else they put in it. Andrew, the team's telling me we've got to go, but I've got to ask: five point five five billion dollars—what do you use the proceeds for? And actually, how flexible can you now be in allocating capacity to new customers? We just have thirty seconds. Oh, I think we will use it to increase capacity. We are excited to bring many new customers on board. There's tremendous demand for what we're doing. I think we can be aggressive on that front. Andrew Feldman, Cerebras CEO, pricing the IPO at 185, indicated to open three hundred and fifty dollars a share. Thank you for your time. Now coming up, Tom Hale, the Oura CEO, discusses the future of wearables. We're going to go back to the Spark Summit next. This is Bloomberg Tech. I am at the Concello Spark Summit right here in Napa Valley, and with wine comes a focus on your health. Many might be looking at their Oura ring today to see how they're feeling after a day in Napa Valley, and I'm joined by Tom Hale, of course, the CEO of Oura. With such exuberance in the market about artificial intelligence, I'm interested as to how you're seeing your own product iterate. You're all about now prediction—not just assessing how I might feel on the day or the inputs that I'm getting, but predicting how I might feel in the future. Yeah, we've been doing AI for many years. So the thing that's most interesting is that the predictions that we make generally are short-term. You might be getting sick in a couple of days, your cycle's coming in a couple of days, and they're very accurate because they're short-term. But I think where this starts to get really interesting is if you can make predictions over a longer time frame. You're starting to help people understand how their behaviors can bend the curve on their health outcomes or maybe even ultimately on the cost of healthcare. And I think this is really disruptive but also really positive because one of the biggest problems with healthcare is there's just simply not enough care to be provided for everybody, and AI lowers the cost and provides access and provides medical information. It has to be high quality, it has to be scientifically validated, it has to be accurate and good, but that opportunity exists. At the moment, an Oura ring is deemed a general wellness product, but there could be even further ways in which you move within the medical space. That requires FDA approval, particularly for blood pressure, for example. How are you thinking about that? Getting the science on board? Well, our view is that we want to be as aligned with the FDA as possible. We want to make sure that what we do is in the path of scientific and medical validation; that includes the FDA. So we're working closely on multiple submissions. Blood pressure is certainly one of them. We're currently running something called the blood pressure profile study. That's almost three hundred thousand people who are taking cuff measurements and comparing to the predictions that the ring is making so we could have a data set that'll support the idea that what this does is accurate, validated, and clinically relevant. Until that time, we'll probably operate as a wellness device, which means that we can provide insight but not something that's like a diagnosis. We'll stop short of that. Though the FDA doesn't have someone at the top right now, does that affect the way in which you work with them? Yeah, not really. I think we're very excited to see how we can work with CDRH, which is the division of the FDA that focuses on that. So we're working with the rank and file, the people who are focused on medical devices, and we have a great collaboration with them. You have a great collaboration with Apple, but you're also kind of frenemies in some way? Well, I think the interesting thing about how Apple and Oura interact is that it turns out that almost two-thirds of Oura ring wearers have a second wearable—most often a wrist wearable and most often an Apple Watch. So we're actually highly complementary. Now going to the people, there's great talent; Apple has produced some of the icons of health and hardware. I think having some of those people join our team has been incredible. I don't know, we feel very proud of the people who have joined us, and we've got some great connectivity with Apple as well. I'm a longtime independent software developer on the Apple platform, so we have a lot of people that we have been working with for years. Software developers are not cheap in this age of AI. How are you thinking about your talent more broadly? So for us, the interesting thing is that about a year ago people would be like, "Wow, I mean hardware's hard; why are you doing hardware?" And of course, the first rule of software school is you don't do hardware. And so I obviously missed that class or lesson at software school, but it's worked out okay because now what people tell us is, "We can't believe how resilient you are to the AI disruption to software." Turns out hardware is very, very attractive right now because you can't vibe code atoms, right? You can't just summon them into existence. Interestingly, I think for Oura, steering the way we have and having always been a hardware company with a very strong software backing, it's actually been really advantageous. Investors look at us and they say, "Good, we're so glad that you're not in the software space and that you're a hardware provider." And you've just been luring more investment and you got a bigger market cap on the private side. I'm interested as to whether there's any inflationary pressures your investors are worried about. How are you thinking about manufacturing in this age of supply chain—we think about Hormuz, we think about China today? Yeah, I don't think we've really seen the impact of inflation on demand, which is good. We certainly have seen some inflation impacts on the supply chain, but nothing material. And interestingly, last year, of course, there was all this drama about tariffs, and I think as a company we were quite lucky. One, we actually announced an intention to have a factory built in the US, and so that's actually one really interesting outcome of that. It's just underway; you know, we'll let you know when it's all rolled out. But at the same time, I think we also were able to, because we manufacture around the globe, be very careful to sort of manage the tariff issues very effectively. Tom Hale, I could speak to you for much longer. The CEO of Oura, fascinating to catch up. We hand it back to you from the Concello Spark Summit here in Napa Valley, Ed. Time now for Talking Tech, and first up, Hon Hai, also known as Foxconn, reported a nineteen percent jump in quarterly profit. It says the US will gradually become the company's largest AI server production hub. The company's become a key AI hardware player by assembling servers that house Nvidia accelerators. Plus, China's tech leaders are facing a reality check. Alibaba narrowly missed revenue targets this morning as e-commerce wars blunted its cloud surge, while Tencent reported its slowest growth in over a year. And AdTech is eyeing a potential four billion dollar debut in Hong Kong. The optical connectivity specialist is reportedly seeking to raise at least five hundred million dollars or more at a valuation of three to four billion dollars according to sources. Big story on the terminal: Alphabet is taking the AI race to the global bond market. After a blockbuster seventeen billion dollar sale at home, the Google parent is now tapping the Japanese Yen market for the first time. Bloomberg's Tasos Vossos joins us to explain Big Tech's move into international debt, right? So that's the evolution here, looking at international markets outside of its home. Why? Well, at some point you have to. If you look at issuance this year, for example, non-financial corporates in the US—which is the deepest, biggest credit market in the world—forty cents out of every dollar that was raised came from the tech sector, which was rather large. So you have to think: in order to avoid saturating even the biggest market in the world, you actually have to look at every corner out there. So hyperscalers—in particular Alphabet, but also Amazon—have taken steps to diversify that. So they've tapped every big market they can imagine. So it's been Euros, Pound Sterling, it's been Swiss Francs, and now we're looking at the Yen market. It's pretty much a global race in order to finance this massive CapEx spend that we're going to see in the next few years. And then in the Euro bond market, we're talking about the second-biggest credit market. Why is it different this time around, Tasos? Like, we've done a lot of breaking news on this show in corporate bonds; is there something new here? There is, actually, and we've had mega deals in the past where you have tens of billions of dollars being issued on a single day. The problem—well, the difference with that—is that was a one-off. Usually, it's on the back of M&A; you have to raise massive amounts of cash in order to do that, but once you've done that, then you can just absorb the company you've just bought. The difference here is that because the CapEx spend is going to be so large in the next few years—hundreds of billions of dollars—you have massive deal after massive deal. That is a conundrum that investors are facing. They want to be involved in them, but their main issue is that another massive deal is just around the corner. So the previous one's going to weaken on the back of that. That trend of massive deal after massive deal is something that we haven't seen before, pretty much in the history of the credit market. Bloomberg's Tasos Vossos, thank you very much. Let's get back to Cisco before we leave today. Today's big number: nine billion dollars. That's how much the company expects to get in orders from hyperscalers in calendar 26, up from its previous five billion dollar target. It suggests this company's AI shift is showing a payoff, right? Cisco, welcome to the AI party. That's part of the story, and the shares are really reflecting that. We're up fifteen percent, on track for the best day for Cisco since 2011. At the open, a seventeen percent gain put them on track for their best day since 2002. Networking, routers, switches, and cables make those components the story again. Wonderful. That does it for this edition of Bloomberg Tech. Standing by for Cerebras to start trade, priced at 185 for its IPO, indicated to open at three hundred and fifty dollars per share. Check out the podcast, a stunning show to recap Apple, Spotify, iHeart, and on Bloomberg. This is Bloomberg Tech.
